topics.title: Sensors (Basic Working Only): Position, Displacement, and Proximity Sensors (Encoders, Potentiometers, LVDTs, IR Sensors, Acoustic Emission Sensors, Vibration Sensors, Tactile Sensors, Flow Sensors)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors (Basic Working Only): Position, Displacement, and Proximity Sensors (Encoders, Potentiometers, LVDTs, IR Sensors, Acoustic Emission Sensors, Vibration Sensors, Tactile Sensors, Flow Sensors)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Sensor? (KTU Definition)

A **sensor** is a physical device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity and converts it into an electrical signal (analog or digital) that can be measured, recorded, or processed. In Mechatronics, sensors act as the "senses" of a machine, providing feedback about the system's state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The syllabus explicitly states *"Basic Working Only"*. Therefore, the examiner tests your ability to explain the **operating principle**, **construction**, **characteristic curve**, and **typical application** — NOT deep electromagnetic derivations beyond the LVDT output equation.

### 1.2 Classification of Sensors (for this Module)

| Category | Sensors Covered | Quantity Measured |
|---|---|---|
| **Position Sensors** | Encoders, Potentiometers, LVDTs | Linear or angular position |
| **Proximity Sensors** | IR Sensors, Inductive/Capacitive | Presence/absence of object |
| **Special Sensors** | Acoustic Emission, Vibration, Tactile, Flow | High-freq sound, vibration, touch, fluid flow |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Nervous System:** Imagine a person (the controller) walking in a dark room. The **eyes** are like optical encoders (they give precise digital position), the **fingertips** are like tactile sensors (they detect touch/pressure), the **inner ear** is like an accelerometer (it detects vibration/tilt), and the **skin sensing heat** is like an IR sensor. Without these senses, the person cannot react. Likewise, a mechatronic system without sensors is "blind" and cannot be controlled.

### 1.4 Generalized Sensor Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Always remember for KTU exams):**
> - **Sensitivity:** Output per unit input
> - **Range:** Min–Max measurable input
> - **Accuracy:** Closeness to true value
> - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change
> - **Linearity:** Output is a straight line w.r.t. input
> - **Hysteresis:** Difference in output for increasing vs decreasing input
> - **Repeatability:** Same output for same input under identical conditions
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Potentiometric Position Sensor

A **potentiometer** is a resistive element with a sliding wiper that moves along its length. As the wiper moves, the resistance ratio changes, producing a varying output voltage proportional to displacement.

**Construction:**
- A uniform resistance track (wire-wound or conductive plastic) of total resistance $R_t$
- A wiper that slides over the track
- Three terminals: two end terminals (across full $R_t$) and one wiper terminal

**Working Principle:**
If input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the full track and the wiper is at fractional position $x$ (where $0 \le x \le 1$), the output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = x \cdot V_{in}$$

**Types:**
- **Rotary Potentiometer:** Angular displacement input (typically up to 360°)
- **Linear Potentiometer:** Linear displacement input

**Advantages:** Simple, cheap, gives analog output, good for large displacements
**Disadvantages:** Contact wear, limited resolution, friction, electrical noise

### 2.2 Encoders (Position Sensors)

An **encoder** is a digital position sensor that converts angular or linear position into a digital (binary) code.

#### 2.2.1 Incremental Encoder
- A disc with equally spaced slots; a light source (LED) and photodetector are placed on either side
- As the disc rotates, pulses of light are detected → produces pulse train
- Position is determined by **counting pulses** from a reference point
- Has two output channels: **Channel A** and **Channel B** (90° out of phase) for direction sensing — this is called **quadrature encoding**

#### 2.2.2 Absolute Encoder
- Disc has unique binary code pattern at each angular position (n tracks for n bits → $2^n$ unique positions)
- Each position has a unique binary word → no need for reference or power-loss recovery
- Resolution: $\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{2^n}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrature Decoding Rule:**
> - If **A leads B** → rotation is **clockwise**
> - If **B leads A** → rotation is **counter-clockwise**

### 2.3 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

The **LVDT** is an inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional AC voltage. It is the most important sensor from an exam perspective — expect a full 7-mark derivation.

**Construction:**
- One **primary coil** ($P$) wound on the central limb of a hollow cylindrical former
- Two identical **secondary coils** ($S_1$ and $S_2$) wound on the outer limbs, connected in **series opposition**
- A movable soft-iron **core** (armature) slides inside the former

**Working Principle:**
The primary is energized by an AC excitation voltage $E_p$. The two secondaries induce EMFs $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$. Since they are wound in opposite sense, the net output is:

$$E_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$

**Three operating regions:**

| Core Position | $E_{s1}$ | $E_{s2}$ | Output $E_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Null (center)** | Equal | Equal | $0$ V |
| **Right of null** | High | Low | Positive |
| **Left of null** | Low | High | Negative |

**Sensitivity and Range:**
- Linear range: typically $\pm 5$ mm
- Sensitivity (typical): 0.5 to 2 V/mm

**Advantages:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, robust, good linearity, no hysteresis

### 2.4 IR (Infrared) Sensor

An **IR sensor** emits infrared light and detects its reflection from an object. Used as a **proximity** or **object-detection** sensor.

**Components:**
- **IR LED** (emitter, wavelength ~ 700 nm to 1 mm)
- **Photodiode / Phototransistor** (receiver)

**Working:**
- IR LED emits IR rays. If an object is in front, rays reflect back and strike the photodiode
- The photodiode resistance decreases → voltage across it changes → output signal
- Range typically 5 cm to 30 cm

**Applications:** Line-following robots, object counters, burglar alarms, obstacle avoidance

### 2.5 Acoustic Emission (AE) Sensor

An **acoustic emission sensor** detects high-frequency elastic waves (typically **20 kHz to 1 MHz**) generated by the **sudden release of energy** within a material — e.g., crack growth, plastic deformation, fiber breakage, weld defects.

**Working Principle:**
- Uses a **piezoelectric crystal** as the sensing element
- When stress waves reach the sensor, they mechanically deform the crystal
- The crystal generates a proportional voltage (piezoelectric effect)
- Output is analyzed for amplitude, frequency, and arrival time to locate the source (triangulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AE vs Ultrasonic Testing — Common Mistake:** Ultrasonic testing uses **externally generated** sound waves sent into the material. AE sensors **listen** to waves **passively generated by the material itself** (like hearing a crack grow).

**Applications:** Structural health monitoring, pressure vessel inspection, leak detection, monitoring machining processes

### 2.6 Vibration Sensor

A **vibration sensor** measures acceleration, velocity, or displacement of a vibrating surface.

**Most Common Type: Piezoelectric Accelerometer**
- Contains a **piezoelectric crystal** with a known **seismic mass** on top
- When the base vibrates, the seismic mass exerts a varying force on the crystal
- The crystal deforms → generates a voltage proportional to acceleration:

$$V_{out} = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$$

where $d$ is the charge sensitivity, $m$ is seismic mass, $a$ is acceleration.

**Other types:** Piezoresistive, capacitive, MEMS accelerometers

**Applications:** Machine health monitoring, earthquake sensing, automotive airbags, structural monitoring

### 2.7 Tactile Sensor

A **tactile sensor** is essentially an "electronic skin" that detects **contact, pressure, and sometimes texture** of an object.

**Working Principle (Generic):**
- Array of sensing elements (taxels) at known (x, y) positions
- When an object presses, each taxel changes its electrical property (resistance, capacitance, or voltage)
- The change is read by a microcontroller to determine **contact location, shape, and pressure**

**Common Technologies:**
- **Piezoresistive:** Pressure changes resistance of a conductive polymer or foam
- **Capacitive:** Pressure changes the distance between two plates → changes capacitance $C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d}$
- **Piezoelectric:** Pressure generates voltage directly

**Applications:** Robotic grippers, prosthetics, touchscreens, medical palpation robots

### 2.8 Flow Sensor

A **flow sensor** measures the rate of fluid (liquid or gas) flow through a pipe or channel.

**Common Types and Working Principles:**

| Type | Working Principle |
|---|---|
| **Turbine flow meter** | Fluid rotates a turbine; rotational speed ∝ flow rate |
| **Ultrasonic flow meter** | Measures difference in transit time of sound waves upstream vs downstream |
| **Electromagnetic flow meter** | Faraday's law — fluid (conductive) in magnetic field induces EMF: $E = B \cdot v \cdot D$ |
| **Vortex flow meter** | Fluid past a bluff body creates alternating vortices; frequency ∝ velocity |
| **Differential pressure (Orifice)** | Pressure drop across a restriction ∝ flow rate (Bernoulli) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Electromagnetic flow meter equation (KTU favorite):**
> $$E = B \cdot v \cdot D$$
> where $E$ = induced EMF, $B$ = magnetic flux density, $v$ = fluid velocity, $D$ = pipe diameter.

**Applications:** Water treatment, chemical plants, fuel metering, HVAC, medical devices

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sensor | Key Equation | Output | Range (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Potentiometer | $V_{out} = x \cdot V_{in}$ | Analog voltage | Few mm to meters |
| Incremental Encoder | $N = \text{counted pulses}$; $v = f \cdot d$ | Digital pulses | Unlimited |
| Absolute Encoder | $\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{2^n}$ | n-bit binary word | $2^n$ positions |
| LVDT | $E_{out} = k \cdot x$ (linear region) | AC voltage | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 250$ mm |
| Piezoelectric Accel. | $V_{out} = d \cdot m \cdot a$ | Voltage ∝ acceleration | $\pm 1g$ to $\pm 50000g$ |
| Capacitive (Tactile) | $C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d}$ | Capacitance change | 0–100 kPa |
| Electromagnetic Flow | $E = B \cdot v \cdot D$ | EMF ∝ velocity | 0.1–10 m/s |
| Ultrasonic Flow | $\Delta t = \frac{2 v D \cos\theta}{c^{2} - v^{2}}$ | Time difference | Varies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Topic Matters in Engineering:** These sensors are the building blocks of feedback control. CNC machines use encoders + LVDTs; robots use tactile sensors; power plants monitor vibration; process industries depend on flow sensors. Every modern automated system is fundamentally a **sensor + controller + actuator** loop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 LVDT Output Voltage Derivation (THE Most Important Derivation)

The LVDT derivation is the single most tested analytical problem in this module. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation.

**Setup:**
- Primary coil $P$ has $N_p$ turns, supplied with AC excitation $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$
- Secondary coils $S_1$ and $S_2$ have equal turns $N_s$, wound in **opposite sense**
- The movable core shifts a distance $x$ from the null (central) position

**Step 1: Mutual Inductances**
As the core moves, the flux linkage with each secondary changes. Define:
- $M_1$ = mutual inductance between $P$ and $S_1$
- $M_2$ = mutual inductance between $P$ and $S_2$

**Step 2: Induced EMFs in Secondaries**

$$e_{s1} = -M_1 \frac{d i_p}{dt}$$

$$e_{s2} = -M_2 \frac{d i_p}{dt}$$

where $i_p$ is the current in the primary. Since $e_p$ drives the primary, $i_p$ is sinusoidal.

**Step 3: Output Voltage (Series Opposition)**
Because the secondaries are connected in series opposition:

$$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = -(M_1 - M_2) \frac{d i_p}{dt}$$

**Step 4: At Null Position ($x = 0$)**
The core is centered → flux links both secondaries equally → $M_1 = M_2$ → $e_{out} = 0$.

**Step 5: Displaced Position ($x \ne 0$)**
The mutual inductances are linear functions of displacement (in the operating range). Define sensitivities $k_1$ and $k_2$ (with $k_1 = -k_2$ by symmetry):

$$M_1 = M_0 + k_1 x$$

$$M_2 = M_0 - k_1 x$$

Subtracting:

$$M_1 - M_2 = 2 k_1 x$$

**Step 6: Final Output Equation**

$$e_{out} = -2 k_1 x \frac{d i_p}{dt} = 2 k_1 x \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$$

The peak (RMS) value is:

$$E_{out, peak} = K \cdot x$$

where $K = 2 k_1 \omega I_p$ is the sensitivity of the LVDT (in V/m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** In the linear operating range, the output voltage of an LVDT is **directly proportional to the core displacement $x$** from the null position, and the **sign of $x$** (direction) is indicated by the **phase** of the output relative to the primary.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (LVDT vs Potentiometer)

| Parameter | LVDT | Potentiometer |
|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | AC voltage (requires demodulation) | DC voltage (direct) |
| **Friction** | None (frictionless) | Yes (wiper contact) |
| **Resolution** | Theoretically infinite | Limited by wire pitch |
| **Hysteresis** | Negligible | Present |
| **Linearity** | Excellent (linear region) | Good |
| **Dynamic response** | Excellent | Limited by wiper mass |
| **Cost** | Higher | Low |
| **Life** | Very long | Limited by wear |

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Absolute Encoder Resolution

**Problem:** An 8-bit absolute encoder is used to measure angular position. Find its resolution in degrees and radians.

**Step 1:** Total positions = $2^n = 2^8 = 256$

**Step 2:** Angular resolution:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{256} = 1.40625°$$

**Step 3:** Convert to radians:

$$\theta_{res} = 1.40625° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} \approx 0.02454 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4:** Verification: $256 \times 0.02454 = 6.283 \approx 2\pi$ ✓

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Electromagnetic Flow Meter

**Problem:** Water flows through a 50 mm diameter pipe. A magnetic field of 0.1 T is applied perpendicular to the flow. If the induced EMF is 2.5 mV, find the flow velocity.

**Step 1:** Use $E = B \cdot v \cdot D$

**Step 2:** Solve for $v$:

$$v = \frac{E}{B \cdot D} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}}{0.1 \text{ T} \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$v = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Volumetric flow rate (for completeness):

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} \times 0.5 \approx 9.817 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\approx 0.982 \text{ L/s}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a sensor. List four static characteristics of a sensor.**

**Model Answer:**
A sensor is a device that detects a physical quantity (such as displacement, temperature, pressure) and converts it into an electrical signal which can be measured or recorded.

Four static characteristics:
1. **Accuracy** — Closeness of the measured value to the true value
2. **Sensitivity** — Ratio of change in output to change in input
3. **Linearity** — How well the output-input curve fits a straight line
4. **Hysteresis** — Difference in output for the same input during increasing vs decreasing cycles

*(Other acceptable: Resolution, Range, Repeatability, Drift)*

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between incremental and absolute encoders.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (counted from reference) | Unique binary word per position |
| Reference needed | Yes (home position) | No |
| Power loss effect | Loses position | Recovers automatically |
| Resolution | Depends on pulses/rev | $360°/2^n$ |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Complexity | Simpler | More complex |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an **LVDT**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An LVDT consists of:
- A hollow cylindrical former (non-magnetic)
- A **primary coil** $P$ wound on the central portion, energized by AC voltage $e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$
- Two identical **secondary coils** $S_1$ and $S_2$ wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition**
- A movable **soft-iron core** (armature) attached to the object whose displacement is to be measured

**Working (2 marks):**
When the core is at the **null (central) position**, the flux linkage with $S_1$ and $S_2$ is equal, so the induced EMFs are equal in magnitude but opposite in phase (due to series-opposition winding). Net output $E_{out} = 0$.

When the core moves toward $S_1$, flux through $S_1$ increases → $E_{s1}$ increases; flux through $S_2$ decreases → $E_{s2}$ decreases. The differential output becomes **positive** and proportional to displacement $x$.

Conversely, when the core moves toward $S_2$, $E_{out}$ becomes **negative** and proportional to $-x$.

The **phase** of the output tells the **direction** of displacement, and the **magnitude** tells the **amount**.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$e_{out} = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = -(M_1 - M_2)\frac{d i_p}{dt}$$

With $M_1 = M_0 + k_1 x$ and $M_2 = M_0 - k_1 x$:

$$M_1 - M_2 = 2 k_1 x$$

Therefore:

$$e_{out} = -2 k_1 x \frac{d i_p}{dt} = 2 k_1 \omega I_p x \cos(\omega t)$$

Peak value: $\boxed{E_{out, peak} = K \cdot x}$, where $K = 2 k_1 \omega I_p$ is the LVDT sensitivity.

**(b)** Explain the working of a **potentiometer** as a displacement sensor. What are its advantages and limitations? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (1 mark):**
A potentiometer consists of a resistive element (wire-wound or conductive plastic) with two fixed end terminals and a sliding wiper. Input voltage is applied across the end terminals; output is taken between the wiper and one end.

**Working (3 marks):**
If $R_t$ is the total resistance and the wiper is at a fractional position $x$ along the track, the resistance between the wiper and the reference end is $x \cdot R_t$. Using the voltage divider rule:

$$V_{out} = \frac{x \cdot R_t}{R_t} \cdot V_{in} = x \cdot V_{in}$$

For a **linear potentiometer**, $x$ is a linear distance. For a **rotary potentiometer**, $x$ is a fraction of the total angular travel. Thus $V_{out}$ is directly proportional to displacement.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Simple, low-cost construction
- Provides continuous analog output
- No external power required for sensing (uses excitation)
- Good for measuring large displacements
- Easy to interface

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Mechanical wear of wiper reduces life
- Contact friction affects accuracy of moving object
- Limited resolution (especially wire-wound type)
- Output is affected by temperature
- Electrical noise due to contact resistance variation

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**(a)** With diagrams, explain the working of an **optical encoder** (incremental and absolute types). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Incremental Encoder (3.5 marks):**
- A disc with **equally spaced radial slots** is mounted on the rotating shaft
- An **LED** (light source) and a **photodetector** are placed on opposite sides of the disc
- As the disc rotates, the slots alternately pass/block the light → photodetector outputs a **pulse train**
- Position is determined by **counting pulses** from a reference (index) mark
- Two photodetectors (Channel A and Channel B) are placed 90° out of phase — this gives **direction information** (quadrature)
  - If A leads B → CW rotation
  - If B leads A → CCW rotation

**Absolute Encoder (3.5 marks):**
- The disc has **multiple concentric tracks**, each with its own unique pattern of transparent and opaque segments
- Each track corresponds to one bit of a binary code (Gray code is preferred to avoid errors)
- For an n-bit encoder, there are $2^n$ unique positions
- Resolution:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{2^n}$$

- Each position produces a **unique n-bit binary word** simultaneously
- **No reference or homing is needed** — position is known instantly after power-on
- The output is fed to a parallel-input port of a microcontroller

**(b)** Explain the working of an **acoustic emission sensor** and a **piezoelectric accelerometer**. Compare their sensing elements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Acoustic Emission Sensor (3.5 marks):**
- AE sensors detect high-frequency transient elastic waves (20 kHz to 1 MHz) released during sudden material events like **crack propagation, fiber breakage, phase transformation, or leak**
- The sensing element is a **piezoelectric crystal** coupled to the test surface
- When the stress wave reaches the crystal, the crystal vibrates mechanically and generates a corresponding voltage (direct piezoelectric effect)
- The output is analyzed for **amplitude, frequency content, and arrival time** at multiple sensors → the source of emission is **triangulated**
- Application: structural health monitoring of pressure vessels, bridges, pipelines, aircraft

**Piezoelectric Accelerometer (3.5 marks):**
- Used to measure **vibration / acceleration** of a body
- Construction: A **seismic mass** $m$ is mounted on a **piezoelectric crystal**, which is fixed to the base
- When the base accelerates, the inertia of the seismic mass produces a force $F = m \cdot a$ on the crystal
- The crystal generates a charge $q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$ (where $d$ is the piezoelectric constant)
- The charge is converted to voltage by a charge amplifier
- Output is proportional to **acceleration**, not velocity or displacement

**Comparison (included in answer):**
- Both use piezoelectric crystals as sensing element
- AE sensor responds to **transient high-frequency stress waves**; accelerometer responds to **continuous vibration**
- AE sensor is **passive** (listens); accelerometer's output is due to the **inertial force of a known mass**
- Frequency range: AE sensor (kHz–MHz) >> Accelerometer (Hz–kHz)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
> 1. **LVDT Direction Logic:** Many students write that output voltage is positive regardless of displacement direction. This is WRONG. Direction is given by the **PHASE** of the AC output. State explicitly: *Core moves right → $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ → positive output*; *Core moves left → negative output*.
> 2. **Encoder Quadrature Confusion:** Students forget that two channels (A, B) are 90° out of phase — it is this phase difference that gives direction, not just pulse count.
> 3. **AE vs Ultrasonic:** Mixing up these two is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss. AE is **passive listening**; Ultrasonic testing uses **externally injected sound**.
> 4. **Potentiometer Equation:** Writing $V_{out} = V_{in}/x$ or missing the voltage-divider logic. Always derive from $V_{out} = I \cdot (xR_t) = \frac{V_{in}}{R_t} \cdot x R_t = x V_{in}$.
> 5. **Forgetting Units:** KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units in final answers. Always write "V", "mm", "Hz", "T", "m/s" etc.
> 6. **Piezoelectric sign convention:** Make clear that the **piezoelectric effect** (mechanical → electrical) is what sensors use; the **inverse piezoelectric effect** (electrical → mechanical) is what actuators use.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- ✅ **Potentiometer** = resistive divider; $V_{out} = x V_{in}$; contact wear is the main drawback
- ✅ **Incremental Encoder** = pulse counter; needs reference; cheaper
- ✅ **Absolute Encoder** = unique binary code per position; resolution = $360°/2^n$; no reference needed
- ✅ **Quadrature** = A leads B = CW; B leads A = CCW
- ✅ **LVDT** = primary in center, two secondaries in series opposition, soft-iron core
- ✅ **LVDT null** = $E_{out} = 0$; $E_{out}$ is linear in $x$ in the operating range; phase gives direction
- ✅ **LVDT advantages** = frictionless, infinite resolution, no hysteresis, robust
- ✅ **IR Sensor** = LED + photodiode; detects reflected IR; range typically 5–30 cm
- ✅ **Acoustic Emission** = passive listening to high-frequency stress waves; piezoelectric crystal
- ✅ **AE Frequency range** = 20 kHz – 1 MHz (well above audible)
- ✅ **Vibration Sensor** = piezoelectric accelerometer; $V_{out} \propto m \cdot a$ (acceleration, not velocity)
- ✅ **Tactile Sensor** = array of taxels; can be piezoresistive, capacitive, or piezoelectric
- ✅ **Capacitive tactile** equation: $C = \varepsilon A / d$ — as $d$ decreases (more pressure), $C$ increases
- ✅ **Flow Sensor Types** = turbine, ultrasonic, electromagnetic, vortex, orifice
- ✅ **Electromagnetic flow meter** equation: $E = B \cdot v \cdot D$ — needs conductive fluid only
- ✅ **Static characteristics** = accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, resolution, repeatability, drift
- ✅ **Sensors convert physical → electrical; Actuators convert electrical → physical**
- ✅ **Phase Sensitive Demodulator** is needed for LVDT output to get clean DC proportional to displacement
- ✅ **AE vs UT**: AE is passive (listens to defects), UT is active (sends waves and receives echo)
- ✅ **Resolution of encoder increases exponentially with bits**: 8-bit → 256 positions; 10-bit → 1024 positions
- ✅ **LVDT linearity range** is typically within $\pm 5$ mm; outside this, output is non-linear
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Actuators and Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Actuators and Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electrical Actuators

An **Electrical Actuator** is a mechatronic power-conversion device that transforms electrical energy into controlled mechanical motion (linear or rotary) in response to a command signal, typically a low-power control voltage or current. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, electrical actuators form the *musculature* of any automated system — they are the final control element that physically performs the work commanded by the controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** An electrical actuator is a transducer that converts an input electrical signal (voltage/current/PWM) into a precisely controlled mechanical output (displacement, velocity, or force/torque), forming the output stage of a mechatronic system.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a robotic arm in a car manufacturing plant. The *brain* (microcontroller/PLC) decides "move 5 cm to the right at 2 m/s." That decision is just a tiny electrical signal. But to actually push a heavy door panel, you need real physical force. The **electrical actuator** is like the *muscle* of this system — it takes the small electrical "thought" and amplifies it into powerful, controlled physical movement. A **solenoid** is like a single muscle twitch (push/pull), a **DC motor** is like a continuous rotating joint, and a **servo motor** is like a precision joint that can hold a specific angle — like your elbow.

## 1.2 Control Systems

A **Control System** is an interconnected arrangement of components that manages, commands, directs, or regulates the behavior of a dynamic system to achieve a desired output (reference/target). The controller compares the desired output with the actual output and generates a corrective action.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Fundamental Architectures:**
> - **Open-Loop Control:** The controller sends a command without any feedback. The system "trusts" the actuator blindly. *Example: A simple washing machine timer.*
> - **Closed-Loop Control (Feedback Control):** The controller continuously measures the actual output via a sensor, compares it with the desired value, and adjusts the actuator in real-time. *Example: A car's cruise control using PID logic.*

### The Two Pillars of Mechatronic Movement

| Pillar | What it does | Example Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Actuators** | Convert electricity → motion | Solenoids, DC motors, Stepper motors, Servo motors |
| **Control Systems** | Decide *how much* and *when* to actuate | PID controllers, PLCs, Microcontroller-based PWM drivers |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 module structure, these two pillars are tightly coupled: a solenoid needs a relay or driver circuit, a DC motor needs a PWM speed controller, and a stepper motor needs a sequenced open- or closed-loop controller.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standardized units and constants** are mandated by the KTU 2024 scheme and SI conventions for mechatronic problems:

- **Torque ($T$ or $\tau$):** $\text{N·m}$ (Newton-meter) — rotational force.
- **Angular velocity ($\omega$):** $\text{rad/s}$ — $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{\pi}{30} \text{ rad/s}$.
- **Power ($P$):** $\text{W}$ (Watt) — $P = T \cdot \omega$.
- **Back-EMF constant ($K_e$) and Torque constant ($K_t$):** $\text{V·s/rad}$ and $\text{N·m/A}$ respectively, with $K_e = K_t$ in SI units.
- **Step angle of stepper motor:** $\text{degrees/step}$ (e.g., **1.8°** for standard 200-step/rev motors).
- **PWM frequency:** $\text{Hz}$ or $\text{kHz}$ (typically **1–25 kHz** for DC motor drives).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electrical Actuators

Electrical actuators used in mechatronics are broadly classified based on the type of motion and the input signal:

### A. Solenoids (Linear / Limited-Stroke Actuators)
A **solenoid** is an electromechanical device consisting of a coil of wire wound around a movable ferromagnetic plunger. When current flows through the coil, it generates a magnetic field that pulls the plunger inward, producing a linear push/pull force.

- **Construction:** Coil + movable ferromagnetic core (plunger) + spring (for return).
- **Operation Principle:** Magnetic force $F \propto \dfrac{N^2 I^2}{\mu_0 A} \cdot \text{air-gap function}$.
- **Key Feature:** Binary (ON/OFF) or proportional; provides short-stroke linear motion.

> [!NOTE]
> Solenoids are *not* continuous-motion devices. They excel in valve actuation (pneumatic/hydraulic valves), door locks, and pin-pushing mechanisms.

### B. DC Motors (Brushed DC Motors)
A **Brushed DC Motor (BDC)** converts DC electrical power into continuous rotary mechanical power via the interaction of magnetic fields and commutated armature windings.

- **Components:** Stator (permanent magnet or field winding) + Rotor (armature with windings) + Brushes + Commutator.
- **Operation:** The commutator mechanically switches the armature current, ensuring unidirectional torque.

### C. Brushless DC Motors (BLDC)
A **Brushless DC Motor** electronically commutates the rotor position using Hall-effect sensors or back-EMF detection, eliminating physical brush wear.

- **Advantage:** Higher efficiency, longer life, better heat dissipation, higher speed range.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires an electronic controller (ESC) — more expensive.

### D. Stepper Motors
A **Stepper Motor** is a digital actuator that rotates in discrete angular steps in response to pulsed input signals. The shaft position is held without any feedback sensor.

- **Open-loop positioning:** Each pulse = one step. **No encoder needed.**
- **Step Angle:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot N_{phases/pole}}$ where $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth.
- **Standard Step Angles:** 1.8° (200 steps/rev), 0.9° (400 steps/rev), 7.5° (48 steps/rev).

### E. Servo Motors (Closed-Loop Rotary Actuators)
A **Servo Motor** is a rotary or linear actuator that allows for *precise control* of angular or linear position, velocity, and acceleration using a built-in position feedback sensor (potentiometer or encoder) and a closed-loop controller.

- **Three-wire hobby servo:** Power, Ground, PWM control signal (50 Hz, 1–2 ms pulse).
- **Industrial servo:** Brushless DC motor + encoder + PID controller in one package.

### F. AC Motors (Induction and Synchronous)
AC motors are widely used in industrial mechatronic systems, especially where mains AC power is available.

- **Induction Motor (Asynchronous):** Rotor slips behind the synchronous speed. $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ where $f$ = supply frequency, $P$ = number of poles. Slip $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$.
- **Synchronous Motor:** Rotor rotates at synchronous speed exactly. $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.

## 2.2 Power Electronics for Actuator Control

### H-Bridge Driver
An **H-bridge** is a 4-switch (transistor/MOSFET/IGBT) circuit that allows a DC motor to be driven in *both directions* and to be braked.

| Switch Pair ON | Motor Behavior |
|---|---|
| Q1 + Q4 | Forward rotation |
| Q2 + Q3 | Reverse rotation |
| Q1 + Q2 (or Q3 + Q4) | Braking / Regenerative |
| All OFF | Coasting |

### Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM)
**PWM** is the standard method for controlling the *average* voltage applied to a DC or servo motor. The motor's effective voltage is:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$

where $D$ is the **duty cycle**, defined as:

$$D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PWM does **not** change the supply voltage; it varies the *average* voltage by switching at high frequency. The motor's mechanical inertia averages out the pulses into smooth motion.

## 2.3 Control System Architectures

### Open-Loop Control
- No feedback sensor.
- The controller sends a fixed command and hopes the actuator does the job.
- **Pros:** Cheap, simple, fast response. **Cons:** No error correction, vulnerable to disturbances.

### Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control
The **basic closed-loop equation** is:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) = K_p \cdot \left[ r(t) - y(t) \right]$$

where:
- $r(t)$ = reference (setpoint)
- $y(t)$ = measured output
- $e(t)$ = error
- $u(t)$ = control signal
- $K_p$ = proportional gain

### PID Controller
The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** controller is the industrial workhorse of closed-loop control:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

| Term | Action | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **P** (Proportional) | Reacts to current error | Reduces rise time, but introduces steady-state offset |
| **I** (Integral) | Reacts to accumulated past error | Eliminates steady-state error |
| **D** (Derivative) | Reacts to rate of change of error | Damps oscillations, improves stability |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** PID controllers are used everywhere — drone flight controllers, CNC machine tool positioning, robotic arm joint control, oven temperature control, automotive cruise control, and even in injection molding machines for pressure control.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power from torque and angular speed | W |
| 2 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | DC motor armature voltage equation | V |
| 3 | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF in a DC motor | V |
| 4 | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Torque produced in a DC motor | N·m |
| 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | No-load speed relation | rad/s |
| 6 | $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of AC motor | rpm |
| 7 | $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Step angle of stepper motor (N = steps/rev) | degrees |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}}$ | PWM duty cycle | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 10 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | Average DC motor voltage via PWM | V |
| 11 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | PID control law | V or % |
| 12 | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 g^2}$ | Solenoid magnetic force (approximate) | N |
| 13 | $\tau = J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta}$ | Rotational load dynamics | N·m |
| 14 | $K_e = K_t$ (SI units) | Equivalence of motor constants | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $0 \le D \le 1$ for PWM.
> - $0 \le s \le 1$ for induction motor slip.
> - DC motor no-load speed $\omega_{nl} = V / K_e$.
> - DC motor stall torque $T_{stall} = K_t \cdot V / R_a$.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DC Motor Steady-State Torque–Speed Characteristic

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** that combines the voltage equation with the torque equation. We will derive the linear relationship between torque and speed.

### Given (Standard KTU Problem Statement)

A DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 1.0 \ \Omega$, back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.05 \ \text{V·s/rad}$, torque constant $K_t = 0.05 \ \text{N·m/A}$, and is supplied with $V = 24 \ \text{V}$. Find:
(a) The no-load speed and stall torque.
(b) The torque–speed equation.
(c) The operating point when the load torque is $T_L = 0.3 \ \text{N·m}$.

### Step 1: Write the Armature Voltage Equation

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature circuit, the supply voltage must overcome the resistive drop and the back-EMF:

$$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$

> **Logic:** The applied voltage drives current through the armature winding (ohmic drop) and also counteracts the back-EMF generated by the rotating armature in the magnetic field.

### Step 2: Substitute the Back-EMF Expression

The back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity via the motor constant $K_e$:

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

Substituting into the voltage equation:

$$V = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$$

> **Logic:** This is the fundamental electromechanical coupling equation of a DC motor — it links electrical input ($V$, $I_a$) with mechanical output ($\omega$).

### Step 3: Express Armature Current in Terms of Torque

For a DC motor, the electromagnetic torque is proportional to armature current via the torque constant $K_t$:

$$T = K_t \cdot I_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \dfrac{T}{K_t}$$

> **Logic:** Every ampere of armature current produces a fixed amount of torque, determined by the motor's geometry and magnetic field strength.

### Step 4: Substitute $I_a$ into the Voltage Equation

$$V = \left(\dfrac{T}{K_t}\right) R_a + K_e \omega$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\omega$ (Torque–Speed Equation)

Rearranging for $\omega$:

$$K_e \omega = V - \dfrac{T R_a}{K_t}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{V}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \cdot T$$

This is the **famous linear torque-speed equation** of a DC motor.

> **Logic:** The speed decreases linearly as torque increases, with slope $-R_a / (K_e K_t)$. The intercept (no-load speed) is $V / K_e$.

### Step 6: Calculate No-Load Speed ($\omega_{nl}$)

At no load, $T = 0$:

$$\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V}{K_e} = \dfrac{24}{0.05} = 480 \ \text{rad/s}$$

Converting to rpm:

$$n_{nl} = \omega_{nl} \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 480 \cdot 9.5493 \approx 4584 \ \text{rpm}$$

> **Logic:** Without any load torque, the motor spins at its maximum speed, limited only by friction and the back-EMF balancing the supply.

### Step 7: Calculate Stall Torque ($T_{stall}$)

At stall, $\omega = 0$ (rotor is locked). Then $E_b = 0$, and all voltage drops across $R_a$:

$$I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_a} = \dfrac{24}{1.0} = 24 \ \text{A}$$

$$T_{stall} = K_t \cdot I_{stall} = 0.05 \cdot 24 = 1.2 \ \text{N·m}$$

> **Logic:** The motor can deliver its maximum torque when fully stopped. This is the rated limit before the windings burn out.

### Step 8: Determine the Operating Point for $T_L = 0.3 \ \text{N·m}$

Using the torque-speed equation:

$$\omega = \dfrac{V}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \cdot T_L$$

$$\omega = 480 - \dfrac{1.0}{(0.05)(0.05)} \cdot 0.3$$

$$\omega = 480 - \dfrac{1.0}{0.0025} \cdot 0.3$$

$$\omega = 480 - 400 \cdot 0.3$$

$$\omega = 480 - 120 = 360 \ \text{rad/s}$$

Converting to rpm:

$$n = 360 \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi} \approx 3438 \ \text{rpm}$$

### Step 9: Calculate the Power Delivered to the Load

$$P = T_L \cdot \omega = 0.3 \cdot 360 = 108 \ \text{W}$$

> **Logic:** The operating point is found by intersecting the motor's torque-speed line with the load's torque requirement.

### Summary of Results

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| No-load speed | $\omega_{nl}$ | 480 | rad/s |
| No-load speed (rpm) | $n_{nl}$ | 4584 | rpm |
| Stall torque | $T_{stall}$ | 1.2 | N·m |
| Operating speed (at 0.3 N·m) | $\omega$ | 360 | rad/s |
| Operating power | $P$ | 108 | W |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stepper Motor Step Angle and Resolution

### Given
A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth ($N_r = 50$) and is driven in full-step mode with 2-phase excitation.

### Step 1: Identify the Pole Configuration

A standard 2-phase hybrid stepper has 2 stator phases, each with 50 rotor teeth and 50 stator teeth. The full-step mode energizes one phase at a time (or two with appropriate polarity) in sequence.

### Step 2: Compute the Step Angle

For a hybrid stepper, the step angle in full-step mode is:

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot N_{phases}} = \dfrac{360°}{50 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

### Step 3: Number of Steps per Revolution

$$N_{steps} = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s} = \dfrac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \ \text{steps/rev}$$

### Step 4: Resolution in Microstepping (1/8 microstepping)

$$\theta_{micro} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{8} = \dfrac{3.6°}{8} = 0.45°$$

> **Logic:** Microstepping subdivides each full step into smaller increments by using sinusoidal current control in the windings, giving smoother motion and finer resolution.

### Step 5: Linear Displacement per Step

If a leadscrew with pitch $p = 4 \ \text{mm/rev}$ is attached:

$$\Delta x = \dfrac{p}{N_{steps}} = \dfrac{4 \ \text{mm}}{100} = 0.04 \ \text{mm/step} = 40 \ \mu\text{m/step}$$

> **Logic:** This is how stepper motors achieve micron-level positioning in 3D printers and CNC machines — by combining the discrete step angle with mechanical reduction via a leadscrew.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Actuator

> [!TIP]
> This is a **high-frequency KTU short-answer / viva question** — "Compare DC, stepper, and servo motors for a given application."

| Feature | Brushed DC Motor | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor | Solenoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Motion type** | Continuous rotary | Discrete steps | Continuous rotary (closed-loop) | Linear, short stroke |
| **Position accuracy** | Poor (no feedback) | Good (open-loop) | Excellent (closed-loop) | Binary |
| **Holding torque at standstill** | None | High (detent torque) | High (active feedback) | High (when energized) |
| **Speed range** | High | Low–medium | High | N/A (instantaneous) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Low |
| **Driver complexity** | Simple (H-bridge) | Moderate (sequencer) | Complex (PID + encoder) | Simple (transistor/relay) |
| **Typical application** | Fans, pumps, toys | 3D printers, CNC | Robotics, automation | Valves, locks |
| **Open/Closed loop** | Usually open | Open | Closed | Open / on-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU:** If you need *position accuracy without a feedback sensor*, choose a stepper. If you need *high speed and dynamic response*, choose a servo. If you need *cheap, continuous rotation*, choose a brushed DC.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop DC Motor Position Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Reference Input r(t)"] --> nodeSum1(("Sum"))
    nodeFb["Feedback Signal y(t)"] --> nodeSum1
    nodeSum1 --> nodeE["Error e(t)"]
    nodeE --> nodeC["PID Controller"]
    nodeC --> nodeAmp["Power Amplifier H-Bridge"]
    nodeAmp --> nodeM["DC Motor Actuator"]
    nodeM --> nodeL["Load / Mechanical Output"]
    nodeL --> nodeOut["Output Position theta"]
    nodeOut --> nodeS["Position Sensor Encoder"]
    nodeS --> nodeFb
    style nodeR fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style nodeC fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style nodeM fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style nodeS fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b
```

> **Interpretation:** The reference position $r(t)$ is compared with the actual measured position $y(t)$ to produce an error $e(t)$. The PID controller computes the corrective signal, which is amplified by the H-bridge to drive the DC motor. The motor moves the load, which is sensed by the encoder and fed back — completing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: H-Bridge Motor Driver Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeV["Vsupply"] --> nodeQ1["Q1 MOSFET High Side Left"]
    nodeV --> nodeQ2["Q2 MOSFET High Side Right"]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeM1["Motor Terminal A"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeM2["Motor Terminal B"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeM0["DC Motor"]
    nodeM0 --> nodeM2
    nodeQ3["Q3 MOSFET Low Side Left"] --> nodeM1
    nodeQ4["Q4 MOSFET Low Side Right"] --> nodeM2
    nodeGnd["Ground"] --> nodeQ3
    nodeGnd --> nodeQ4
    nodeCtrl1["Logic Input IN1"] --> nodeQ1
    nodeCtrl1 --> nodeQ4
    nodeCtrl2["Logic Input IN2"] --> nodeQ2
    nodeCtrl2 --> nodeQ3
    style nodeV fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style nodeM0 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style nodeCtrl1 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style nodeCtrl2 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

> **Interpretation:** Four switches form an "H" shape around the motor. Activating Q1+Q4 drives current left-to-right (forward); activating Q2+Q3 drives current right-to-left (reverse). The logic inputs IN1 and IN2 (often from a microcontroller) select the direction.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Stepper Motor Full-Step Drive Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Pulses from Controller"] --> nodeSeq["Sequencer Translator"]
    nodeSeq --> nodeDR["Dual H-Bridge Driver"]
    nodeDR --> nodeA["Phase A Winding"]
    nodeDR --> nodeB["Phase B Winding"]
    nodeA --> nodeM["Rotor Shaft Output"]
    nodeB --> nodeM
    style nodeP fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style nodeSeq fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style nodeM fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> **Interpretation:** A digital pulse train is decoded by a translator IC (e.g., L297, A4988) which sequences the dual H-bridge to energize the two phases in the correct order, producing stepwise rotation.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: PID Controller Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR["Setpoint r t"] --> nodeSum(("e t = r minus y"))
    nodeFb["Process Variable y t"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeP["P Term Kp times e"]
    nodeSum --> nodeI["I Term Ki times integral e"]
    nodeSum --> nodeD["D Term Kd times derivative e"]
    nodeP --> nodeAdd(("Sum of Terms"))
    nodeI --> nodeAdd
    nodeD --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeOut["Control Output u t"]
    nodeOut --> nodeProc["Plant DC Motor"]
    nodeProc --> nodeFb
    style nodeP fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style nodeI fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style nodeD fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style nodeProc fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
```

> **Interpretation:** The error signal $e(t)$ is processed in three parallel branches — proportional, integral, and derivative — and summed to form the control output $u(t)$ that drives the plant. The plant's measured output closes the loop.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "duty cycle" in Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) and explain how it controls the average voltage applied to a DC motor.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **duty cycle ($D$)** of a PWM signal is defined as the ratio of the ON-time ($T_{on}$) to the total period ($T_{period}$) of the waveform:
> $$D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$
> The average voltage delivered to a DC motor is the product of the duty cycle and the supply voltage:
> $$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$$
> By varying $D$ between 0 and 1, the effective voltage across the motor terminals is controlled, thereby regulating the motor speed. The motor's mechanical inertia smooths out the high-frequency switching into continuous motion. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for working principle)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems. Give one mechatronic example of each.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
> |---|---|---|
> | Feedback | Absent | Present (sensor required) |
> | Accuracy | Low — no error correction | High — continuous error correction |
> | Disturbance rejection | Poor | Good |
> | Cost & complexity | Low | Higher |
>
> **Mechatronic Examples:**
> - **Open-Loop:** A stepper-motor-driven 3D printer axis (no encoder needed; pulses = steps).
> - **Closed-Loop:** A CNC spindle motor with encoder feedback and PID speed control. *(3 marks: 1 for table/differences, 1 for open-loop example, 1 for closed-loop example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. The student must answer **ONE full question** (either OR option). Both alternatives are provided below for practice.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Q: (a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a closed-loop DC motor position control system using a PID controller. Derive the transfer function of the DC motor (voltage-to-angular position) assuming the load dynamics are $\tau = J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The closed-loop system consists of: Reference input $r(t)$ → Summing junction → PID Controller $G_c(s)$ → Power amplifier (H-bridge, gain $K_a$) → DC motor $G_m(s)$ → Load (mechanical output $\theta(t)$) → Position sensor (encoder, gain $K_s$) → Feedback to summing junction. The error $e(t) = r(t) - K_s \theta(t)$ is fed to the PID controller.

**Step 2 — Motor Transfer Function Derivation (4 marks):**
From the armature voltage equation (in Laplace domain, with zero initial conditions):

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + E_b(s)$$

$$E_b(s) = K_e \cdot s \cdot \Theta(s)$$

The mechanical load equation:

$$T(s) = (J s^2 + b s) \Theta(s)$$

$$T(s) = K_t \cdot I_a(s)$$

Eliminating $I_a(s)$:

$$I_a(s) = \dfrac{T(s)}{K_t} = \dfrac{(J s^2 + b s) \Theta(s)}{K_t}$$

Substituting into the voltage equation:

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot \dfrac{(J s^2 + b s) \Theta(s)}{K_t} + K_e s \Theta(s)$$

Solving for $\Theta(s) / V_a(s)$:

$$\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s^2 + b s) + K_t K_e s}$$

> **Logic:** The denominator contains the electrical time constant ($L_a/R_a$) and the mechanical time constant ($J/b$). The coupling is via $K_t K_e$.

---

**(b) For a PID controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$, derive the open-loop transfer function of the complete system and discuss the role of each term ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) in improving the transient and steady-state response. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Open-Loop Transfer Function (3 marks):**
The forward path consists of PID → Amplifier → Motor:

$$G(s) = \left(K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) \cdot K_a \cdot \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s^2 + b s) + K_t K_e s}$$

The complete closed-loop transfer function (with unity feedback $H(s) = 1$) is:

$$\dfrac{\Theta(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Role of Each PID Term (4 marks):**

| Term | Effect on Response | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **$K_p$ (Proportional)** | Increases response speed; reduces rise time | Large $K_p$ causes overshoot and oscillation; cannot eliminate steady-state error completely |
| **$K_i$ (Integral)** | Eliminates steady-state error by integrating past errors | Slow response; can cause integral windup if not clamped |
| **$K_d$ (Derivative)** | Predicts future error; provides damping | Sensitive to noise; derivative kick on setpoint changes |

> **Conclusion:** Tuning the three gains balances the conflicting goals of *fast response*, *no overshoot*, *zero steady-state error*, and *disturbance rejection*. A well-tuned PID yields a critically damped or slightly underdamped response with settling time within specification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the construction and working principle of a stepper motor. Derive the expression for step angle of a hybrid stepper motor with 50 rotor teeth driven in full-step mode with 2-phase excitation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (2 marks):**
A **hybrid stepper motor** combines features of both variable-reluctance and permanent-magnet steppers. It has:
- A multi-toothed stator with two phases (A and A', B and B').
- A multi-toothed permanent-magnet rotor (typically axially magnetized).
- Two end caps with toothed structures offset by half a tooth pitch.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
When phase A is energized, rotor teeth align with stator A-teeth. When phase A is de-energized and phase B is energized, the rotor rotates by one step angle to align with B-teeth. Sequential energization of phases in the order A → B → A' → B' produces continuous stepwise rotation. The direction is reversed by changing the sequence order.

**Step 3 — Step Angle Derivation (3 marks):**
For a hybrid stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth and 2 phases, the full-step angle is:

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot N_{phases}}$$

Substituting $N_r = 50$, $N_{phases} = 2$:

$$\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{50 \times 2} = \dfrac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$

Number of steps per revolution:

$$N_{steps} = \dfrac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \ \text{steps/rev}$$

---

**(b) Compare stepper motors and servo motors in terms of (i) feedback, (ii) holding torque, (iii) speed, (iv) positioning accuracy, (v) applications. Discuss when each is preferred in a mechatronic system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Feedback | Open-loop (no sensor needed) | Closed-loop (encoder/Resolver mandatory) |
| (ii) Holding torque | High (detent + electromagnetic) | High (active PID maintains position) |
| (iii) Speed | Low to medium (typically < 2000 rpm) | High (3000–6000 rpm typical) |
| (iv) Positioning accuracy | Good (limited by step resolution; can lose steps under overload) | Excellent (encoder resolution + PID correction) |
| (v) Applications | 3D printers, small CNC, linear actuators, camera focus | Industrial robots, CNC spindles, AGVs, drone gimbals |

**When to prefer:**
- **Stepper:** When the load is predictable, low-speed, and budget is constrained. No encoder wiring simplifies assembly. Acceptable in 3D printers and educational CNC.
- **Servo:** When high dynamic performance, accuracy under varying load, and high speed are required. Critical in industrial automation where step-loss cannot be tolerated.

> **Conclusion:** For high-precision, high-dynamic mechatronic systems (e.g., robotic arms, CNC machining centers), servos are the industry standard. For low-cost, open-loop positioning (e.g., benchtop 3D printers), stepper motors dominate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** rpm must be converted to rad/s (or vice versa) when using $P = T \cdot \omega$. The factor is $2\pi/60$ — many students lose 2–3 marks by mixing units.
> 2. **Sign convention in back-EMF:** Always remember $E_b = K_e \omega$ **opposes** $V$ in the armature loop. The correct KVL is $V = I_a R_a + E_b$, not $V = I_a R_a - E_b$.
> 3. **Confusing stepper "step angle" with motor pole pairs:** Step angle depends on **rotor teeth count × phases**, not on the number of stator poles. Memorize the formula.
> 4. **PID tuning misconceptions:** Students often think "high $K_p$ is always better." The correct answer requires explaining the trade-off with stability and overshoot.
> 5. **PWM and analog confusion:** PWM is a digital switching technique — the motor's inductance and inertia filter it into an analog average voltage. Don't confuse PWM with true analog voltage control.
> 6. **Induction motor slip range:** $s = 0$ means synchronous speed (no torque), $s = 1$ means stalled rotor. Normal operation is $s = 0.01$ to $0.05$. Students often forget that $s \neq 0$ during running.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrical Actuators and Control Systems**

### A. Actuator Selection
- ✅ **Solenoid** → linear, short-stroke, ON/OFF (valves, locks).
- ✅ **Brushed DC motor** → cheap, continuous rotation, no precise positioning.
- ✅ **Brushless DC (BLDC)** → high efficiency, no brush wear, needs ESC.
- ✅ **Stepper motor** → open-loop precise steps, no feedback sensor, can lose steps under overload.
- ✅ **Servo motor** → closed-loop, high precision, encoder + PID, industrial-grade.
- ✅ **Induction motor** → AC-powered, robust, used in pumps/conveyors.
- ✅ **Synchronous motor** → exact speed control via frequency (used in robotics, VFDs).

### B. DC Motor Equations
- ✅ Armature: $V = I_a R_a + E_b$ (KVL).
- ✅ Back-EMF: $E_b = K_e \omega$.
- ✅ Torque: $T = K_t I_a$, with $K_e = K_t$ in SI.
- ✅ Torque–speed line: $\omega = V/K_e - (R_a / (K_e K_t)) T$.

### C. Stepper Motor Essentials
- ✅ $\theta_s = 360° / (N_r \cdot N_{phases})$.
- ✅ Standard values: 1.8° (200 steps), 0.9° (400 steps), 7.5° (48 steps).
- ✅ Modes: full-step, half-step, microstepping.
- ✅ Open-loop — can lose steps if overloaded.

### D. AC Motor Essentials
- ✅ $n_s = 120 f / P$ (synchronous speed).
- ✅ Slip: $s = (n_s - n_r) / n_s$.
- ✅ Induction motor needs a VFD for variable speed.

### E. Power Electronics
- ✅ **H-bridge:** 4 switches enable bidirectional DC motor control.
- ✅ **PWM duty cycle:** $D = T_{on} / T_{period}$, $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$.
- ✅ **Driver ICs:** L298N, L293D, BTS7960, DRV8871, A4988 (stepper).

### F. Control System Essentials
- ✅ **Open-loop:** no feedback, cheap, no error correction.
- ✅ **Closed-loop:** feedback sensor + controller (PID) ensures accuracy.
- ✅ **PID formula:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- ✅ **P** → speed, **I** → accuracy, **D** → damping.
- ✅ **Transfer function of DC motor:** $\Theta(s) / V_a(s) = K_t / [(L_a s + R_a)(J s^2 + b s) + K_t K_e s]$.

### G. KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- ✅ Always **state units** explicitly.
- ✅ Always **draw the block diagram** for control system questions — it carries easy 2–3 marks.
- ✅ For 14-mark derivations, **state the assumptions** (e.g., constant field current, linear magnetic circuit, no saturation).
- ✅ For comparison questions, use a **table** — it ensures all sub-parts are answered.
- ✅ Convert rpm ↔ rad/s using $n (\text{rpm}) = \omega (\text{rad/s}) \times 60 / (2\pi)$.
- ✅ Remember $K_e = K_t$ in SI units — this saves time in derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Electrical Actuators and Control Systems**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework, targeting Course Outcomes CO2 and CO3 (Design and analyze mechatronic systems with electrical actuation and feedback control).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Actuators (Basics Only): Electromechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, and Piezoelectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Actuators (Basics Only): Electromechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, and Piezoelectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition
An **Actuator** is a mechatronic device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into a mechanical motion or physical action (linear displacement, rotary motion, force, or torque). It is the **"muscle" of a mechatronic system**, executing the final command issued by the controller after signal conditioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope:** This topic is restricted to the **basic classifications** of actuators only. The advanced internal construction, control loops, and tuning of proportional-integral-derivative (PID) controllers for each actuator type are covered in later modules.

### 1.2 The Four Fundamental Actuator Families
According to the energy domain used for conversion, the four principal actuator types studied in this module are:

1. **Electromechanical Actuators** — Convert electrical energy to mechanical motion (e.g., DC servo motors, stepper motors, solenoids).
2. **Hydraulic Actuators** — Use incompressible fluid (oil) under high pressure to generate large linear/rotary forces.
3. **Pneumatic Actuators** — Use compressible gas (air) at moderate pressure for fast, low-cost motion.
4. **Piezoelectric Actuators** — Exploit the reverse piezoelectric effect to produce extremely precise nano-scale displacements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Body-as-a-System Model):**
> - **Electromechanical actuators** = *your arm muscles* — precise, fast, electrically controlled by nerves.
> - **Hydraulic actuators** = *an industrial crane's hydraulic ram* — incredibly strong, slow, fluid-driven.
> - **Pneumatic actuators** = *a bicycle pump* — uses air pressure, light and fast but weaker.
> - **Piezoelectric actuators** = *atomic force microscope probes* — produce microscopic, ultra-precise movements using crystalline deformation.

### 1.3 Energy Flow Diagram in a Mechatronic Actuator Loop
The generalized signal and energy flow in any actuator-driven mechatronic system follows this canonical order:

$$
\text{Controller Output} \rightarrow \text{Power Amplifier / Driver} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Load}
$$

The **key performance metrics** (must be memorized for KTU exams) are:
- **Stroke Length** *(L)* — Maximum linear displacement, measured in **mm** or **m**.
- **Force Output** *(F)* — Pushing/pulling capability, measured in **N**.
- **Speed** *(v)* — Velocity of motion, measured in **m/s** or **rad/s** (for rotary).
- **Bandwidth** — Frequency response range, typically in **Hz**.
- **Stiffness** — Resistance to deflection under load, measured in **N/m**.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Fact:** A common KTU question theme asks students to *match the actuator to the application*. Always link the application requirement (force, speed, precision) to the actuator's inherent physical principle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Actuator Type

#### 2.1.1 Electromechanical Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Interaction between magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors (Lorentz Force Law) or between magnetic fields and ferromagnetic materials.

**Key Sub-Types:**
- **DC Servo Motors** — Provide continuous rotary motion with excellent speed control via armature voltage.
- **Stepper Motors** — Rotate in discrete angular steps (typically **1.8°** per step for hybrid types). No feedback required (open-loop positioning).
- **Solenoids** — Produce linear motion via electromagnetic pull on a movable plunger.
- **Voice Coil Actuators** — Provide linear motion proportional to current, used in hard disk drives.

**Why Used:** High precision, fast response, easy computer interfacing, clean operation (no fluid leaks).

**How It Works (Step-by-Step):**
1. Controller sends a low-power signal (e.g., **PWM**, 0–5 V or 4–20 mA).
2. Power amplifier boosts the signal to drive the motor windings.
3. Magnetic field interaction produces a force/torque on the rotor.
4. Output shaft rotates or translates, driving the mechanical load.

#### 2.1.2 Hydraulic Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Pascal's Law — pressure applied to an incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.

**Key Sub-Types:**
- **Linear (Cylinder)** — Piston driven by pressurized oil (typical pressures: **70–350 bar**).
- **Rotary (Hydraulic Motor)** — Gear, vane, or piston-type motor driven by oil flow.

**Why Used:** Highest power density among all four types. Can move extremely heavy loads (e.g., excavator arms, aircraft landing gear).

**How It Works:**
1. Electric motor drives a hydraulic pump, pressurizing oil.
2. Directional control valve routes oil to one side of the cylinder.
3. Pressure acts on the piston area, generating force $F = P \times A$.
4. Exhaust oil returns to the reservoir through the valve.

#### 2.1.3 Pneumatic Actuators
**Operating Principle:** Compressed gas (typically air) expansion under pressure, governed by the ideal gas law.

**Key Sub-Types:**
- **Linear Cylinders** — Double-acting (air on both sides) or single-acting (spring return).
- **Pneumatic Motors** — Rotary vane or gear-type motors.
- **Air Muscles** — Contractile rubber bladder with braided mesh (Robotics).

**Why Used:** Clean (no oil leaks in sensitive environments like food/pharma), fast, cheap, safe in explosive atmospheres.

**How It Works:**
1. Compressor pressurizes air to typically **4–8 bar**.
2. Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL) unit conditions the air.
3. 5/2 or 3/2 solenoid valves direct airflow to the cylinder.
4. Air pressure pushes the piston, returning via exhaust ports.

#### 2.1.4 Piezoelectric Actuators
**Operating Principle:** **Reverse Piezoelectric Effect** — certain crystals (e.g., Lead Zirconate Titanate, PZT) deform mechanically when subjected to an electric field.

**Key Sub-Types:**
- **Stack Actuators** — Multiple thin ceramic discs stacked in series (stroke = N × d₃₃).
- **Bimorph / Unimorph** — Two-layer cantilever producing bending motion.
- **Tube Actuators** — Hollow cylinders for scanning probe microscopy.

**Why Used:** Sub-nanometer resolution, extremely high stiffness, no moving parts (no friction), vacuum compatible.

**How It Works:**
1. High-voltage driver applies **0–200 V** (or up to **1000 V** for stacks).
2. Electric field aligns the crystal dipoles.
3. Crystal lattice physically elongates along the poling axis.
4. Resulting displacement is in the range of **0.1% to 0.2%** of the actuator's length.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Physical Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Applicable Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic Force | $F = P \cdot A$ | $P$ = Pressure (Pa), $A$ = Piston area (m²) | Hydraulic Cylinder |
| 2 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = Q \cdot \Delta P$ | $Q$ = Flow rate (m³/s), $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop (Pa) | Hydraulic Motor |
| 3 | Pneumatic Force | $F = P \cdot A - F_{friction}$ | $P$ = Gauge pressure, $A$ = Piston area, $F_{friction}$ = Seal friction | Pneumatic Cylinder |
| 4 | DC Motor Torque | $T = K_t \cdot I$ | $K_t$ = Torque constant (Nm/A), $I$ = Armature current (A) | Electromechanical |
| 5 | DC Motor Speed | $\omega = \dfrac{V - I R_a}{K_e}$ | $V$ = Applied voltage, $R_a$ = Armature resistance, $K_e$ = Back-EMF constant | Electromechanical |
| 6 | Stepper Resolution | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_{steps}}$ | $N_{steps}$ = Number of full steps per revolution | Electromechanical |
| 7 | Piezo Displacement | $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V$ | $d_{33}$ = Piezo strain coefficient (m/V), $n$ = Number of layers, $V$ = Applied voltage | Piezoelectric |
| 8 | Piezo Generated Force | $F = A \cdot Y \cdot S$ | $A$ = Cross-section area, $Y$ = Young's modulus, $S$ = Strain | Piezoelectric |
| 9 | Solenoid Pull Force | $F \propto \dfrac{N^2 I^2 \mu_0 A}{2 g^2}$ | $N$ = Turns, $I$ = Current, $g$ = Air gap length | Electromechanical |
| 10 | Compressor Power (Pneumatic) | $P_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | $\dot{m}$ = Mass flow rate, $c_p$ = Specific heat, $T$ = Temperature | Pneumatic System |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — Real-World Mapping:**
> - The $F = P \cdot A$ equation dictates the **sizing of hydraulic presses** in automobile body stamping plants.
> - The $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V$ equation governs the design of **atomic force microscopes (AFMs)** used in semiconductor quality control.
> - The $T = K_t \cdot I$ relation is the **backbone of every CNC machine** and robotic arm joint controller.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Power Output

**Problem Context:** A hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of $D = 0.05$ m. The system pressure is $P = 100 \times 10^5$ Pa (i.e., **100 bar**). Calculate the theoretical pushing force and the hydraulic power if the flow rate is $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

#### Step 1: Identify the Physical Law
Pascal's Law states that fluid pressure acts equally on all parts of a confined fluid. The force generated is simply pressure multiplied by the area on which it acts.

#### Step 2: Compute the Piston Cross-Sectional Area
We treat the piston as a circle, so:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}
$$

#### Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values
Insert the diameter $D = 0.05$ m:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.05)^2}{4}
$$

#### Step 4: Evaluate the Square of the Diameter
Square the diameter value:

$$
(0.05)^2 = 0.0025 \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Step 5: Multiply by π
Apply the numerical value of π:

$$
\pi \cdot 0.0025 = 0.00785398 \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Step 6: Divide by 4
Complete the area calculation:

$$
A = \frac{0.00785398}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Step 7: Apply the Force Equation
Use $F = P \cdot A$:

$$
F = (100 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})
$$

#### Step 8: Multiply the Numerical Values
Calculate the product:

$$
F = 10^7 \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 1.9635 \times 10^4 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 9: State the Final Answer with Units
The pushing force is:

$$
\boxed{F \approx 19{,}635 \text{ N} \approx 19.6 \text{ kN}}
$$

#### Step 10: Compute the Hydraulic Power
The mechanical power output is pressure times volumetric flow:

$$
P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q
$$

#### Step 11: Substitute Values
Insert $P = 10^7$ Pa and $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s:

$$
P_{hyd} = (10^7) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4})
$$

#### Step 12: Evaluate the Product

$$
P_{hyd} = 2 \times 10^{3} \text{ W} = 2 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd} = 2 \text{ kW}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Piezoelectric Stack Displacement

**Problem Context:** A piezoelectric stack actuator consists of $n = 100$ ceramic layers. The piezoelectric strain coefficient is $d_{33} = 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m/V. A voltage of $V = 200$ V is applied. Calculate the total free displacement $\Delta L$.

#### Step 1: Recall the Governing Equation
The total free displacement of a stack actuator is the sum of displacements of all layers:

$$
\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V
$$

#### Step 2: Substitute the Numerical Values
Insert $d_{33} = 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m/V, $n = 100$, and $V = 200$ V:

$$
\Delta L = (3 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot (100) \cdot (200)
$$

#### Step 3: Simplify the Exponents
Group the powers of 10:

$$
\Delta L = 3 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 2 \times 10^{4}
$$

#### Step 4: Multiply the Numerical Prefactors
Compute $3 \times 2 = 6$:

$$
\Delta L = 6 \times 10^{-10+4}
$$

#### Step 5: Evaluate the Exponent Sum
Combine the powers:

$$
\Delta L = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}
$$

#### Step 6: Convert to Micrometers for Intuitive Understanding

$$
\Delta L = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 6 \text{ μm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L = 6 \text{ μm} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Actuator Types

The matrix below is a **high-yield KTU comparison table** — examiners often ask students to differentiate based on these exact parameters.

| Parameter | Electromechanical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Electrical | Pressurized oil (incompressible) | Compressed air (compressible) | Electric field (voltage) |
| **Typical Pressure/Voltage** | 12–240 V DC | 70–350 bar | 4–8 bar | 0–1000 V |
| **Force Range** | Low–Medium (0.1 N – 10 kN) | Very High (up to MN) | Medium (up to 50 kN) | Very Low (N range) |
| **Stroke Range** | Unlimited (rotary) / mm–m | mm – 2 m | mm – 1 m | nm – μm (very small) |
| **Position Accuracy** | High (μm with encoder) | Medium (mm with sensors) | Low (mm) | Ultra-High (sub-nm) |
| **Speed / Bandwidth** | High (kHz) | Low–Medium (10s of Hz) | High (100s of Hz) | Very High (kHz–MHz) |
| **Stiffness** | Medium | Very High | Low | Very High |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | High (pump, valves, hoses) | Low (FRL is cheap) | Very High |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High (oil, seals, leaks) | Medium (filters, drains) | Very Low (no moving parts) |
| **Cleanliness** | Clean | Risk of oil contamination | Very clean (food-grade possible) | Vacuum-clean |
| **Typical Application** | Robot joints, CNC axes | Excavators, presses | Packaging, pick-and-place | AFMs, optics, fuel injectors |
| **Energy Efficiency** | High (70–95%) | Medium (50–70%) | Low (10–30%) | High (no friction loss) |
| **Response Time** | ms | 10s of ms | ms | μs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Mechatronic Actuator Position Control Loop

The diagram below illustrates the universal signal and energy flow in an actuator-driven mechatronic system. This same block topology applies whether the actuator is a DC motor, a hydraulic cylinder, or a piezo stack.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input Signal] --> B[Controller eg PID]
    B --> C[Power Amplifier or Driver]
    C --> D[Actuator]
    D --> E[Mechanical Load]
    E --> F[Feedback Sensor]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioner]
    G --> B
    H[Energy Source] --> C
    H --> D
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** represents the closed-loop feedback path.
- The **Reference Input** is the desired setpoint (e.g., position, velocity).
- The **Controller** computes the error between reference and feedback.
- The **Driver** boosts the controller's low-power signal to actuator levels.
- The **Actuator** block is the focus of this module — its type is interchangeable.
- The **Feedback Sensor** (LVDT, encoder, strain gauge) closes the loop.

### 4.2 Sub-Type Classification Map for All Four Actuator Families

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ACT[ACTUATORS]
    ACT --> EMA[Electromechanical]
    ACT --> HYD[Hydraulic]
    ACT --> PNE[Pneumatic]
    ACT --> PIE[Piezoelectric]
    EMA --> EM1[DC Servo Motor]
    EMA --> EM2[Stepper Motor]
    EMA --> EM3[Solenoid]
    EMA --> EM4[Voice Coil Actuator]
    HYD --> HY1[Linear Cylinder]
    HYD --> HY2[Rotary Motor]
    HYD --> HY3[Servo Valve]
    PNE --> PN1[Single Acting Cylinder]
    PNE --> PN2[Double Acting Cylinder]
    PNE --> PN3[Pneumatic Muscle]
    PNE --> PN4[Rotary Vane Motor]
    PIE --> PI1[Stack Actuator]
    PI2[Bimorph Cantilever]
    PI3[Tube Scanner]
    PI1
    PI2
    PI3
    style ACT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EMA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style HYD fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PNE fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style PIE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Energy-Domain Conversion Schematic

This block diagram shows the exact energy transformation occurring in each actuator type, from input energy to mechanical work output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS [INPUT ENERGY DOMAINS]
        I1[Electrical Energy]
        I2[Hydraulic Oil Pressure]
        I3[Compressed Air Pressure]
        I4[Electric Field on Crystal]
    end
    subgraph CONVERSIONS [CONVERSION MECHANISM]
        C1[Lorentz Force in Magnetic Field]
        C2[Pascal Law on Piston]
        C3[Gas Expansion Force]
        C4[Reverse Piezoelectric Strain]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS [MECHANICAL OUTPUT]
        O1[Rotary or Linear Motion]
        O2[Linear Piston Stroke]
        O3[Linear Piston Stroke]
        O4[Nano Scale Displacement]
    end
    I1 --> C1 --> O1
    I2 --> C2 --> O2
    I3 --> C3 --> O3
    I4 --> C4 --> O4
    style INPUTS fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CONVERSIONS fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUTPUTS fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#000,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between hydraulic and pneumatic actuators.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Hydraulic Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **incompressible** oil as the working fluid. | Uses **compressible** air as the working fluid. |
| 2 | Operates at very high pressure, typically **70–350 bar**. | Operates at low pressure, typically **4–8 bar**. |
| 3 | Generates very high forces (suitable for excavators, presses). | Generates moderate forces (suitable for packaging, pick-and-place). |
| 4 | Requires pump, reservoir, and oil-seal maintenance. | Requires compressor and FRL unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator). |
| 5 | Higher energy efficiency (**50–70%**). | Lower energy efficiency (**10–30%**). |

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip:** Award 1 mark per valid comparison point. Listing any three distinct differences earns full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is the reverse piezoelectric effect? Name one application of a piezoelectric actuator.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **reverse piezoelectric effect** (also called the **converse piezoelectric effect**) is the phenomenon in which certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials (such as **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)**, Quartz, or Barium Titanate) undergo a **mechanical deformation** (strain) when an **external electric field** is applied across them.

The induced strain is given by:

$$
S = d \cdot E
$$

where $S$ is the mechanical strain, $d$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (m/V), and $E$ is the applied electric field (V/m).

**Application (1 Mark):** Used in **scanning probe microscopes (SPM/AFM)** for sub-nanometer positioning of the probe tip, or in **piezo fuel injectors** for precise diesel engine injection timing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
