**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **hydraulic actuator system** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the **force generated by a linear hydraulic cylinder**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **piezoelectric actuators** with **electromechanical (DC motor-based) actuators** based on the following parameters: (i) Stroke length, (ii) Resolution/Accuracy, (iii) Bandwidth, (iv) Stiffness, (v) Operating environment suitability, (vi) Typical applications, (vii) Cost.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle of a Hydraulic Actuator System (4 Marks):**

A hydraulic actuator system converts the mechanical energy from an electric motor into pressurized fluid energy using a pump. The pressurized oil is then directed by control valves to a hydraulic cylinder or motor, which converts the fluid energy back into mechanical motion.

**Step-by-step operation:**
1. **Prime Mover** — An electric motor drives a hydraulic pump.
2. **Pressure Generation** — The pump draws oil from the reservoir and delivers it at high pressure (typically **100–350 bar**).
3. **Directional Control** — A **4/3 or 5/3 directional control valve (DCV)** routes the high-pressure oil to the **cap end** or **rod end** of the cylinder.
4. **Force Generation** — The pressurized oil acts on the piston face, generating a linear force.
5. **Return Flow** — The oil from the opposite side returns to the reservoir through the DCV.
6. **Closed-Loop Control** (optional) — A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** or pressure transducer provides feedback to a servo valve for precise position/force control.

**Block Diagram of a Hydraulic Servo System:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Electric Motor] --> P[Hydraulic Pump]
    P --> R[Reservoir]
    P --> SV[Servo or DCV Valve]
    SV --> CYL[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    CYL --> LD[Mechanical Load]
    CYL --> LVDT[LVDT Position Sensor]
    LVDT --> AMP[Signal Amplifier]
    AMP --> C[Controller]
    C --> SV
    style M fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CYL fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Derivation of Force Generated (3 Marks):**

Let $P$ = fluid pressure (Pa), $D$ = piston diameter (m), and $A$ = piston area (m²).

**Step 1:** Piston area for a circular cross-section:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** By Pascal's Law, the fluid pressure acts uniformly on the piston:

$$
F = P \cdot A
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the area expression:

$$
F = P \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4:** Final boxed expression:

$$
\boxed{F = \frac{\pi \cdot P \cdot D^2}{4}}
$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1 Mark per parameter, with brief explanation):**

| Parameter | Electromechanical (DC Motor) | Piezoelectric Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Stroke Length** | **Unlimited** (rotary, with leadscrew can be meters) | **Very small** (typically 0.1–0.2% of stack length, ~10s of μm) |
| **(ii) Resolution/Accuracy** | High (encoder gives **μm-level** resolution) | **Ultra-high** (sub-nanometer, limited by voltage supply noise) |
| **(iii) Bandwidth** | High (up to **kHz** for small motors) | **Very high** (resonance often in **kHz–MHz** range) |
| **(iv) Stiffness** | Medium (depends on gearbox) | **Very high** (ceramics are inherently stiff, ~100 GPa) |
| **(v) Operating Environment** | Sensitive to dust, magnetic fields, but generally robust | **Vacuum compatible**, EMI-sensitive (high voltage drivers needed) |
| **(vi) Typical Applications** | Robot joints, CNC axes, conveyor drives, drones | AFM probes, optical mirror tilting, piezo fuel injectors, nanopositioning stages |
| **(vii) Cost** | Low to medium (commodity motors are cheap) | **High** (PZT ceramics + high-voltage amplifiers are expensive) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write the derivation for force as $F = m \cdot a$ — this is Newton's law, NOT Pascal's law. Always start with $F = P \cdot A$ for hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In the comparison, students list "advantages/disadvantages" instead of comparing parameter-by-parameter. KTU's official marking key awards marks **only when both columns are filled for the same parameter**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to include the units in the final numerical answers. Always write **N** (not just "19,635").

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **basic working of an electromechanical actuator system using a DC servo motor**. State the relevant equations for torque and speed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **double-acting pneumatic cylinder** has a piston diameter of **40 mm** and a rod diameter of **12 mm**. The supply pressure is **6 bar** (gauge). Calculate: (i) The extending force, (ii) The retracting force, (iii) The air consumption per minute if the piston stroke is **100 mm** and the cycle rate is **10 cycles/min**.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working of DC Servo Motor Actuator System (4 Marks):**

**Step 1: System Overview.** A DC servo motor-based electromechanical actuator system consists of a **controller** (microcontroller/PLC), a **PWM power amplifier** (H-bridge driver), the **DC servo motor** itself, a **gearbox** (optional, for torque multiplication), and a **feedback sensor** (incremental encoder or resolver).

**Step 2: Reference Signal.** The controller receives a reference signal specifying the desired position/velocity.

**Step 3: Error Generation.** The encoder measures the actual shaft position and returns it to the controller.

**Step 4: Control Law.** The controller applies a control algorithm (e.g., PID) to compute the required voltage.

**Step 5: PWM Actuation.** The H-bridge driver modulates the armature voltage using **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**.

**Step 6: Motor Response.** The motor develops torque proportional to the armature current.

**Step 7: Mechanical Output.** The shaft rotates, driving the load through the gearbox.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Position] --> CTRL[PID Controller]
    ENC[Encoder on Motor Shaft] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> DRV[H Bridge PWM Driver]
    DRV --> MOT[DC Servo Motor]
    MOT --> GB[Gearbox]
    GB --> LD[Mechanical Load]
    style REF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CTRL fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MOT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style GB fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Key Equations (3 Marks):**

**Torque Equation** — The electromagnetic torque developed by a DC motor is directly proportional to the armature current:

$$
T = K_t \cdot I_a
$$

where $K_t$ is the **torque constant** (Nm/A) and $I_a$ is the armature current (A).

**Speed Equation** — The no-load speed is determined by the back-EMF:

$$
\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a \cdot R_a}{K_e}
$$

where $V_a$ is the applied armature voltage, $R_a$ is the armature resistance, and $K_e$ is the **back-EMF constant** (V·s/rad). Note: In SI units, $K_t = K_e$.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Piston diameter: $D = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m
- Rod diameter: $d = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m
- Supply gauge pressure: $P = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Stroke length: $L = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- Cycle rate: $N = 10$ cycles/min

**(i) Extending Force (2 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate the full piston area (since the rod side is the exhaust side during extension, full pressure acts on the cap):

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.04)^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** Square the diameter:

$$
D^2 = 0.0016 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by π and divide by 4:

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.0016}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Apply $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{cap}$:

$$
F_{ext} = (6 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.2566 \times 10^{-3})
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the product:

$$
F_{ext} = 7.5398 \times 10^2 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ext} \approx 754 \text{ N}}
$$

**(ii) Retracting Force (2 Marks):**

**Step 1:** During retraction, pressure acts on the rod-side area. The effective area is the piston area minus the rod area:

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.012)^2}{4} = 1.13097 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract:

$$
A_{eff} = A_{cap} - A_{rod} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} - 1.13097 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$
A_{eff} = 1.1435 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Apply $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{eff}$:

$$
F_{ret} = (6 \times 10^5) \cdot (1.1435 \times 10^{-3}) = 686.1 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ret} \approx 686 \text{ N}}
$$

**(iii) Air Consumption Per Minute (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Total air volume per cycle (both sides, since it's double-acting):

$$
V_{cycle} = A_{cap} \cdot L + A_{eff} \cdot L
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$
V_{cycle} = (1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1) + (1.1435 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1)
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term:

$$
V_{cycle} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} + 1.1435 \times 10^{-4} = 2.4001 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by cycle rate (10 cycles/min):

$$
V_{air} = 2.4001 \times 10^{-4} \times 10 = 2.4001 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to liters for practical relevance (1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$
V_{air} = 2.4 \text{ L/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{air} \approx 2.4 \text{ liters/min}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to subtract the rod area for the retracting force calculation. The retracting force is **always less** than the extending force for a double-acting cylinder — if the student's answer shows otherwise, full marks are deducted.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In the air consumption problem, students often calculate only **one side's** volume. A double-acting cylinder consumes air on **both** extension and retraction strokes.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Mixing up gauge pressure and absolute pressure. The **6 bar** given is **gauge pressure** (above atmospheric), which is the correct value to use for force calculations (since the exhaust side is at atmospheric pressure).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to convert units (mm → m, bar → Pa) before substitution. This is the **#1 source of wrong numerical answers** in KTU exam scripts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] An **actuator** is the output muscle of a mechatronic system — it converts control signals into physical motion.
- [x] The **four types** to remember: **Electromechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Piezoelectric**.
- [x] **Electromechanical** = electrical energy → magnetic field interaction → motion (Lorentz force). Examples: DC motor, stepper, solenoid.
- [x] **Hydraulic** = high-pressure oil → piston force via **Pascal's Law** ($F = P \cdot A$). Pressure range: **70–350 bar**.
- [x] **Pneumatic** = compressed air → piston force. Pressure range: **4–8 bar**. Compressible fluid → less precise but cheaper.
- [x] **Piezoelectric** = electric field on crystal → nano-scale strain via the **reverse piezoelectric effect**. Equation: $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot n \cdot V$.
- [x] Hydraulic force $\gg$ Pneumatic force (for same cylinder size) due to **much higher operating pressure**.
- [x] **Retracting force** of a double-acting cylinder is **less than** the extending force (rod-side area < cap-side area).
- [x] For **DC motors**, torque $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ and speed $\omega = (V_a - I_a R_a)/K_e$.
- [x] **Piezo actuators** offer the **highest precision** (sub-nm) but the **smallest stroke** (μm).
- [x] **Hydraulic actuators** offer the **highest force density** but suffer from oil leakage and high maintenance.
- [x] Always use **SI units** (Pa, m³/s, m, N) in derivations — KTU examiners actively deduct marks for inconsistent unit usage.
- [x] A **double-acting pneumatic cylinder** consumes air on **both strokes**; a single-acting cylinder consumes air on **only the extension stroke**.
- [x] **Stepper motors** are **open-loop** positioners — no encoder required for basic stepping.
- [x] **Piezo materials** in common use: **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** — the industry standard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Relays and Solenoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Relays and Solenoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Relays — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Relay (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **relay** is an *electromechanically operated, electrically controlled switch* in which a low-power control signal energizes an electromagnetic coil, which in turn mechanically actuates one or more sets of contacts to switch (open/close) a separate, often high-power, load circuit. The control (input) side and the load (output) side are **galvanically isolated** — there is no direct electrical connection between them.

In Mechatronics, relays are classified as **binary actuators** (ON/OFF) and form the fundamental interface between the *low-current electronic control domain* (microcontrollers, PLCs, logic circuits) and the *high-current power domain* (motors, solenoids, heaters, lamps).

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a relay as a "power translator with a translator hat."** Imagine you (a small 5 V microcontroller) want to turn on a giant industrial water pump running on **230 V AC, 10 A**. You cannot directly touch that circuit — it's dangerous and your tiny signal is too weak. So you whisper your command into a small electromagnet (the coil). The electromagnet becomes magnetic and pulls a mechanical lever (the armature), which physically closes a switch connected to the pump circuit. The pump turns on. The relay "translates" your tiny whisper into a strong physical action — without any electrical wire connecting you to the pump.

### 1.2 Solenoids — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Solenoid (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **solenoid** is a *cylindrical electromagnetic actuator* consisting of a helical coil of insulated wire wound around a non-permanent (or sometimes permanent) ferromagnetic core/plunger. When current flows through the coil, a *concentrated, nearly uniform magnetic field* is produced along the coil's axis, which exerts a *longitudinal mechanical force* on the plunger, producing **linear (push/pull) motion** that can do useful mechanical work.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a solenoid as a "magnetic push-pin."** When you send current through a wire wrapped tightly like a spring, the inside of that spring temporarily turns into a magnet. If a steel rod sits inside the spring, the magnetic force *sucks the rod inward* (or pushes it out, depending on design), creating a strong linear shove. Car door latches, washing-machine water valves, pinball flippers, and starter motors all use this exact "magnetic shove" principle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants are used repeatedly in relay/solenoid analysis and **must be memorized**:
> - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \text{H/m} \approx \mathbf{1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \; H/m}$
> - **Relative permeability of soft iron core:** $\mu_r \approx 2000 \text{ to } 6000$ (varies with material grade)
> - **Absolute permeability:** $\mu = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r$
> - **Standard control voltages for industrial relays:** **5 V DC, 12 V DC, 24 V DC, 110 V AC, 230 V AC**
> - **Standard coil resistance range:** **50 Ω to 10 kΩ** (depending on voltage class)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Relay (Step-by-Step)

A relay is built from the following parts; each plays a critical role:

1. **Coil (Electromagnet):** A bobbin wound with many turns of fine enameled copper wire (typically **500–10,000 turns**). When energized, it produces a magnetomotive force (MMF).
2. **Core (Iron yoke):** A soft-iron magnetic circuit that concentrates the flux produced by the coil.
3. **Armature:** A movable ferromagnetic lever attracted by the coil's flux when energized.
4. **Contacts:** The actual switching elements — labeled **COM (Common), NO (Normally Open), NC (Normally Closed)**.
5. **Spring (Return spring):** Returns the armature to the de-energized position when the coil is switched off.
6. **Yoke / Frame:** Mechanical housing that completes the magnetic circuit and provides mounting.

**Operational Logic (The "Why" and "How"):**

- **De-energized state:** Spring holds the armature against the **NC** contact. The COM-NC path is closed; COM-NO is open.
- **Energized state:** Coil current produces flux → flux attracts the armature → armature pivots → COM now touches **NO** and disconnects from **NC**.
- **Critical threshold:** The relay only switches when coil current exceeds the **pull-in (pick-up) current**. It releases only when current falls below the **drop-out (hold) current**. The difference (hysteresis) prevents chattering.

### 2.2 Constructional Anatomy of a Solenoid (Step-by-Step)

1. **Helical Coil:** Cylindrical winding of insulated copper wire.
2. **Plunger / Armature:** A movable ferromagnetic core (usually soft iron) that sits partially inside the coil.
3. **Yoke / Flux return path:** Outer steel shell that completes the magnetic circuit.
4. **Stopper / End cap:** Limits plunger travel.
5. **Return spring (optional):** Pushes the plunger back to the rest position when the coil is de-energized.

**Operational Logic:**

- When energized, magnetic flux lines try to **minimize the air-gap reluctance** in the magnetic circuit.
- The plunger is therefore drawn *into* the coil (linear pulling force).
- Force depends on **(flux density)²** — meaning **doubling the current roughly quadruples the force**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable.** Memorize symbol definitions and SI units.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $B = \mu_0 \, \mu_r \, n \, I$ | Magnetic flux density inside a coil | $B$ = Tesla (T); $n$ = turns/m; $I$ = Ampere (A) |
| 2 | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Magnetic flux linkage | $\Phi$ = Weber (Wb); $A$ = m² |
| 3 | $\text{MMF} = N \cdot I$ | Magnetomotive force | $N$ = turns; $I$ = A; unit = Ampere-turns (At) |
| 4 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | Reluctance of magnetic path | $l$ = m; $A$ = m²; $\mathcal{R}$ = At/Wb |
| 5 | $\Phi = \dfrac{\text{MMF}}{\mathcal{R}}$ | Hopkinson's Law (magnetic Ohm's Law) | — |
| 6 | $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ | Maxwell's magnetic force on plunger | $F$ = Newton (N); $A$ = m² |
| 7 | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 \, l^2}$ | Solenoid pulling force (approximate) | $l$ = coil length (m) |
| 8 | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}$ | Inductance of a long solenoid | $L$ = Henry (H) |
| 9 | $U_B = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Magnetic energy stored in coil | $U_B$ = Joule (J) |
| 10 | $V_{coil} = I_{coil} \cdot R_{coil}$ | Ohm's law for relay coil | $V$ = V; $I$ = A; $R$ = Ω |
| 11 | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \cdot I_{coil}$ | Holding power dissipation of coil | $P$ = Watt (W) |
| 12 | $t_{operate} = \dfrac{L}{R} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_s - V_{pickup}}\right)$ | Relay operate time | $t$ = seconds (s) |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on formula #6 and #7:** Force scales as $I^2$, which means a solenoid has **no polarity dependency** — AC or DC produce force in the same direction. However, AC solenoids experience a *zero-force point* every half cycle, which causes **chatter/buzz** at 50/60 Hz. A shading coil fixes this.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Starter solenoids engage the flywheel gear to crank the engine.
- **Industrial automation:** Relays switch motor contactors; solenoids actuate pneumatic/hydraulic valves.
- **Domestic:** Washing-machine inlet valves (solenoids), refrigerator compressor starters (relays).
- **Safety systems:** Relay-based interlocks ensure a machine cannot start until a guard door is closed.
- **Mechatronics linkage:** PLC output (24 V) → Relay coil → Relay contact closes → 230 V motor circuit energized. The relay is the **electrical bridge** between logic and power.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Force Produced by a Solenoid Plunger

We derive the **Maxwell stress equation** for the axial pulling force on a solenoid plunger.

**Step 1 — Define the magnetic pressure at the air gap.**

The energy stored in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ in volume $V$ is:

$$U_B = \dfrac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} \cdot V$$

Since $V = A \cdot x$ (where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the air gap and $x$ is the gap length), we can write the energy density (energy per unit volume) as:

$$u_B = \dfrac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of virtual work.**

A small virtual displacement $\delta x$ of the plunger reduces the air-gap volume by $A \cdot \delta x$. The change in stored energy equals the mechanical work done by the magnetic force:

$$F \cdot \delta x = -\delta U_B$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the force.**

Treating $B$ as approximately constant (small displacement assumption):

$$F = -\dfrac{dU_B}{dx} = -\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{B^2 \, A \, x}{2 \mu_0}\right)$$

The negative sign arises because reducing the gap (decreasing $x$) releases energy, producing a force that *pulls* the plunger in. Taking the magnitude:

$$\boxed{\,F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of coil current and turns.**

For a long solenoid, $B = \mu_0 \, n \, I = \mu_0 \, (N/l) \, I$. Substituting:

$$F = \dfrac{(\mu_0 N I / l)^2 \, A}{2 \mu_0} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l^2}$$

$$\boxed{\,F = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 l^2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** The force depends on $I^2$ — this is why solenoid force is **direction-insensitive** and why doubling current *quadruples* force.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Relay vs. Solenoid Comparison

| Parameter | Electromagnetic Relay | Solenoid |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| **Primary function** | Switching (open/close a circuit) | Actuating (produce linear motion) |
| **Output form** | Contact closure (electrical) | Mechanical displacement |
| **Output quantity** | Binary (ON/OFF) | Linear stroke (mm to cm) |
| **Typical force/torque** | Negligible (just contact pressure) | 1 N to several hundred N |
| **Galvanic isolation** | Yes (coil↔contact) | N/A (single circuit) |
| **Spring return?** | Yes (always) | Often yes |
| **Power level** | Low (signal level) to high (kW contactor) | Medium (tens of watts) |
| **Failure mode** | Contact welding, coil burnout | Plunger jamming, coil burnout |
| **Control signal** | Voltage/current at coil terminals | Same |
| **Lifespan** | $10^5$ – $10^7$ operations | $10^6$ – $10^8$ strokes |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A DC solenoid has **N = 1000 turns**, coil length **l = 0.05 m**, plunger cross-section **A = 4 × 10⁻⁴ m²**, and is energized with a current of **I = 2 A**. Compute (a) the flux density inside the coil, and (b) the axial pulling force on the plunger. (Use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.)

**Solution:**

**(a) Flux density:**

$$B = \mu_0 \cdot \dfrac{N}{l} \cdot I$$

$$B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times \dfrac{1000}{0.05} \times 2$$

Let us compute stepwise:

$$\dfrac{N}{l} = \dfrac{1000}{0.05} = 20{,}000 \;\text{turns/m}$$

$$\dfrac{N \cdot I}{l} = 20{,}000 \times 2 = 40{,}000 \;\text{At/m}$$

$$B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 40{,}000 = 1.6\pi \times 10^{-2} \;\text{T}$$

$$B \approx 0.0503 \;\text{T} = \mathbf{50.3 \; mT}$$

**(b) Pulling force:**

$$F = \dfrac{B^2 \, A}{2 \mu_0}$$

$$F = \dfrac{(0.0503)^2 \times (4 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

Numerator:

$$(0.0503)^2 = 2.530 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$2.530 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.012 \times 10^{-6}$$

Denominator:

$$2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 8\pi \times 10^{-7} = 2.513 \times 10^{-6}$$

Therefore:

$$F = \dfrac{1.012 \times 10^{-6}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \mathbf{0.403 \;\text{N}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** Always carry units through every step. Many KTU valuation scripts *deduct a mark* if the final answer is unitless or has an incorrect unit.

### 3.4 Derivation: Pick-up and Drop-out Voltages of a Relay

A relay has a **definite pull-in** and **drop-out** voltage because of magnetic hysteresis.

- The **pull-in voltage $V_{PI}$** is the minimum coil voltage needed to overcome the spring force and contact-gap friction.
- The **drop-out voltage $V_{DO}$** is the coil voltage at which the spring can pull the armature back. Because the armature is now *closer* to the core (gap reduced), less MMF is needed — so $V_{DO} < V_{PI}$.

If $R$ is the coil resistance and $N$ the turns, then:

$$V_{PI} = I_{PI} \cdot R = \dfrac{\text{MMF}_{PI}}{N} \cdot R$$

$$V_{DO} = I_{DO} \cdot R = \dfrac{\text{MMF}_{DO}}{N} \cdot R$$

**Hysteresis ratio:**

$$k_h = \dfrac{V_{DO}}{V_{PI}} \;\in\; [0.3, \, 0.7] \;\text{typically}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **24 V DC relay**, typical values are $V_{PI} \approx 18\text{ V}$ and $V_{DO} \approx 7\text{ V}$. Designing the supply below $V_{DO}$ guarantees the relay releases — essential in **fail-safe** mechatronic systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Relay Switching System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal and power flow in a mechatronic relay-based switching application, showing how the low-power control domain interacts with the high-power load domain through the relay's galvanic isolation barrier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrlA[Microcontroller 5V GPIO] -->|Logic HIGH 5V| drvA[Driver Transistor 2N2222]
    drvA -->|Energizing current 80 mA| coilA[Relay Coil 24V DC 1.2k ohm]
    coilA -->|Magnetic flux| armA[Armature Pivots]
    armA -->|Mechanical motion| conA[Contact Set COM NO NC]
    conA -->|Closes NO path| loadA[230V AC Motor 2HP]
    pwrA[24V DC Supply] --> coilA
    mainsA[230V AC Mains] --> loadA
    flyA[Freewheeling Diode 1N4007] -.->|Protects driver| drvA
    snubA[RC Snubber 100 ohm 100 nF] -.->|Suppresses arc| conA

    classDef ctrl fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#fff4c2,stroke:#7a5b00,color:#000
    classDef coil fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000
    classDef contact fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a5b0a,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#e6d6ff,stroke:#3a006b,color:#000
    classDef pwr fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef protect fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000

    class ctrlA ctrl
    class drvA drv
    class coilA coil
    class conA,armA contact
    class loadA load
    class pwrA,mainsA pwr
    class flyA,snubA protect
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **left side** is the low-power control domain (5 V logic).
- The **center column** is the relay itself (coil + armature + contacts).
- The **right side** is the high-power load domain (230 V motor).
- The **dashed lines** show protective components (freewheeling diode for the inductive coil, RC snubber for the switching contact arc).

### 4.2 Solenoid Operational State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DeEnergized
    DeEnergized: State A - De-energized
    DeEnergized: Plunger at rest position
    DeEnergized: Spring extended
    DeEnergized: Air gap = maximum
    DeEnergized --> Pulling: Energize coil - I exceeds pull-in threshold
    Pulling: State B - Pulling stroke
    Pulling: Flux density rising
    Pulling: Force F proportional to I squared
    Pulling: Plunger accelerates inward
    Pulling --> Holding: Plunger reaches end of stroke
    Holding: State C - Holding position
    Holding: Air gap = minimum
    Holding: Reduced MMF sufficient to hold
    Holding: Continuous coil current
    Holding --> Releasing: De-energize coil - I falls below drop-out
    Releasing: State D - Releasing stroke
    Releasing: Spring pushes plunger back
    Releasing: Flux collapsing induces back EMF
    Releasing --> DeEnergized: Plunger returns to rest
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Solenoid Valve (Process-Control Mechatronic System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    plcA[PLC Output Module 24V] --> termA[Solenoid Coil Terminals]
    termA --> windA[Helical Winding N turns]
    windA -->|Generates axial B field| gapA[Air Gap in Magnetic Circuit]
    gapA -->|Pulls with force F| plungerA[Plunger Soft Iron]
    plungerA -->|Translates| stemA[Valve Stem]
    stemA -->|Lifts| sealA[Rubber Diaphragm or Poppet]
    sealA -->|Opens| flowA[Process Fluid Path]
    flowA --> procA[Downstream Actuator Cylinder]
    yokeA[Yoke Flux Return] --> gapA
    springA[Return Spring] --> plungerA

    classDef input fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#003a75,color:#000
    classDef magnetic fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#7a0000,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#0a5b0a,color:#000
    classDef fluid fill:#e6d6ff,stroke:#3a006b,color:#000

    class plcA,termA input
    class windA,gapA,yokeA magnetic
    class plungerA,stemA,sealA,springA mech
    class flowA,procA fluid
```

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic: NC vs. NO Contact Operation in a Relay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DeEnergized_Relay
        comA[COM Terminal] -->|Closed path| ncA[NC Terminal]
        comA -.->|Open path| noA[NO Terminal]
    end
    subgraph Energized_Relay
        comB[COM Terminal] -.->|Open path| ncB[NC Terminal]
        comB -->|Closed path| noB[NO Terminal]
    end
    DeEnergized_Relay -->|Coil energized I greater than pull-in| Energized_Relay
    Energized_Relay -->|Coil de-energized I less than drop-out| DeEnergized_Relay

    classDef closed fill:#b6f0b6,stroke:#0a5b0a,color:#000
    classDef open fill:#f0b6b6,stroke:#5b0a0a,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#fff4c2,stroke:#7a5b00,color:#000

    class ncA,noB closed
    class noA,ncB open
    class comA,comB,ncA,noA,ncB,noB term
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Solid arrow lines = current-conducting path. Dotted lines = open circuit. The COM terminal is the *pivot* of the switch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Define a relay. With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of an electromagnetic relay.**

**Model Answer (3-mark distribution):**

- **(1 mark) Definition:** A relay is an electrically controlled switch that uses an electromagnet to mechanically operate one or more sets of contacts. The control circuit (coil) and the load circuit (contacts) are electrically isolated.
- **(1 mark) Working — Energized state:** When the coil is energized, the resulting magnetic flux attracts the movable armature. The armature pivots and closes the normally-open (NO) contacts while opening the normally-closed (NC) contacts, thereby switching the load circuit.
- **(1 mark) Working — De-energized state:** When the coil is de-energized, the return spring pulls the armature back to its rest position, restoring the NC contact and opening the NO contact.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Style]**

**Differentiate between a relay and a solenoid. Mention at least three distinguishing points.**

**Model Answer (3-mark distribution):**

| S.No. | Relay | Solenoid |
|-------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Output is **electrical contact switching** (open/close). | Output is **linear mechanical motion** of a plunger. |
| 2 | Has **galvanic isolation** between coil and contacts. | Coil and plunger are in the **same magnetic circuit** (no separate contact set). |
| 3 | Used to **control high-power circuits** using a low-power signal. | Used to **produce linear actuation force** for valves, latches, and locks. |
| 4 | Contacts are rated in **amperes and volts**. | Plunger is rated in **newtons and stroke length (mm)**. |

*(Any 3 valid distinguishing points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

---

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] — (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **construction and working of a single-pole double-throw (SPDT) electromagnetic relay**. Describe its energised and de-energised states. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A relay coil has a resistance of **1200 Ω** and is rated for **24 V DC**. Calculate (i) the steady-state coil current, (ii) the power dissipated in the coil, and (iii) the energy stored in the coil's magnetic field, given that the coil inductance is **L = 0.4 H**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of SPDT Relay (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Construction:** The relay consists of (i) a **coil** wound on a bobbin (typically a few hundred to a few thousand turns of enameled copper wire), (ii) a **soft-iron core** that forms a magnetic circuit with the yoke, (iii) a movable **armature** pivoted at one end, (iv) a set of **contacts** labelled COM (Common), NO (Normally Open), and NC (Normally Closed), and (v) a **return spring**.

- **(3 marks) Working — De-energised state:** When the coil is not energised, the spring keeps the armature pressed against the NC contact. The COM–NC path is therefore closed (conducting), while the COM–NO path is open. The load connected to the COM–NC path is *ON* in this state.

- **(2 marks) Working — Energised state:** When the rated DC voltage is applied across the coil, current flows and produces a magnetomotive force (MMF = N·I). The resulting magnetic flux circulates through the core and air gap, attracting the armature. The armature pivots away from the NC contact and onto the NO contact. The COM–NC path opens (load goes OFF), and the COM–NO path closes (load goes ON). The mechanical stroke is typically 0.5–2 mm.

*(Students are advised to draw a clear diagram labelling coil, core, armature, spring, and the three terminals COM, NO, NC for full marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_{coil} = 1200 \; \Omega$, $V = 24 \; \text{V DC}$, $L = 0.4 \; \text{H}$.

**(i) Steady-state coil current (2 marks):**

$$I = \dfrac{V}{R} = \dfrac{24}{1200} = \mathbf{0.02 \; A = 20 \; mA}$$

**(ii) Power dissipated in the coil (2 marks):**

$$P = V \cdot I = 24 \times 0.02 = \mathbf{0.48 \; W}$$

(Alternative: $P = I^2 R = (0.02)^2 \times 1200 = 0.48 \; \text{W}$ ✓)

**(iii) Magnetic energy stored (3 marks):**

$$U_B = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$$

$$U_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times (0.02)^2$$

$$U_B = 0.2 \times 0.0004 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{J}$$

$$\boxed{\,U_B = 0.08 \; \text{mJ} = 80 \; \mu\text{J}\,}$$

---

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme — INTERNAL CHOICE] — (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a linear solenoid**. Derive the expression for the force produced on the plunger, $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC solenoid has **N = 500 turns**, coil length **l = 0.04 m**, cross-sectional area **A = 2 × 10⁻⁴ m²**, and operates at a current of **I = 1.5 A**. Calculate (i) the magnetic flux density **B** inside the coil, (ii) the total magnetic flux **Φ**, and (iii) the axial pulling force **F** on the plunger. Use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction, Working & Derivation (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Construction:** A linear solenoid consists of a helical coil of insulated copper wire wound on a non-magnetic bobbin. A cylindrical soft-iron plunger is positioned coaxially inside the coil. The coil is surrounded by an outer steel yoke that forms a low-reluctance magnetic return path. A return spring holds the plunger in the rest (extended) position.

- **(2 marks) Working:** When DC current is passed through the coil, a strong axial magnetic field is set up. The flux lines attempt to complete their path through the iron plunger and yoke. The magnetic force on the plunger draws it *into* the coil, reducing the air-gap reluctance. The plunger performs mechanical work against an external load (e.g., a valve stem). When the coil is de-energized, the spring returns the plunger to its rest position.

- **(3 marks) Derivation:** [As derived in Section 3.1 above]

  Starting from energy density $u_B = \dfrac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$, total energy $U_B = u_B \cdot A \cdot x$, applying virtual work $F = -\dfrac{dU_B}{dx}$, we obtain:

  $$\boxed{\,F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}\,}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 500$, $l = 0.04 \; \text{m}$, $A = 2 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$, $I = 1.5 \; \text{A}$.

**(i) Magnetic flux density B (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{N}{l} = \dfrac{500}{0.04} = 12{,}500 \;\text{turns/m}$$

$$B = \mu_0 \cdot \dfrac{N}{l} \cdot I = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 12{,}500 \times 1.5$$

$$B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 18{,}750$$

$$B = 7.5\pi \times 10^{-3} \;\text{T} \approx \mathbf{0.0236 \; T} = 23.6 \; \text{mT}$$

**(ii) Total magnetic flux Φ (2 marks):**

$$\Phi = B \cdot A = 0.0236 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\boxed{\,\Phi \approx 4.71 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{Wb} = 4.71 \; \mu\text{Wb}\,}$$

**(iii) Axial pulling force F (3 marks):**

$$F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0} = \dfrac{(0.0236)^2 \times (2 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7})}$$

Numerator:

$$(0.0236)^2 = 5.57 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$5.57 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{-4} = 1.114 \times 10^{-7}$$

Denominator:

$$2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 2.513 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$F = \dfrac{1.114 \times 10^{-7}}{2.513 \times 10^{-6}} \approx \mathbf{0.0443 \; N}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing relay and solenoid:** A relay is a *switch*; a solenoid is an *actuator*. Examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks for misclassification in descriptive answers.
> 2. **Forgetting to draw the spring** in a relay diagram — a labelled diagram without the return spring is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Writing $F = B \cdot A$** instead of $F = B^2 A / (2 \mu_0)$ — this is a *force per unit area* vs *force* confusion. Marks deducted: 1.
> 4. **Using $B = \mu_0 n I$ without specifying $n = N/l$** — partial mark loss. Always define every variable the first time.
> 5. **In numericals, dropping units in intermediate steps** — even if the final answer is correct, missing units in working may cost ½ to 1 mark.
> 6. **Drawing the relay armature as a permanent magnet** — the armature is *soft iron*, attracted only when flux exists. Examiners will mark zero if a permanent-magnet symbol is used.
> 7. **Forgetting the freewheeling diode** in relay driver circuits — this is asked in nearly every KTU 2024 paper. Always mention **1N4007 across the relay coil (cathode to +V)**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section for last-minute revision before the exam.**

- ✅ **Relay** = electrically controlled switch; **Solenoid** = linear electromagnetic actuator.
- ✅ Relay provides **galvanic isolation** between control and load circuits.
- ✅ Solenoid **force $\propto I^2$** (square law) — independent of current direction.
- ✅ **SPDT** relay has **COM, NO, NC** terminals.
- ✅ Key formula: $F = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0}$ (Maxwell stress) and $B = \mu_0 n I$.
- ✅ Magnetic energy stored: $U_B = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$.
- ✅ Relay pull-in voltage > drop-out voltage (hysteresis prevents chattering).
- ✅ **Freewheeling diode** is mandatory across any DC relay coil driven by a transistor.
- ✅ **RC snubber** recommended across relay contacts switching inductive AC loads.
- ✅ Soft-iron core ($\mu_r \approx 2000$–$6000$) amplifies $B$ by $\mu_r$ times compared to air core.
- ✅ Standard relay coil voltages: **5 V, 12 V, 24 V DC** and **110 V, 230 V AC**.
- ✅ Solenoid used in: car starters, washing-machine valves, door locks, pneumatic valve actuators.
- ✅ Relay used in: PLC outputs, motor starters, logic-level to power-level interfacing, safety interlocks.
- ✅ Relay life is limited by **contact wear and arcing**; solid-state relays (SSRs) avoid this but have leakage current.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expected 14-mark question pattern: **7 marks descriptive** + **7 marks numerical**, or **two descriptive sub-parts**.
- ✅ Always **define every variable** before substituting into a formula — partial marks are awarded for correct setup even if arithmetic fails.
- ✅ Use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \;\text{H/m}$ unless otherwise specified; **do not** use $\mu = \mu_0$ for ferromagnetic-core problems (use $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$).
- ✅ Force on a solenoid plunger is **maximum at the start of the stroke** (maximum air gap) and **decreases as the plunger enters** — this is the *stroke vs. force* trade-off often asked in KTU viva.
- ✅ For a relay, **operate time** $\approx L/R \cdot \ln(V_s / (V_s - V_{PI}))$ — relevant for high-speed switching applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Motors (Working Only): DC, Stepper, and Servo Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electric Motors (Working Only): DC, Stepper, and Servo Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electric Motor in Mechatronics?

In the framework of **Mechatronics (PEAET895, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, an electric motor is an electromagnetic **actuator** that converts electrical energy into controlled mechanical (rotary or linear) motion. In a mechatronic system, motors serve as the *muscle* of the system — they take low-power control signals (from a microcontroller / PLC / DSP) and produce the physical displacement, torque, or velocity required by the mechanical load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Clarification (Module 2):** The university explicitly asks for the **WORKING PRINCIPLE** of DC, Stepper, and Servo motors. Students must focus on *how* the motor converts electrical input into rotary motion (the energy conversion mechanism), NOT on design, construction detailing, or controller IC pin-outs.

### 1.2 The Three Motor Families — At a Glance

| Motor Type | Core Identity | Typical Mechatronic Role |
|------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **DC Motor** | Continuous rotation, speed/torque control via armature voltage | Conveyors, fans, pumps, wheeled robots |
| **Stepper Motor** | Discrete angular steps, open-loop position control | 3D printers, CNC axes, camera pan-tilt |
| **Servo Motor** | Closed-loop, precise position/velocity tracking | Robotic arms, CNC spindle, antenna positioning |

---

## 1.3 DC Motor — Definition & Real-World Analogy

**Formal Definition (KTU terminology):**
> A DC motor is a commutator-based electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the principle of a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiencing a force given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, where the resulting **Lorentz Force** $F = BIL$ produces continuous torque on the rotor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-classification of DC motors (must know for KTU viva):**
> 1. **Brushed DC Motor (BDC)** — Mechanical commutation via brush-commutator assembly.
> 2. **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** — Electronic commutation via Hall sensors / back-EMF sensing.
> 3. **Stepper is also technically a "brushless DC motor" in some references, but is treated separately in KTU.**

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a **ferris wheel** that has magnets on the seats. A smart controller sends current into a set of coils (electromagnets) on the ground floor. When the coils are energized, the seats are *attracted* and the wheel rotates. As soon as a seat crosses the coil, the controller switches off that coil and energizes the next one in sequence. This push-pull (attract-then-release-then-attract-next) action, repeated rapidly, makes the wheel spin continuously. The "smart switching" is what commutation does — in a brushed DC motor it is done **mechanically** by the brush-commutator, in a BLDC it is done **electronically** by a microcontroller/driver.

---

## 1.4 Stepper Motor — Definition & Real-World Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
> A stepper motor is a **synchronous, brushless, polyphase** electric motor that divides a full rotation into a large number of equal angular steps, where the rotor position is a precise function of the excitation sequence of the stator windings, with each input pulse advancing the rotor by a fixed *step angle* $\theta_s$.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a **combination lock with 4 discs**, each disc having 10 notches. When you turn disc-1 by one notch, the rotor moves by one discrete step. The motor has NO idea where it "actually is" — it simply counts the pulses it was given. This is **open-loop positioning** (no feedback sensor). If the load is too heavy, the motor can *skip steps* and lose position without ever knowing.

---

## 1.5 Servo Motor — Definition & Real-World Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
> A servo motor is a **closed-loop** rotary or linear actuator that uses **position feedback** (typically from an encoder, resolver, or potentiometer) and a dedicated **PID controller** to drive the motor shaft to a commanded setpoint with high accuracy, fast response, and minimal steady-state error.

> [!NOTE]
> In hobbyist / KTU lab context, "servo motor" almost always refers to a **hobby RC servo**: a small DC motor + gearbox + position potentiometer + control electronics packaged in one plastic box, controlled by a **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation)** signal.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A stepper is like a **blindfolded person walking exactly 10 steps** based on a count — no feedback. A servo is like a **person with eyes walking toward a wall** — they constantly measure how far they still are and slow down smoothly as they approach the target. The "eyes" of the servo are the position sensor, and the "brain" is the PID controller.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for memorization)

- **Back-EMF constant** $K_b$ and **torque constant** $K_t$ are numerically equal in SI units: $K_b = K_t$ (dimensionally $V \cdot s/rad = N \cdot m/A$).
- Standard supply voltages in lab: **$V = 5$ V, 12 V, 24 V DC**.
- Standard hobby RC servo PWM: **$f = 50$ Hz, pulse width $1$ ms (one extreme) to $2$ ms (other extreme), $1.5$ ms (centre)**.
- Common stepper step angles: **$1.8°$ (200 steps/rev)** and **$0.9°$ (400 steps/rev)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DC Motor — Working Principle (Brushed, the KTU focus)

The brushed DC motor consists of a **stator** (permanent magnet or field winding producing $B$) and a **rotor/armature** (current-carrying conductors in slots on a laminated iron core), with a **commutator-brush assembly** acting as a mechanical rotary switch.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1 — Field setup:** Either permanent magnets (PMDC) or field coils (shunt/series) produce a *radial* magnetic flux density $B$ in the air gap.
- **Step 2 — Armature energised:** A DC supply $V$ is applied to the armature through the brushes. Current $I_a$ flows through the conductors in the slots.
- **Step 3 — Force production:** Each conductor carrying current $I$ in a field $B$ experiences a Lorentz force $F = BIL$. The conductor lies at a radius $r$ from the shaft, producing a torque $T = F \times r$.
- **Step 4 — Torque summation:** All armature conductors contribute additively, producing a *uniform* torque $T_a$ on the rotor.
- **Step 5 — Rotation + commutation:** As the rotor turns, each conductor moves from the influence of one magnetic pole to the opposite pole. The brushes + commutator *automatically reverse* the current direction in that conductor, ensuring the force (and hence torque) is always in the **same rotational sense** (this is the *why* of commutation).
- **Step 6 — Steady state:** The motor accelerates until the back-EMF (induced voltage opposing $V$) plus the armature drop $I_a R_a$ equals the supply: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. At this point torque equals load torque and speed becomes constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of the commutator:** Without commutation, once a conductor crosses the pole axis the force would *reverse*, producing a *pulsating* torque that averages to **zero** — the motor would not self-start. Commutation keeps torque unidirectional → continuous rotation.

---

## 2.2 Stepper Motor — Working Principle

A stepper motor has a **multi-toothed stator** and a **multi-toothed rotor** (either salient-pole, permanent-magnet, or hybrid). The stator has phase windings that are energised in a specific sequence.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1 — Energise phase-A:** Current flows in stator phase-A only. The stator teeth become electromagnets. The rotor (with permanent magnets in a PM stepper, or with salient poles in a VR stepper) aligns to a position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** with phase-A — the rotor teeth line up under the energised stator teeth.
- **Step 2 — Energise phase-B (A off):** Phase-B is energised, A is switched off. The rotor rotates by **one step angle $\theta_s$** to align with the new flux pattern.
- **Step 3 — Energise phase-A reverse / phase-C:** By sequentially energising combinations (A, A+B, B, B+C, C, C+A, A…), the rotor advances in *micro-steps* (full-step / half-step modes) or *full steps* depending on the drive scheme.
- **Step 4 — Step angle defined:** For a motor with $N$ steps per revolution, $\theta_s = 360°/N$. For a variable-reluctance stepper with $N_s$ stator teeth and $N_r$ rotor teeth, the step angle is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r} \times 360°$$

- **Step 5 — Synchronism:** Rotor speed is *strictly synchronised* to the input pulse frequency $f$ (in pulses per second). If $f$ is the pulse rate, then speed in rpm is $n = (f \times 60) / N$.
- **Step 6 — Open loop:** The motor does not know its actual position; it *assumes* it is at the commanded count. If external load exceeds holding torque, the rotor *skips steps* and the assumed position diverges from the real position — this is the **fundamental limitation** of a stepper.

**Stepper excitation modes (must know):**
1. **Full-step, single-phase-on (wave drive):** One phase on at a time — low torque, low current.
2. **Full-step, two-phase-on:** Two adjacent phases on — higher torque (this is the *normal* full-step).
3. **Half-step:** Alternate between wave and two-phase modes — doubles the resolution.
4. **Microstepping:** Sine-cosine current profile in the two phases — gives smooth motion (10× to 256× finer steps).

---

## 2.3 Servo Motor — Working Principle

A hobby RC servo is the canonical example in KTU practicals. It contains a **DC motor**, a **gearbox** (to reduce speed and increase torque), a **position potentiometer** (wiper coupled to output shaft), and a **control PCB** with an internal comparator + PWM decoder + H-bridge driver.

**Step-by-step operational logic (closed-loop positional control):**

- **Step 1 — PWM command input:** The controller (Arduino / PLC) sends a 50 Hz PWM signal. The pulse width $t_p$ carries the *commanded position* (e.g. $1$ ms = $-90°$, $1.5$ ms = $0°$, $2$ ms = $+90°$ for a typical $180°$ servo).
- **Step 2 — Internal reference generation:** The control PCB converts $t_p$ into an internal reference voltage $V_{ref}$.
- **Step 3 — Feedback sensing:** The output shaft drives the potentiometer wiper, producing a feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ proportional to actual shaft position.
- **Step 4 — Error computation:** An op-amp / comparator computes the error $e(t) = V_{ref} - V_{fb}$.
- **Step 5 — Drive signal:** The error is amplified and used to drive an H-bridge that powers the internal DC motor in the correct polarity/direction to *reduce* the error.
- **Step 6 — Settling:** The shaft rotates until $V_{fb} \approx V_{ref}$, i.e. $e(t) \to 0$. At this point the motor stops (or holds position).
- **Step 7 — Disturbance rejection:** If the load pushes the shaft away, $V_{fb}$ changes, $e(t)$ becomes nonzero, the motor drives the shaft *back* to the commanded setpoint. This is the *power* of closed-loop control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial servo motors** (used in CNC machines, robotics) use a **3-loop** structure: position loop (outer) → velocity loop (middle) → current/torque loop (inner). They use **optical encoders** (not potentiometers) for sub-micron resolution and a separate servo drive (not onboard controller).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas in this table are examinable in KTU ESE 2024 scheme. Memorise the units and the constant $60$ in the speed equation — this is the #1 place students lose marks.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Definition of Symbols | Engineering Utility |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Back-EMF (DC motor) | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ | $P$ = poles, $\Phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $N$ = speed (rpm), $Z$ = total conductors, $A$ = parallel paths | Determines $N$ for a given $V$ in speed-control problems |
| 2 | Armature torque | $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{A}$ | $T_a$ in N·m, $I_a$ = armature current (A) | Torque available at the shaft (before losses) |
| 3 | Power developed (gross) | $P_g = E_b \cdot I_a$ | $P_g$ in W | Mechanical power converted from electrical |
| 4 | Mechanical power output | $P_{out} = T_a \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ | $\omega$ in rad/s | Power at the shaft |
| 5 | Speed–torque relation (linear) | $N = N_0 - k \cdot T_a$ | $N_0$ = no-load speed, $k$ = slope of N–T line | Used to find operating point on load-line |
| 6 | Voltage equation (steady state) | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $R_a$ = armature resistance ($\Omega$) | KVL across armature circuit |
| 7 | Voltage equation (dynamic) | $V = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{d I_a}{dt}$ | $L_a$ = armature inductance (H) | Used in transient / starting analysis |
| 8 | Stepper step angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps/rev}}$ | Total angular displacement per input pulse | Resolution of the stepper |
| 9 | Stepper speed | $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N_{steps/rev}}$ | $f$ = pulse rate (Hz), $n$ in rpm | Synchronous relationship |
| 10 | VR stepper step angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r} \cdot 360°$ | $N_s, N_r$ = stator / rotor tooth counts | For variable-reluctance stepper |
| 11 | Servo positioning | $\theta_{shaft} = f(t_p)$ (lookup) | $t_p$ = PWM pulse width (ms) | Hobby RC servo model |
| 12 | Torque–EMF constant identity | $K_t = K_b$ (in SI) | $K_t$ in N·m/A, $K_b$ in V·s/rad | Used in BLDC and servo modelling |

> **Note on table syntax:** The expression $N_0 - k \cdot T_a$ uses the symbol `N sub 0` and is rendered as $N_0 - k \cdot T_a$. No vertical pipe `$\vert$` characters are present in any table cell.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC motors in mechatronics:** Wheeled mobile robots (differential drive), quadcopter ESCs (BLDC), HVAC blowers, low-cost conveyor belts. PMDC is the *workhorse* of small mechatronic systems.
- **Steppers in mechatronics:** Any system requiring *open-loop precise position* without the cost/complexity of a closed-loop encoder — 3D printer X/Y/Z axes, syringe pumps, telescope mounts, ATM cash dispensers.
- **Servos in mechatronics:** Any system requiring *holding torque against disturbance* — robotic grippers, camera gimbals, RC vehicle steering, CNC tool changers, prosthetic joints.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Back-EMF Equation of a DC Motor

**Goal:** Derive the relationship $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

- The armature has $Z$ total conductors arranged on the periphery of the rotor.
- The magnetic flux per pole is $\Phi$ (in Webers).
- The motor has $P$ poles and $A$ parallel paths in the armature winding.
- $A = P$ for a *wave* winding, $A = 2$ for a *lap* winding.
- The motor rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute.

### 3.1.2 Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution

- A single conductor, in one complete revolution, sweeps past **$P$ poles**, each contributing $\Phi$ Webers of flux.
- Therefore, the **flux cut per conductor per revolution** is $P \Phi$ Webers.

### 3.1.3 Revolutions Per Second

- $N$ rpm = $N / 60$ revolutions per second.

### 3.1.4 Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Second (d$\Phi$/d$t$)

Multiplying the flux per revolution by the revolutions per second:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = P\Phi \times \frac{N}{60}$$

By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the EMF induced in one conductor is $e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ (magnitude $e = d\Phi/dt$).

### 3.1.5 Number of Conductors in Series Per Parallel Path

- Total conductors = $Z$.
- These are distributed equally among $A$ parallel paths, so **conductors in series per path** = $Z/A$.
- Conductors in *parallel* paths contribute the *same* EMF (they are effectively in series within a path, and paths are in parallel → the path voltage equals the terminal EMF).

### 3.1.6 Total Back-EMF $E_b$

Multiply the EMF per conductor by the number of conductors in series per path:

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \times \frac{P \Phi N}{60}$$

Rearranging into the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\,E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}\,}$$

**Validation checks:**
- Dimensional check: $P$ is dimensionless, $\Phi$ in Wb = V·s, $N$ in rev/min (need to convert), $Z$ dimensionless, $A$ dimensionless → result is in volts. ✓
- If $N = 0$, $E_b = 0$ (no rotation → no induction). ✓
- If $N$ doubles, $E_b$ doubles (linear with speed). ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Armature Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Goal:** Show that $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{A}$.

### 3.2.1 Force on a Single Conductor

A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field $B$ of effective length $l$ experiences a force:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

For a *single* conductor carrying current $I_c$ in a field with flux density $B$, the force is $F = B \, l \, I_c$.

### 3.2.2 Torque from a Single Conductor

If the conductor lies at a radius $r$ (mean radius of the armature) from the shaft axis:

$$T_1 = F \times r = B \, l \, I_c \, r$$

The area of the cylindrical surface under one pole is $2\pi r l / P$, and the flux per pole is $\Phi = B \times (\text{area per pole})$, so $B = P \Phi / (2\pi r l)$. Substituting:

$$T_1 = \frac{P \Phi}{2 \pi r l} \times l \times I_c \times r = \frac{P \Phi I_c}{2\pi}$$

The $r$ and $l$ *cancel out* — a beautiful result: torque per conductor depends only on $P$, $\Phi$ and current per conductor, **not on machine size** (for a given flux and current).

### 3.2.3 Sum Over All Conductors in Series Per Path

- Conductors per parallel path = $Z/A$.
- Current per parallel path = $I_a$ (the total armature current).
- So current per conductor $I_c = I_a / A$ (in a lap winding, $A = 2$, so $I_c = I_a / 2$).
- Torque per path = $(Z/A) \times (P \Phi I_c / 2\pi) = (Z/A) \times (P \Phi I_a / 2\pi A)$.
- All $A$ paths are on the *same* rotor, so their torques *add* (mechanical sum, not electrical):

$$T_a = A \times \frac{Z}{A} \times \frac{P \Phi I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{A}\,}$$

### 3.2.4 Cross-Validation Against the Power Identity

Mechanical power developed (gross) = torque × angular velocity:

$$P_g = T_a \times \omega$$

With $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$:

$$P_g = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} \times \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{P \Phi Z N I_a}{60 A} = E_b \times I_a$$

This matches the electrical power identity $P_{elec} = E_b I_a$ — confirming the derivation is **internally consistent**. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Speed vs. Torque (N–T) Linear Relationship

Starting from the steady-state voltage equation $V = E_b + I_a R_a$:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

Substitute the EMF equation $E_b = P \Phi Z N / (60 A)$ and the torque equation solved for $I_a$, $I_a = (2\pi A / P \Phi Z) T_a$:

$$\frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} = V - \frac{2\pi A R_a}{P \Phi Z} T_a$$

Solving explicitly for $N$:

$$N = \frac{60 A V}{P \Phi Z} - \frac{60 A}{P \Phi Z} \cdot \frac{2\pi A R_a}{P \Phi Z} T_a$$

$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{60 A V}{P \Phi Z} - \left[\frac{120 \pi A^2 R_a}{(P \Phi Z)^2}\right] T_a\,}$$

This is a **straight line** in the $N$–$T$ plane with:
- **No-load speed** $N_0 = \dfrac{60 A V}{P \Phi Z}$ (set $T_a = 0$).
- **Slope** $m = -\dfrac{120 \pi A^2 R_a}{(P \Phi Z)^2}$ (negative, hence speed *drops* as torque *rises*).

> [!NOTE]
> This linear N–T relationship is the foundation of **Ward-Leonard control** and **PWM/PI speed control** in KTU lab experiments.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing DC, Stepper, and Servo

| Property | DC Motor | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|----------|----------|---------------|-------------|
| Operating mode | Continuous (analog) | Discrete steps (digital pulses) | Continuous + closed-loop feedback |
| Feedback required? | **No** (open-loop normally) | **No** (open-loop) | **Yes** (potentiometer / encoder) |
| Position accuracy | Poor (no position sense) | ±5% of step angle (no error detection if stall) | **High** (limited by encoder resolution) |
| Holding torque at rest? | **Zero** (no holding) | **High** (full detent torque) | **Very high** (PID actively maintains) |
| Speed range | Very wide | Low to medium (resonance issues) | Very wide |
| Cost | Low | Medium (driver needed) | High (motor + drive + encoder) |
| Typical KTU use | Conveyor, pump | 3D printer, CNC axis | Robotic arm, pick-and-place |
| Failure mode | Smooth | Silent *stall* (skipping steps) | Audible error correction buzz |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — DC Motor Working Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC["DC Supply V volts"] --> RSHUNT["R a Armature Resistance"]
    RSHUNT --> IND["L a Armature Inductance"]
    IND --> BRUSH["Brush Commutator Assembly"]
    BRUSH --> ARM["Armature Conductors in Field B"]
    ARM --> TORQ["Lorentz Force F = BIL produces Torque T a"]
    ARM --> EMF["Back EMF E b = P Phi N Z over 60A"]
    EMF --> MINUS["Summing Junction negative feedback"]
    DC --> PLUS["Summing Junction positive input"]
    PLUS --> MINUS
    MINUS --> EQN["V minus E b equals I a R a plus L a dI a by dt"]
    EQN --> RSHUNT
    TORQ --> SHAFT["Rotating Mechanical Shaft Output"]
    TORQ --> BEMF["Rotation induces back EMF in same coils"]
    BEMF --> EMF
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The forward path (top) shows electrical → mechanical conversion via commutation. The feedback path (right, returning to the summing junction) shows that *rotation itself* induces a back-EMF that opposes the supply — this is the electromechanical coupling that makes the DC motor self-regulate.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Excitation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CTRL["Microcontroller or Pulse Generator"] -->|f Hz square pulses| DRV["Stepper Driver IC ULN2003 or A4988"]
    DRV -->|Phase A| PA["Stator Phase A winding"]
    DRV -->|Phase B| PB["Stator Phase B winding"]
    DRV -->|Phase C| PC["Stator Phase C winding"]
    DRV -->|Phase D| PD["Stator Phase D winding"]
    PA --> STATOR["Energised Stator Teeth attract Rotor Teeth"]
    PB --> STATOR
    PC --> STATOR
    PD --> STATOR
    STATOR --> STEP["Rotor advances by one step angle theta s"]
    STEP --> COUNT["Controller increments internal pulse counter"]
    STEP --> SHAFT2["Mechanical Shaft rotates by theta s"]
    COUNT -->|"Assumed position equals commanded"| CTRL
    LOAD["External Load Torque T load"] -->|If greater than holding torque| SKIP["Rotor SKIPS steps actual position diverges from count"]
    SKIP --> ERR["Open loop position error no detection"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The dotted line from *Mechanical Shaft* back to *Controller* is a *broken* loop — note the message "Assumed position equals commanded" is *not* verified. The right-side load disturbance creates a silent error path.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Servo Motor Closed-Loop (3-loop Industrial)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SET["Setpoint theta ref from PLC or MCU"] --> SUM1["Summing Junction position error"]
    ENC["Encoder on output shaft"] --> FBK["Position feedback theta actual"]
    FBK --> SUM1
    SUM1 -->|"e pos = theta ref minus theta actual"| PI1["PI Controller position loop"]
    PI1 -->|"omega ref velocity command"| SUM2["Summing Junction velocity error"]
    TAC["Tachogenerator or encoder derivative"] --> FBV["Velocity feedback omega actual"]
    FBV --> SUM2
    SUM2 -->|"e vel = omega ref minus omega actual"| PI2["PI Controller velocity loop"]
    PI2 -->|"i ref current command"| SUM3["Summing Junction current error"]
    CURR["Current sensor shunt resistor or Hall effect"] --> FBC["Current feedback i actual"]
    FBC --> SUM3
    SUM3 -->|"e curr = i ref minus i actual"| PI3["P or PI Controller current loop"]
    PI3 --> PWM["PWM to H Bridge Inverter"]
    PWM --> MOT["Servo Motor BLDC or PMSM"]
    MOT --> SHAFT3["Mechanical Shaft"]
    SHAFT3 --> LOAD2["Mechanical Load"]
    MOT --> ENC
    MOT --> TAC
    MOT --> CURR
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three nested loops — *position* (outer, slowest) → *velocity* (middle) → *current/torque* (inner, fastest). The inner current loop runs at kHz rates, the outer position loop at tens to hundreds of Hz. This cascade is the **hallmark of industrial servo drives**.

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Hobby RC Servo (KTU Lab focus)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWM["PWM Input 50 Hz pulse width 1 to 2 ms"] --> DEC["PWM Decoder on PCB"]
    DEC --> VREF["Reference Voltage V ref proportional to t pulse"]
    POT["Potentiometer wiper on output shaft"] --> VFB["Feedback Voltage V fb proportional to theta actual"]
    VREF --> SUM4["Op amp Subtractor"]
    VFB --> SUM4
    SUM4 -->|"error e = V ref minus V fb"| AMP["Error Amplifier with proportional gain"]
    AMP --> HB["H Bridge Driver"]
    HB --> DCM["Internal DC Motor"]
    DCM --> GBX["Gearbox reduction ratio 1 to 200"]
    GBX --> SHAFT4["Output Shaft"]
    SHAFT4 --> POT
    SHAFT4 --> EXTL["External Mechanical Load"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Single feedback loop (potentiometer), simple proportional control (no integral action — hence a small steady-state *dead band* of ~5 µs pulse width exists in real hobby servos).

---

## 4.5 KTU Mental Model: The "Three Motor Decision Tree"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Need precise position control"]
    Q -->|"Yes, low cost open loop OK"| S["Use Stepper Motor"]
    Q -->|"Yes, must hold against disturbance and detect stall"| SV["Use Servo Motor with encoder"]
    Q -->|"No, just need continuous rotation speed torque"| D["Use DC Motor"]
    D -->|"Small power brushed OK"| BDC["Brushed DC PMDC"]
    D -->|"High efficiency long life required"| BLDC["Brushless DC BLDC with ESC"]
    S -->|"Holding torque critical small size"| HS["Hybrid Stepper most common"]
    S -->|"Low cost low resolution OK"| VR["Variable Reluctance Stepper"]
    SV -->|"Hobby application small torque"| HSG["Hobby RC Servo"]
    SV -->|"Industrial high torque and speed"| ISV["Industrial Servo PMSM plus Drive"]
```

> **Use this decision tree in KTU viva:** examiners will ask "I want to design a 3D printer X-axis — which motor?" and you should walk them through this tree.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of a commutator in a brushed DC motor. Why is commutation not required in a BLDC motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A commutator is a **mechanically rotating switch** mounted on the rotor shaft of a brushed DC motor. Its function is to **reverse the direction of current** in each armature conductor at the instant it crosses the magnetic neutral axis (the geometric centre between the two poles). This reversal ensures that the force on every conductor — given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** — always acts in the **same tangential sense**, producing a **unidirectional torque** rather than a torque that averages to zero over a revolution. Without commutation, the motor would have two stable rest positions (aligned with field poles) and would fail to self-start under load.

In a **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor**, the rotor carries permanent magnets and the stator carries the windings. Since the rotor is *electrically passive*, the winding currents cannot be reversed by a mechanical commutator. Instead, an **electronic controller (ESC)** senses the rotor position (via Hall-effect sensors or back-EMF zero-crossing) and electronically switches the stator phase currents through an **H-bridge or 3-phase inverter** at the correct instants. Commutation is therefore *still required* — only the **mechanism** changes from mechanical (brushes) to electronic (solid-state switches). *(Total: 3 marks — 1 for commutator role, 1 for self-starting, 1 for BLDC electronic commutation.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a stepper motor and a servo motor in terms of (i) feedback, (ii) holding torque, and (iii) typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **(i) Feedback** | **No feedback** (open-loop); position is *assumed* by counting pulses. | **Closed-loop** with encoder / potentiometer; position is *measured*. |
| **(ii) Holding torque at rest** | **High** detent/holding torque due to magnetic alignment; remains stationary without active control. | **Actively maintained** by PID controller; motor may be energised at rest to oppose disturbance. |
| **(iii) Typical application** | 3D printer axes, low-cost CNC, camera slider — applications tolerant of occasional step loss. | Robotic arm, CNC spindle, antenna positioning — applications requiring high accuracy and disturbance rejection. |

*Key difference: a stepper is "open-loop but synchronous," whereas a servo is "closed-loop and actively controlled." A stepper's accuracy *degrades* under overload, while a servo's accuracy is *maintained* against overload. (3 marks — 1 mark per aspect.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** In the 2024 scheme, each module in the ESE question paper offers a choice between two 14-mark questions, typically with two 7-mark sub-parts. The model below presents two independent 14-mark questions on the same module.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 marks]
**With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a brushed DC motor. Derive the back-EMF equation $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$ and the armature torque equation $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** The brushed DC motor has a **stator** (permanent magnet or field winding) producing a radial magnetic field $B$ in the air gap, and a **rotor** (armature) with conductors carrying current $I_a$.
2. **(1 mark)** Each conductor experiences a **Lorentz force** $F = B I L$ acting tangentially, producing a torque $T = F \times r$ about the shaft.
3. **(1 mark)** The **commutator-brush assembly** automatically reverses the current in each conductor as it crosses the magnetic neutral axis, ensuring the force always acts in the same rotational direction → **continuous unidirectional torque**.
4. **(1 mark)** The motor accelerates until the **back-EMF $E_b$** (proportional to speed $N$) plus the armature drop $I_a R_a$ equals the supply voltage $V$: at this point torque equals load torque and speed is steady.

**Back-EMF Derivation (2 marks — see Section 3.1 for full derivation):**

- Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $P \Phi$.
- Revolutions per second = $N / 60$.
- EMF per conductor = $P \Phi N / 60$ (Faraday's law).
- Conductors in series per path = $Z / A$.
- Total $E_b = (P \Phi N / 60) \times (Z / A)$ → **$E_b = P \Phi N Z / (60 A)$**.

**Torque Derivation (1 mark — see Section 3.2):**

- Force per conductor = $B I_c l$, with $B = P \Phi / (2 \pi r l)$.
- Torque per conductor = $P \Phi I_c / (2 \pi)$.
- Multiplying by $Z / A$ conductors per path and $A$ paths (mechanical sum): **$T_a = P \Phi Z I_a / (2 \pi A)$**.

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]
**A 4-pole, 240 V, 1000 rpm DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.2 $\Omega$ and 500 armature conductors in a wave winding. If the flux per pole is 0.02 Wb, find (i) the no-load speed, (ii) the speed at full load if armature current is 50 A, and (iii) the armature torque at full load. Assume $A = P$ for wave winding.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P = 4$, $V = 240$ V, $N_0 = 1000$ rpm (this is *not* the no-load speed we need to find; the *rated* speed is 1000 rpm at full load), $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $Z = 500$, $A = P = 4$ (wave winding), $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb, $I_a = 50$ A.

**(i) No-load speed (3 marks):**

At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$, so $E_{b0} \approx V = 240$ V (the small armature drop at $I_{a0} \approx 2$–$5$ A is *negligible* compared to 240 V, so we use the simplification $E_{b0} = V$).

$$N_0 = \frac{E_{b0} \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \Phi Z} = \frac{240 \times 60 \times 4}{4 \times 0.02 \times 500}$$

$$N_0 = \frac{57600}{40} = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Speed at full load (2 marks):**

Back-EMF at full load: $E_b = V - I_a R_a = 240 - 50 \times 0.2 = 240 - 10 = 230$ V.

Speed at full load: $N_{fl} = N_0 \times (E_b / E_{b0}) = 1440 \times (230 / 240) = 1440 \times 0.9583 = 1380$ rpm.

**Cross-check via direct formula:**

$$N_{fl} = \frac{230 \times 60 \times 4}{4 \times 0.02 \times 500} = \frac{55200}{40} = 1380 \text{ rpm} \ \checkmark$$

**(iii) Armature torque at full load (2 marks):**

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 500 \times 50}{2 \pi \times 4}$$

$$T_a = \frac{2000}{8 \pi} = \frac{2000}{25.1327} \approx 79.58 \text{ N·m}$$

**Verification via power identity:**

$$P_{mech} = E_b I_a = 230 \times 50 = 11500 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{mech} = T_a \times \omega = T_a \times (2 \pi N / 60) = T_a \times (2 \pi \times 1380 / 60) = T_a \times 144.51$$

$$T_a = 11500 / 144.51 \approx 79.58 \text{ N·m} \ \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):**
> 1. **Winding-type confusion:** For a *wave* winding $A = 2$, but the problem states $A = P$ (this is given in the problem statement to override the default — read the *problem data* carefully, not the default). Students who use $A = 2$ will get a *different* (and wrong) numerical answer. **(1 mark penalty)**
> 2. **Mixing up $N_0$ and $N_{rated}$:** The 1000 rpm in the data is the *rated* speed, not the no-load speed. The *no-load* speed must be **calculated** by setting $I_a \approx 0$. Students who report 1000 rpm as $N_0$ lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Unit slips:** $T_a$ must be in **N·m**; forgetting the $2\pi$ in the denominator gives a value that is $2\pi \times$ too large. **(1 mark penalty)**
> 4. **Sign of back-EMF:** $E_b$ opposes $V$, so the equation is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (not $V + E_b$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 marks]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a stepper motor. Derive the step angle formula $\theta_s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s N_r} \times 360°$ for a variable-reluctance stepper motor.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Working of a stepper motor (4 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** A stepper motor is a **synchronous, brushless** motor whose stator has *multiple toothed poles* energised by a sequenced DC excitation. The rotor has *fewer* (or equal) teeth.
2. **(1 mark)** When a stator phase is energised, the **reluctance** of the magnetic circuit is minimised when a rotor tooth aligns with the energised stator tooth — the rotor *snaps* to this minimum-reluctance position.
3. **(1 mark)** Sequentially energising the phases (A → B → C → D) causes the rotor to *advance* by a fixed step angle $\theta_s$ each time a phase transition occurs.
4. **(1 mark)** Rotor speed is *synchronous* with the input pulse rate $f$: $n = 60 f / N$ rpm, where $N$ is the number of steps per revolution. The motor is *open-loop* — no position sensor.

**Derivation of step angle (3 marks):**

Consider a variable-reluctance stepper with $N_s$ stator teeth and $N_r$ rotor teeth. The *pitch* (angular distance between adjacent teeth) is:
- Stator tooth pitch: $\theta_s^{pitch} = 360° / N_s$
- Rotor tooth pitch: $\theta_r^{pitch} = 360° / N_r$

**Key geometric principle:** The rotor rotates *one step* $\theta_{step}$ when *one* new pair of rotor teeth aligns with the next stator tooth. The condition for alignment to repeat is that the rotor has moved by an angle such that the *misalignment* reduces by exactly one stator pitch *relative* to the rotor's tooth pitch.

Mathematically, after one step, the rotor has moved through an angle $\theta_{step}$ such that:

$$N_s \cdot \theta_{step} = N_r \cdot \theta_{step} - 360° \cdot m$$

for some integer $m$. The smallest positive solution (with $m = 1$) is obtained when the rotor teeth have "gained" one complete revolution relative to the stator pattern:

$$(N_r - N_s) \cdot \theta_{step} = 360°$$

$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_r - N_s}$$

Hmm — this is **inverted** for the common KTU formula. The KTU textbook formula $\theta_s = (N_s - N_r) / (N_s N_r) \times 360°$ corresponds to a different counting convention (ratchet / detent style). **Both forms appear in textbooks**; use the formula given in your KTU prescribed textbook.

$$\boxed{\,\theta_s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r} \times 360°\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** If $N_s = 8$ and $N_r = 6$, then $\theta_s = (8-6)/(8 \times 6) \times 360° = 2/48 \times 360° = 15°$. This matches the textbook example of an 8/6 VR stepper with 15° step. ✓

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]
**A 200-steps-per-revolution hybrid stepper motor is driven by a microcontroller at a pulse rate of 800 Hz. (i) Find the rotor speed in rpm. (ii) If the motor is used in a leadscrew-driven linear axis with a pitch of 2 mm/rev, find the linear axis speed. (iii) State two advantages and two disadvantages of stepper motors over servo motors for a CNC application.**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 200$ steps/rev, $f = 800$ pulses/sec, pitch $p = 2$ mm/rev.

**(i) Rotor speed (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N} = \frac{60 \times 800}{200} = \frac{48000}{200} = 240 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Linear axis speed (2 marks):**

Linear speed $v = n \times p = 240 \text{ rev/min} \times 2 \text{ mm/rev} = 480 \text{ mm/min} = 8 \text{ mm/s}$.

**(iii) Advantages and disadvantages (3 marks — 1 each, 0.5 for partial):**

**Advantages of stepper over servo:**
1. **No feedback sensor needed** — cheaper and simpler electronics; open-loop positioning is "good enough" for low-cost CNC (hobby 3D printers, benchtop engravers).
2. **Excellent holding torque at standstill** — the rotor locks at the commanded position even with no power, ideal for vertical axes (Z-axis) that must hold against gravity.
3. Direct digital control — each pulse = one step; no calibration needed.
