**Disadvantages of stepper over servo:**
1. **Step loss / stall is silent and uncorrected** — if the load exceeds the motor's pull-out torque, the rotor skips steps with no error detection; the part is ruined.
2. **Resonance at low speeds** — steppers exhibit torsional vibration and resonance in the 50–300 rpm range, requiring microstepping or damping.
3. **Lower efficiency and heat dissipation** — current is drawn continuously even when stationary, causing heating.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Pulse-rate units:** Some students use the formula $n = f / N$ (treating $f$ as pulses per *minute*). Always check: if $f$ is in **Hz** (pulses per **second**), multiply by 60 to get rpm. **(1 mark penalty)**
> 2. **Linear unit consistency:** $480$ mm/min must be **converted** to mm/s if the question asks for "speed in mm/s" — show the conversion step, do not just write 480.
> 3. **Confusing step angle and step number:** A 200-step motor has $\theta_s = 360/200 = 1.8°$ per step. Students who interchange $\theta_s$ and $N$ in the formula lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Servo question trap:** "State two advantages" means **two** *distinct* advantages — not two rewordings of the same point (e.g. "no feedback needed" and "no encoder needed" are the *same* point). **(1 mark penalty)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Electric Motors (Working Only):**

### DC Motor
- ☐ Lorentz force $F = BIL$ → tangential force → torque $T = F \times r$.
- ☐ Commutator reverses current to keep torque *unidirectional*.
- ☐ Back-EMF: $E_b = P \Phi N Z / (60 A)$ — increases with speed.
- ☐ Torque: $T_a = P \Phi Z I_a / (2 \pi A)$ — proportional to armature current.
- ☐ Voltage eqn: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (steady state) → speed drops as load increases.
- ☐ BLDC replaces mechanical commutation with electronic switching (ESC + Hall sensors).

### Stepper Motor
- ☐ Synchronous, brushless, open-loop.
- ☐ Each input pulse = one step angle $\theta_s = 360° / N$.
- ☐ Speed $n = 60 f / N$ — *synchronous* with pulse rate.
- ☐ VR stepper formula: $\theta_s = (N_s - N_r)/(N_s N_r) \times 360°$.
- ☐ Drive modes: wave, full-step (two-phase), half-step, microstepping.
- ☐ Failure mode: *silent step loss* under overload — no detection in open loop.
- ☐ Holding torque at rest is **non-zero** (detent + electromagnetic).

### Servo Motor
- ☐ Closed-loop with position feedback (potentiometer / encoder / resolver).
- ☐ Hobby RC servo: 50 Hz PWM, $1$ ms to $2$ ms pulse width = full range.
- ☐ Industrial servo: 3-loop cascade (position → velocity → current).
- ☐ PID controller drives error $e(t) = \theta_{ref} - \theta_{actual} \to 0$.
- ☐ Active disturbance rejection — maintains position against external load.
- ☐ Higher cost (encoder + drive) but higher accuracy and speed range.

### Cross-Motor Comparison
- ☐ **DC motor** = continuous rotation, no inherent position sense.
- ☐ **Stepper** = discrete digital positioning, open-loop, holding torque, can stall silently.
- ☐ **Servo** = closed-loop, high accuracy, active holding, expensive.
- ☐ Choice rule: stepper for *cheap open-loop precise discrete moves*; servo for *closed-loop high-precision dynamic positioning*; DC for *simple continuous rotation*.

### Formula Hot-Spots (memorise verbatim)
- ☐ $E_b = P \Phi N Z / (60 A)$
- ☐ $T_a = P \Phi Z I_a / (2 \pi A)$
- ☐ $V = E_b + I_a R_a$
- ☐ $\theta_s = 360° / N$
- ☐ $n = 60 f / N$
- ☐ $K_b = K_t$ (in SI units)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motor Control Techniques: PWM Control, H-bridge Circuits, Motor Drivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motor Control Techniques: PWM Control, H-bridge Circuits, Motor Drivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motor Control in Mechatronics — Foundational Perspective

In the domain of mechatronic systems, **DC motors** (both brushed and brushless) form the fundamental actuation backbone — converting electrical energy into controlled mechanical motion. The intelligence of a mechatronic system is judged not merely by its ability to switch a motor ON or OFF, but by its **precision in regulating speed, direction, and torque**. This regulatory capability is achieved through three tightly coupled concepts: **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**, **H-Bridge Circuits**, and **Integrated Motor Driver ICs**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to explain the principles of PWM generation, design and analyze H-bridge configurations for bi-directional motor control, and interpret the working of commercially available motor driver ICs (e.g., L293D, L298N). Numerical treatment of duty cycle, average armature voltage, and switching losses is mandatory.

---

## 1.2 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) — The Core Concept

### Formal Definition
**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a digital modulation technique in which the width (duration) of a rectangular pulse is varied in direct proportion to the amplitude of the message/control signal, while the pulse amplitude and period remain constant. In motor control applications, PWM serves to vary the **average DC voltage** applied to the motor armature by rapidly switching the supply voltage ON and OFF at a fixed frequency.

### Key Parameters of a PWM Signal

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range (Motor Control) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Period** | T | Total time of one ON + OFF cycle | 1 ms to 50 ms |
| **Frequency** | f = 1/T | Switching cycles per second | **1 kHz to 25 kHz** (audible noise >20 kHz avoided) |
| **ON Time** | T_on | Duration when switch is closed | 0 to T |
| **OFF Time** | T_off | Duration when switch is open | 0 to T |
| **Duty Cycle** | D | Ratio of ON time to total period | **0 % to 100 %** |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Faucet & Bucket

Imagine you are filling a **bucket of water** to wash your car, and the tap only flows in two states: **fully ON** (high pressure blast) or **fully OFF** (no water). If you open the tap for **3 seconds** out of every **10 seconds** and close it for the remaining **7 seconds**, the *average* flow rate over time is exactly **30 %** of the maximum flow.

Now, the bucket (your motor) experiences an *effective* flow of 30 %, even though the tap never partially opens. This is exactly what PWM does to a motor:
- The switch is **fully ON** (12 V) for a fraction of the time.
- The switch is **fully OFF** (0 V) for the rest of the time.
- The motor, due to its **mechanical inertia** (rotational momentum), averages this chopped waveform and behaves as if it is receiving a lower continuous voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the motor "average" the signal?**
> The armature winding acts as a low-pass filter (a combination of inductance L and resistance R). Since the rotor's mechanical time-constant (τ_m = J/B, where J is moment of inertia and B is viscous damping) is much greater than the PWM period, the motor's speed cannot respond to the rapid switching — it responds only to the **average voltage**.

---

## 1.3 H-Bridge Circuit — The Direction Controller

### Formal Definition
An **H-Bridge** is an electronic switching circuit, configured in the shape of the letter "H", consisting of **four switches** (transistors, MOSFETs, or relays) arranged around a central DC motor load. By selectively activating pairs of switches, the polarity of the voltage applied across the motor terminals can be **reversed**, thereby controlling the **direction of rotation** of the motor (clockwise or anti-clockwise).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Train Track Switch

Think of an H-bridge as a **train track switching mechanism**:
- The motor is the train at a station.
- The four switches are like track switches.
- Depending on which pair of switches is set, the train (current) flows in one direction through the motor or the opposite.
- If both switches on the same side are activated, the train goes nowhere — this is the **braking** condition (current short-circuits through the motor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The H-bridge itself does **NOT** control speed — it only controls **direction** and acts as a current amplifier. To control speed, the H-bridge switches are typically driven by **PWM signals**.

---

## 1.4 Motor Driver ICs — The Practical Interface

### Formal Definition
A **Motor Driver IC** is a monolithic integrated circuit that packages one or more H-bridge circuits, along with **logic-level interfacing, free-wheeling diodes, thermal protection, and current-sense amplifiers** into a single chip. It acts as a **current/voltage amplifier** that bridges the low-current control outputs of microcontrollers (typically 5 V, 20 mA) and the high-current demands of motors (often 12–36 V, several amperes).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Power Steering System

Driving a heavy truck directly by hand-pushing the steering wheel is impossible. You need a **hydraulic power steering system** that amplifies your small steering input into a powerful movement. Similarly, a microcontroller's tiny 5 V/20 mA output cannot drive a 12 V/2 A motor directly — a **motor driver IC** is the "power steering" that amplifies the weak logic signal into a strong, motor-ready output.

### Common Motor Driver ICs Encountered in KTU Curriculum

| IC | Channels | Max Voltage | Max Continuous Current | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **L293D** | 2 (Dual H-bridge) | 36 V | **600 mA per channel** | Built-in clamp diodes; TTL compatible |
| **L298N** | 2 (Dual H-bridge) | 46 V | **2 A per channel** | Higher power; needs external diodes in some versions |
| **DRV8871** | 1 (Single H-bridge) | 45 V | 3.6 A peak | Integrated current sensing |
| **BTS7960** | 1 (Half-bridge pair) | 45 V | 43 A peak | High-current robotics/automotive |

> [!NOTE]
> The **L293D** is the most extensively discussed motor driver in the KTU Mechatronics syllabus due to its simplicity, dual-channel capability, and built-in protection diodes. It is used in the lab sessions for driving two DC motors or one stepper motor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PWM — Mathematical Foundation and Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Duty Cycle — The Heart of PWM Control

The **Duty Cycle (D)** is the most critical parameter in PWM. It is defined as the ratio of the ON-time (T_on) of the pulse to its total period (T), expressed either as a fraction (0 to 1) or a percentage (0 % to 100 %).

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

Equivalently, since f = 1/T:

$$D = f \cdot T_{on}$$

The complementary OFF duty cycle is:

$$D_{off} = 1 - D = \frac{T_{off}}{T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** In exam questions, always express the duty cycle as a **percentage** when answering "what is the duty cycle" type questions. Example: D = 0.4 means 40 %.

### 2.1.2 Average Armature Voltage

When a PWM signal of amplitude V_s (supply voltage) and duty cycle D is applied to a DC motor, the **average voltage** seen by the motor armature is:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = \left(\frac{T_{on}}{T}\right) \cdot V_s$$

This equation forms the basis of all DC motor speed control via PWM. By varying D, we effectively vary V_avg, which in turn controls the motor speed (since back-EMF and speed are proportional).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This relationship is the foundation of:
> - Variable-speed DC fans in laptops and computers
> - Cruise control in electric vehicles
> - Servo positioning systems in robotics
> - LED dimming circuits

### 2.1.3 Power Delivered to the Motor

The instantaneous power is either P_max = V_s · I (when ON) or 0 (when OFF). The **average power** delivered is:

$$P_{avg} = D \cdot V_s \cdot I_{avg} = V_{avg} \cdot I_{avg}$$

This is consistent with the average voltage concept, assuming current ripples are smoothed by motor inductance.

### 2.1.4 Frequency Selection Criteria

The PWM switching frequency **f_PWM** must be carefully selected based on:

1. **Audible noise constraint:** f_PWM > **20 kHz** to be inaudible to humans (ultrasonic operation).
2. **Switching loss constraint:** f_PWM should not be too high to avoid excessive MOSFET switching losses.
3. **Motor inductance constraint:** f_PWM should be high enough so that the motor's electrical time-constant τ_e = L_a / R_a is much larger than T = 1/f_PWM. This ensures current ripple is small.

A typical compromise for small DC motors: **f_PWM ≈ 1 kHz to 25 kHz**.

---

## 2.2 H-Bridge Circuit — Operating Modes

### 2.2.1 Switch Configuration Analysis

An H-bridge has four switches conventionally labeled **Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4**, with the motor connected at the central "bridge" between them. Each switch is paired diagonally:

| Mode | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Motor State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward (CW)** | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Current flows: V+ → Q1 → Motor → Q4 → GND |
| **Reverse (CCW)** | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Current flows: V+ → Q3 → Motor → Q2 → GND |
| **Brake (Fast stop)** | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | Motor terminals shorted; rapid deceleration |
| **Coast (Free run)** | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor disconnected; decelerates naturally |
| **SHOOT-THROUGH (FORBIDDEN)** | ON | ON | – | – | **Direct short across V+ and GND — DESTROVES CIRCUIT** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Shoot-Through is the #1 cause of H-bridge failure.** Q1 and Q2 (or Q3 and Q4) must NEVER be ON simultaneously. This is prevented in practice using:
> - **Dead-time insertion** in control signals (typically 100–500 ns delay)
> - Hardware interlocks (cross-coupled NAND gates)
> - Dedicated driver ICs with built-in shoot-through protection

### 2.2.2 PWM Modulation with H-Bridge

When combined with PWM, the H-bridge enables **regenerative braking** and **four-quadrant operation** of DC motors:

- **Quadrant I (Forward Motoring):** Q1 and Q4 PWM-modulated; current and torque both positive.
- **Quadrant II (Forward Braking):** Q1 OFF, Q4 ON, Q2 and Q3 modulated such that current reverses.
- **Quadrant III (Reverse Motoring):** Q3 and Q2 PWM-modulated; current and torque both negative.
- **Quadrant IV (Reverse Braking):** Q3 OFF, Q2 ON, Q1 and Q4 modulated.

### 2.2.3 Free-Wheeling (Flyback) Diodes

When a PWM switch turns OFF, the motor's inductive armature winding tries to maintain the current flow (Lenz's law). This generates a high reverse-voltage spike:

$$V_{spike} = -L_a \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

To protect the switching transistors, **free-wheeling diodes** (D1–D4) are placed anti-parallel to each switch. These diodes provide a path for the inductive current to circulate safely during the OFF period.

> [!NOTE]
> The **L293D** motor driver IC has these four free-wheeling diodes **integrated inside the chip**, which is why it is preferred for educational and rapid-prototyping applications — no external diode assembly is required.

---

## 2.3 Motor Speed Control — The Complete Picture

The steady-state speed of a separately excited DC motor is governed by:

$$N = \frac{V_{avg} - I_a R_a}{K \cdot \Phi}$$

Where:
- **N** = Motor speed (RPM)
- **V_avg** = Average applied voltage (controlled by PWM)
- **I_a** = Armature current
- **R_a** = Armature resistance
- **K·Φ** = Motor voltage constant × field flux

Since V_avg = D · V_s, we can directly write:

$$N = \frac{D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a}{K \cdot \Phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation is the **master equation** for KTU problems on PWM-based speed control. The relationship between N and D is approximately **linear** (assuming I_a is small and R_a is small) — this is why PWM provides excellent speed control linearity.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = T_{on} / T$ | Duty cycle (fraction) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $D(\%) = (T_{on} / T) \times 100$ | Duty cycle (percentage) | % |
| 3 | $f = 1 / T$ | PWM frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ | Average armature voltage | V |
| 5 | $P_{avg} = V_{avg} \cdot I_{avg}$ | Average motor power | W |
| 6 | $V_{spike} = -L_a \cdot (di/dt)$ | Inductive flyback voltage | V |
| 7 | $N = (V_{avg} - I_a R_a) / (K\Phi)$ | Steady-state motor speed | RPM |
| 8 | $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$ | Motor electrical time-constant | s |
| 9 | $\tau_m = J / B$ | Motor mechanical time-constant | s |
| 10 | $T_{dead} \approx 100$ to $500$ ns | Shoot-through dead-time | ns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Substitution Rule for KTU Problems:** Whenever a question mentions PWM with a motor, the FIRST step is always to compute $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$, and then plug it into the motor speed equation. This is a guaranteed 4–5 marks on the exam.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These techniques are not merely academic — they are the **industrial workhorses** of modern automation:

- **CNC Machines:** PWM-controlled servo motors achieve micron-level positioning.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** The traction motor is driven by a 3-phase IGBT inverter (an extension of the H-bridge concept) at switching frequencies of 5–20 kHz.
- **Drones & Robotics:** BLDC motor ESCs (Electronic Speed Controllers) use 3-phase H-bridges switching at 8–50 kHz.
- **HVAC Systems:** Variable-frequency drives (VFDs) for AC induction motors use the same PWM principle (called SVPWM — Space Vector PWM).
- **Consumer Electronics:** Laptop fans, camera autofocus mechanisms, and vibration motors all rely on PWM drivers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Voltage of a PWM Waveform

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the average voltage of a periodic PWM signal with amplitude V_s, ON time T_on, and period T.

**Given:**
- PWM signal amplitude: V_s
- ON time: T_on
- OFF time: T_off = T − T_on
- Period: T = T_on + T_off

**Step 1 — Define the time-domain PWM signal v(t):**
The signal is piecewise constant:

$$v(t) = \begin{cases} V_s & \text{for } 0 \leq t < T_{on} \\ 0 & \text{for } T_{on} \leq t < T \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of average value for a periodic signal:**

The mathematical definition of the average (DC component) of a periodic function over one period T is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the piecewise expression and split the integral:**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the first integral (the second integral is zero):**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot (t)\Big|_{0}^{T_{on}} \right] = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to obtain the final result:**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \cdot V_s = D \cdot V_s$$

**Conclusion:** The average voltage of a PWM waveform is the product of the duty cycle and the supply voltage. This is the **foundational result** used in all KTU numerical problems. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between PWM Duty Cycle and Motor Speed

**Statement:** A separately excited DC motor has armature resistance R_a = 1.2 Ω, voltage constant K·Φ = 0.05 V·s/rad, and is supplied via PWM with V_s = 24 V at D = 0.6. Assuming the motor runs at steady state with no load, derive the no-load speed in RPM.

**Step 1 — At no-load, the armature current I_a is approximately zero (no torque is required to overcome mechanical load):**

$$I_a \approx 0 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state DC motor speed equation:**

$$N = \frac{V_{avg} - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute I_a = 0 (no load condition):**

$$N = \frac{V_{avg}}{K\Phi}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the average voltage from the PWM signal:**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.6 \times 24 \, \text{V} = 14.4 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Convert angular speed (rad/s) to RPM:**

Since K·Φ is given in V·s/rad, dividing V_avg by K·Φ gives speed in rad/s. We need to convert rad/s to RPM using:

$$N_{RPM} = N_{rad/s} \times \frac{60}{2\pi}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the speed in rad/s:**

$$N_{rad/s} = \frac{14.4}{0.05} = 288 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to RPM:**

$$N_{RPM} = 288 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 288 \times 9.5493 \approx 2750.2 \, \text{RPM}$$

**Final Answer:** The no-load speed of the motor is approximately **2750 RPM**. ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Duty Cycle Required for a Desired Speed

**Statement:** The same motor (R_a = 1.2 Ω, K·Φ = 0.05 V·s/rad) draws a current of I_a = 2 A at a load that requires it to run at exactly 1800 RPM. If the supply is V_s = 24 V, determine the required PWM duty cycle D.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the speed equation to solve for V_avg:**

$$N = \frac{V_{avg} - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}$$

$$V_{avg} = N \cdot K\Phi + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Convert 1800 RPM to rad/s:**

$$N_{rad/s} = 1800 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 1800 \times 0.10472 = 188.496 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute V_avg:**

$$V_{avg} = (188.496 \times 0.05) + (2 \times 1.2)$$

$$V_{avg} = 9.4248 + 2.4 = 11.8248 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required duty cycle:**

$$D = \frac{V_{avg}}{V_s} = \frac{11.8248}{24} = 0.4927$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage:**

$$D(\%) = 49.27 \, \% \approx 49.3 \, \%$$

**Final Answer:** The required duty cycle is **D ≈ 0.493 (49.3 %)**. ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Loss in the H-Bridge Switch

**Statement:** An H-bridge uses MOSFETs with ON-state resistance R_DS(on) = 0.05 Ω. The motor draws a continuous current of I_a = 3 A during the ON period of the PWM cycle. Calculate the conduction power loss in the H-bridge for D = 0.7.

**Step 1 — Identify the current path:** During the ON time, current flows through **two** MOSFETs in series (one high-side + one low-side). So total ON-state resistance in the current path is:

$$R_{path} = 2 \times R_{DS(on)} = 2 \times 0.05 = 0.1 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute the conduction loss during the ON period (per unit time, when switches are ON):**

$$P_{ON, inst} = I_a^2 \cdot R_{path} = (3)^2 \times 0.1 = 0.9 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average conduction loss over the full PWM cycle:**

The switches conduct only during the ON time (fraction D of the period). The OFF period has zero conduction loss. So the average power loss is:

$$P_{loss, avg} = D \cdot P_{ON, inst} = 0.7 \times 0.9 = 0.63 \, \text{W}$$

**Final Answer:** The average conduction power loss in the H-bridge is **P_loss = 0.63 W**. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> This is why low R_DS(on) MOSFETs are preferred for high-current motor drivers — even a 0.01 Ω reduction in R_DS(on) can save significant power at high currents (e.g., 10 A motor: P = I²·R = 100 × 0.01 = 1 W per MOSFET).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A DC motor is controlled by a PWM signal with V_s = 48 V, T_on = 0.8 ms, T = 2 ms. The motor has R_a = 0.8 Ω, L_a = 5 mH, K·Φ = 0.06 V·s/rad. The motor draws 5 A at a particular load. Calculate:
(a) The duty cycle and PWM frequency.
(b) The average voltage applied to the motor.
(c) The steady-state speed in RPM.
(d) The electrical time-constant of the motor.
(e) Comment on whether the PWM frequency is sufficient to ensure low current ripple.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Duty cycle and frequency:**

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T} = \frac{0.8 \, \text{ms}}{2 \, \text{ms}} = 0.4 = 40 \, \%$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Part (b) — Average voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 48 = 19.2 \, \text{V}$$

**Part (c) — Steady-state speed:**

$$N_{rad/s} = \frac{V_{avg} - I_a R_a}{K\Phi} = \frac{19.2 - (5 \times 0.8)}{0.06} = \frac{19.2 - 4}{0.06} = \frac{15.2}{0.06} = 253.33 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$N_{RPM} = 253.33 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 253.33 \times 9.5493 = 2419.1 \, \text{RPM}$$

**Part (d) — Electrical time-constant:**

$$\tau_e = \frac{L_a}{R_a} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8} = 6.25 \, \text{ms}$$

**Part (e) — Sufficiency check:**

For smooth current (low ripple), we require: $T \ll \tau_e$, i.e., $1/f \ll \tau_e$.

$$T = 2 \, \text{ms}, \quad \tau_e = 6.25 \, \text{ms}$$

The ratio: $T / \tau_e = 2 / 6.25 = 0.32$.

Since 0.32 < 1, the period is shorter than the time-constant, meaning the current cannot change much during one PWM cycle. **The PWM frequency is marginally sufficient**, but for a more robust design, a higher frequency (smaller T) would be recommended. In practice, f = 500 Hz is in the audible range and may produce audible whine — a frequency above 20 kHz is preferred for quiet operation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PWM Waveform — Visual Anatomy

The following Mermaid block renders a precise state-transition diagram of a PWM cycle, illustrating the relationship between ON time, OFF time, period, and duty cycle.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Start of Period T] -->|t=0| B[Switch CLOSED - Vout equals Vs]
    B -->|After Ton seconds| C[Switch OPEN - Vout equals 0V]
    C -->|After Toff seconds| A
    B -.- D[Duty Cycle D equals Ton divided by T]
    C -.- E[Off Fraction equals 1 minus D]
    D -.- F[Average Voltage equals D times Vs]
```

**Key Mappings of the Diagram:**
- The closed loop `A → B → C → A` represents the **periodic repetition** of the PWM signal.
- `Ton` corresponds to the segment `A → B`.
- `Toff` corresponds to the segment `B → C`.
- The averaged output is **NOT a separate node** — it is the *low-pass-filtered response* of the motor.

---

## 4.2 H-Bridge Circuit — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of an H-bridge, showing the four switching elements, the motor load, the control inputs, and the direction-mode logic.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PowerRail["DC Supply Rail Vs"]
        P[Vs Positive Terminal]
        G[Ground Reference]
    end
    subgraph HSW["High-Side Switches"]
        Q1[Q1 High-Side MOSFET]
        Q3[Q3 High-Side MOSFET]
    end
    subgraph LSW["Low-Side Switches"]
        Q2[Q2 Low-Side MOSFET]
        Q4[Q4 Low-Side MOSFET]
    end
    subgraph MOTOR["Central Motor Load"]
        M[DC Motor M with Terminal A and Terminal B]
    end
    subgraph CTRL["Direction Control Logic"]
        DIR[Direction Input Signal]
        PWM[PWM Input Signal]
    end
    subgraph PROT["Protection Subsystem"]
        D1[Freewheeling Diode 1]
        D2[Freewheeling Diode 2]
        D3[Freewheeling Diode 3]
        D4[Freewheeling Diode 4]
    end
    P --> Q1
    P --> Q3
    Q1 --> A
    Q3 --> B
    Q2 --> G
    Q4 --> G
    A --> M
    M --> B
    CTRL --> Q1
    CTRL --> Q2
    CTRL --> Q3
    CTRL --> Q4
    Q1 -.- D1
    Q2 -.- D2
    Q3 -.- D3
    Q4 -.- D4
```

**Mode-to-Path Mapping (Reading the Diagram):**
- **Forward Mode:** CTRL energizes Q1 and Q4 → current path `Vs → Q1 → Terminal A → Motor → Terminal B → Q4 → GND`.
- **Reverse Mode:** CTRL energizes Q3 and Q2 → current path `Vs → Q3 → Terminal B → Motor → Terminal A → Q2 → GND`.
- **Brake Mode:** CTRL energizes Q2 and Q4 → motor terminals shorted through low-side switches.

---

## 4.3 PWM Generation using 555 Timer — Block Schematic

This Mermaid block depicts the **functional architecture** of a classic 555-timer-based PWM generator, which is part of the KTU Mechatronics lab syllabus.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TIMER["NE555 Timer IC in Astable Mode"]
        PIN8[Pin 8 VCC]
        PIN4[Pin 4 Reset]
        PIN7[Pin 7 Discharge]
        PIN6[Pin 6 Threshold]
        PIN2[Pin 2 Trigger]
        PIN3[Pin 3 Output]
        PIN1[Pin 1 Ground]
    end
    subgraph NET["External RC Network"]
        R1[Resistor RA from VCC to Pin 7]
        R2[Variable Resistor RB from Pin 7 to Pin 6]
        C1[Capacitor C from Pin 6 to Ground]
    end
    subgraph DRV["Output Driver Stage"]
        BJT[Transistor Switch Q]
        M[DC Motor Load]
    end
    PIN8 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> PIN7
    R2 --> PIN6
    PIN6 --> C1
    PIN2 --> PIN6
    PIN3 --> BJT
    BJT --> M
    M --> PIN1
```

**Operating Principle:**
- The capacitor C1 charges through **R1 + R2** during the ON time.
- The capacitor discharges through **R2** during the OFF time.
- The duty cycle is varied by adjusting R2 (the variable resistor / potentiometer).

**Standard KTU Formulas (555 Timer PWM):**

$$T_{on} = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$$

$$T_{off} = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$$

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B}$$

---

## 4.4 L293D Motor Driver — Internal Block Architecture

The L293D is a **quadruple half-H driver** — it contains two complete H-bridges internally, allowing it to drive two DC motors independently or one stepper motor. The following Mermaid block depicts its **internal functional blocks**.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph L293D["L293D Dual H-Bridge Motor Driver IC"]
        subgraph CH1["Channel 1 H-Bridge"]
            H1A[Half-H Driver A for Motor 1]
            H1B[Half-H Driver B for Motor 1]
        end
        subgraph CH2["Channel 2 H-Bridge"]
            H2A[Half-H Driver C for Motor 2]
            H2B[Half-H Driver D for Motor 2]
        end
        subgraph LOG["Logic Input Buffers"]
            L1[Input 1 and Input 2 for Channel 1]
            L2[Input 3 and Input 4 for Channel 2]
        end
        subgraph PROT["Built-in Protections"]
            CLAMP[Internal Clamp Diodes D1 through D8]
            THERM[Thermal Shutdown Circuit]
        end
        subgraph PWR["Power Pins"]
            VCC1[VCC1 Pin 16 - Logic Supply 5V]
            VCC2[VCC2 Pin 8 - Motor Supply up to 36V]
        end
    end
    subgraph EXT["External Microcontroller"]
        MCU[Arduino or 8051 or PIC Microcontroller]
    end
    subgraph MLOAD["External Motor Loads"]
        M1[DC Motor 1]
        M2[DC Motor 2]
    end
    MCU --> L1
    MCU --> L2
    L1 --> H1A
    L1 --> H1B
    L2 --> H2A
    L2 --> H2B
    H1A --> M1
    H1B --> M1
    H2A --> M2
    H2B --> M2
    VCC1 --> LOG
    VCC2 --> CH1
    VCC2 --> CH2
    CLAMP --- CH1
    CLAMP --- CH2
    THERM --- CH1
    THERM --- CH2
```

**Pin Configuration (KTU Exam Favorite):**

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| Pin 1 (Enable 1,2) | High = Channel 1 active; PWM applied here for speed control |
| Pin 2 (Input 1) | Logic input for Motor 1, half-H A |
| Pin 3 (Output 1) | Motor 1 terminal A |
| Pin 4, 5 (GND) | Both logic and motor grounds tied here |
| Pin 6 (Output 2) | Motor 1 terminal B |
| Pin 7 (Input 2) | Logic input for Motor 1, half-H B |
| Pin 8 (V_CC2) | Motor supply (up to 36 V) |
| Pin 9 (Enable 3,4) | High = Channel 2 active; PWM applied here |
| Pin 10 (Input 3) | Logic input for Motor 2, half-H C |
| Pin 11 (Output 3) | Motor 2 terminal A |
| Pin 12, 13 (GND) | Heat sink and ground |
| Pin 14 (Output 4) | Motor 2 terminal B |
| Pin 15 (Input 4) | Logic input for Motor 2, half-H D |
| Pin 16 (V_CC1) | Logic supply (5 V) |

**Direction Control Truth Table (per channel):**

| Input A | Input B | Enable | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| HIGH | LOW | HIGH | Rotate forward |
| LOW | HIGH | HIGH | Rotate reverse |
| HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | Brake (fast stop) |
| LOW | LOW | HIGH | Coast (free run) |
| X | X | LOW | Motor disabled |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) and explain how it is used to control the speed of a DC motor.**

**Model Answer:**

PWM is a modulation technique in which the width of a rectangular pulse is varied in proportion to a control signal, while the pulse period and amplitude remain constant. For DC motor speed control, the PWM signal switches the supply voltage V_s across the motor at a high frequency, with the ON-time fraction (duty cycle D) determining the effective average voltage. The relationship is **V_avg = D × V_s**. Since motor speed N is approximately proportional to V_avg (N = V_avg / K·Φ at no load), varying D linearly controls the motor speed. The high switching frequency (typically >1 kHz) ensures the motor's mechanical inertia smooths the pulsed waveform into a steady rotation. The key advantage of PWM over linear voltage regulation is its high efficiency, since the switch is either fully ON (low loss) or fully OFF (zero loss), unlike a linear regulator that dissipates excess power as heat.

*(3 marks — full credit: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for V_avg formula, 1 mark for advantage)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is an H-bridge circuit? List its four operating modes and explain why 'shoot-through' must be prevented.**

**Model Answer:**

An H-bridge is a switching circuit with four switches (transistors/MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" shape with the motor at the center. The four operating modes are:

1. **Forward (CW rotation):** Diagonal pair (Q1, Q4) ON; current flows Vs → Motor → GND.
2. **Reverse (CCW rotation):** Diagonal pair (Q3, Q2) ON; current flows Vs → Motor → GND in opposite direction.
3. **Brake (fast stop):** Both low-side switches (Q2, Q4) ON; motor terminals are shorted, causing rapid deceleration.
4. **Coast (free run):** All switches OFF; motor decelerates naturally due to friction.

**Shoot-through** occurs when both high-side and low-side switches on the same leg (e.g., Q1 and Q2) are ON simultaneously. This creates a direct low-resistance path from V_s to GND, causing a very large current that instantly destroys the switches. Prevention methods include dead-time insertion in control signals, hardware interlocks, and dedicated driver ICs with built-in protection.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the four modes, 1 mark for shoot-through explanation)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**A DC motor has armature resistance R_a = 1.5 Ω, voltage constant K·Φ = 0.04 V·s/rad, and no-load current I_a0 = 0.5 A. It is driven by a PWM signal with V_s = 30 V and duty cycle D = 0.5.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate:
(i) The average voltage applied to the motor.
(ii) The no-load speed in RPM.
(iii) The back-EMF at no-load.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the motor now drives a load that draws a current of 8 A:
(i) Calculate the new steady-state speed in RPM.
(ii) Determine the percentage drop in speed from no-load to loaded condition.
(iii) Find the developed electromagnetic torque at this loaded condition (given K_T = 0.04 N·m/A, the torque constant, which equals K·Φ in SI units).

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — No-Load Analysis

**(i) Average voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) No-load speed:**

The speed equation is:

$$N = \frac{V_{avg} - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}$$

At no-load, I_a = 0.5 A (small but non-zero due to friction and windage losses):

$$N_{rad/s} = \frac{15 - (0.5 \times 1.5)}{0.04} = \frac{15 - 0.75}{0.04} = \frac{14.25}{0.04} = 356.25 \, \text{rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_{RPM} = 356.25 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 356.25 \times 9.5493 = 3401.95 \, \text{RPM} \approx 3402 \, \text{RPM}$$

**(iii) Back-EMF at no-load:**

$$E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a = 15 - 0.75 = 14.25 \, \text{V}$$

**Verification using K·Φ:** $E_b = K\Phi \cdot \omega = 0.04 \times 356.25 = 14.25 \, \text{V}$ ✓

#### Part (b) — Loaded Analysis

**(i) Loaded speed:**

With I_a = 8 A:

$$N_{rad/s} = \frac{15 - (8 \times 1.5)}{0.04} = \frac{15 - 12}{0.04} = \frac{3}{0.04} = 75 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$N_{RPM} = 75 \times 9.5493 = 716.2 \, \text{RPM}$$

**(ii) Percentage drop in speed:**

$$\% \text{ drop} = \frac{N_{no-load} - N_{load}}{N_{no-load}} \times 100 = \frac{3402 - 716.2}{3402} \times 100 = \frac{2685.8}{3402} \times 100 \approx 78.95 \, \%$$

**(iii) Developed electromagnetic torque:**

$$T = K_T \cdot I_a = 0.04 \times 8 = 0.32 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Note for the student:** A drop of nearly **79 %** in speed under load is unusually high — this indicates the motor is being operated at a very low V_avg (15 V) compared to its rated voltage, and the high armature current causes a large IR drop. In practice, this motor would need a higher V_s (e.g., 48 V or 60 V) and a lower duty cycle to operate efficiently under load.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**A robot uses an H-bridge motor driver (L293D) to control a 12 V, 1.5 A DC gear motor through a microcontroller-based PWM signal. The PWM frequency is 2 kHz and the duty cycle is 75 %.**

**(a) [7 Marks]**
(i) Calculate the ON time and OFF time of the PWM signal.
(ii) Determine the average voltage applied to the motor.
(iii) The motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω. If the no-load current is 0.2 A, find the no-load speed of the motor in RPM. (Given K·Φ = 0.03 V·s/rad)

**(b) [7 Marks]**
(i) Draw the truth table of the L293D motor driver for a single channel and explain each mode of operation.
(ii) Explain with a neat sketch how the H-bridge achieves forward, reverse, and braking modes.
(iii) State two advantages of using PWM over linear voltage regulation for motor speed control.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a)

**(i) ON time and OFF time:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2000} = 0.5 \, \text{ms}$$

$$T_{on} = D \cdot T = 0.75 \times 0.5 \, \text{ms} = 0.375 \, \text{ms}$$

$$T_{off} = T - T_{on} = 0.5 - 0.375 = 0.125 \, \text{ms}$$

**(ii) Average voltage:**

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.75 \times 12 = 9 \, \text{V}$$

**(iii) No-load speed:**

$$N_{rad/s} = \frac{V_{avg} - I_a R_a}{K\Phi} = \frac{9 - (0.2 \times 0.5)}{0.03} = \frac{9 - 0.1}{0.03} = \frac{8.9}{0.03} = 296.67 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$N_{RPM} = 296.67 \times 9.5493 = 2832.9 \, \text{RPM}$$

#### Part (b)

**(i) L293D Truth Table for a single channel (Channel 1):**

| Input 1 (Pin 2) | Input 2 (Pin 7) | Enable 1 (Pin 1) | Motor 1 Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| HIGH | LOW | HIGH | Rotate Forward (CW) |
| LOW | HIGH | HIGH | Rotate Reverse (CCW) |
| HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | Fast Brake |
| LOW | LOW | HIGH | Coast / Free Run |
| X | X | LOW | Motor Disabled (Hi-Z) |

**Mode Explanations:**
- **Forward:** Current flows from Pin 3 → through motor → Pin 6 (H-bridge Q1, Q4 ON).
- **Reverse:** Current flows from Pin 6 → through motor → Pin 3 (H-bridge Q3, Q2 ON).
- **Fast Brake:** Both low-side switches ON; motor terminals shorted; kinetic energy dissipated as heat in armature resistance.
- **Coast:** All switches OFF; motor coasts down naturally to zero.

**(ii) H-bridge forward/reverse/brake operation:**
*(Refer to Section 4.2 of this document for the detailed Mermaid block diagram of the H-bridge functional architecture.)*

Forward: Q1 and Q4 ON → Current `V+ → Q1 → Motor Terminal A → Motor → Terminal B → Q4 → GND`.
Reverse: Q3 and Q2 ON → Current `V+ → Q3 → Motor Terminal B → Motor → Terminal A → Q2 → GND`.
Brake: Q2 and Q4 ON → Motor terminals shorted through low-side switches.

**(iii) Two advantages of PWM over linear regulation:**

1. **High efficiency:** In PWM, the switching element is either fully ON (low V_DS, low power loss) or fully OFF (zero current, zero power loss). Efficiency can exceed **90 %**. In contrast, a linear regulator (e.g., LM317) drops the excess voltage as heat in the pass transistor, with efficiency as low as 30–40 % at low duty cycles.

2. **Reduced thermal dissipation:** Since the switch is never in the linear region for a sustained period, heat generation is minimal. This allows compact, lightweight driver designs without bulky heat sinks. The same task in linear mode would require a large heat sink capable of dissipating several watts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in PWM:** Students often forget to convert rad/s to RPM. Remember: multiply rad/s by `60 / (2π)` = **9.5493** to get RPM.
> 2. **Duty cycle representation:** When a question says "duty cycle 75", students must write **D = 0.75** (fraction) OR **D = 75 %**, NOT just "75". One of these formats is mandatory.
> 3. **H-bridge pair matching:** When explaining forward mode, the EXACT pair is (Q1, Q4) and reverse is (Q3, Q2). Mixing up pairs is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Shoot-through is forbidden:** NEVER write that Q1 and Q2 are ON together. Even if the question says "list all combinations", explicitly state that Q1+Q2 (or Q3+Q4) ON is a **DESTRUCTIVE FAULT**, not a valid operating mode.
> 5. **Neglecting IR drop:** At no-load, students often write I_a = 0. This is technically true in the ideal case, but the KTU exam expects I_a to be a small finite value (friction + windage). Always include the IR term in the speed formula.
> 6. **Forgetting the conversion factor:** When the back-EMF constant K·Φ is given in V·s/rad, the speed N is in rad/s, NOT RPM. A direct division gives rad/s; conversion to RPM requires the `60/(2π)` factor.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PWM, H-Bridge, Motor Drivers**

- ☐ **PWM Definition:** A modulation technique that varies pulse width to control average power/voltage.
- ☐ **Master formula:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$. This appears in nearly every numerical.
- ☐ **Duty cycle formula:** $D = T_{on} / T = f \cdot T_{on}$.
- ☐ **Speed formula:** $N = (V_{avg} - I_a R_a) / (K\Phi)$.
- ☐ **Conversion factor:** $1 \, \text{rad/s} = 9.5493 \, \text{RPM}$; $1 \, \text{RPM} = 0.10472 \, \text{rad/s}$.
- ☐ **H-Bridge has 4 switches (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4)** arranged around the motor in an "H" topology.
- ☐ **Forward mode pair:** (Q1, Q4). **Reverse mode pair:** (Q3, Q2). **Brake mode pair:** (Q2, Q4). **Shoot-through (forbidden):** (Q1, Q2) or (Q3, Q4).
- ☐ **Freewheeling diodes** must be anti-parallel to each switch to handle inductive flyback voltage: $V_{spike} = -L_a (di/dt)$.
- ☐ **L293D:** Dual H-bridge, 600 mA/channel, built-in diodes, 5 V logic compatible. Most common in KTU labs.
- ☐ **L298N:** Dual H-bridge, 2 A/channel, needs external diodes in some configurations.
- ☐ **PWM frequency for motors:** 1 kHz to 25 kHz; above 20 kHz preferred to avoid audible noise.
- ☐ **Dead time** between high-side and low-side switch activation: typically 100–500 ns to prevent shoot-through.
- ☐ **PWM vs Linear:** PWM efficiency > 90 %; Linear efficiency often < 50 % — PWM is preferred for thermal and energy reasons.
- ☐ **Electrical time-constant:** $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$. For smooth current ripple, $T_{PWM} \ll \tau_e$.
- ☐ **Mechanical time-constant:** $\tau_m = J / B$. The motor's mechanical inertia must be much greater than the PWM period for smooth rotation.
- ☐ **Motor driver enable pin** receives the PWM signal; logic input pins (Input 1, Input 2) select direction.
- ☐ **4-Quadrant operation** of H-bridge + PWM: Forward motoring, Forward braking, Reverse motoring, Reverse braking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection Criteria for Actuators and Drive Systems in Mechatronic Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Selection Criteria for Actuators and Drive Systems in Mechatronic Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme Mechatronics (PEAET895)**, the *Selection Criteria for Actuators and Drive Systems in Mechatronic Design* refers to the systematic, multi-attribute decision-making process used by engineers to choose the most appropriate energy-conversion device (actuator) and its associated power transmission mechanism (drive system) that satisfies a given set of performance, environmental, economic, and operational specifications in an integrated mechanical-electronic-control system. An **actuator** is a device that converts a control signal (electrical, fluid, or thermal energy) into physical motion — typically linear or rotary displacement, force, or torque. A **drive system** is the intermediate power-transmission stage (gears, belts, lead screws, hydraulic lines, pneumatic circuits, amplifiers, or electronic drivers) that conditions, amplifies, and delivers the actuator's output to the load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Selection is **NOT** based on a single parameter. It is a *trade-off study* across **performance**, **power density**, **bandwidth**, **precision**, **cost**, **reliability**, **environment**, and **integration with the controller**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Muscle and the Tendon" Think of a mechatronic system as a **human body**: - The **actuator** is the **muscle** (it generates the force/motion). - The **drive system** is the **tendon, bone, and joint** (it transmits, modifies, and directs that force to where it is needed). - The **controller** is the **brain** (sends the signal). - The **sensor** is the **nerve ending** (provides feedback). When you want to lift a heavy box versus pick up a feather, you do **not** use the same muscle group with the same intensity. Your brain selects a different strategy — bicep vs. finger twitch — based on the *required force, speed, precision, and duration*. The engineering equivalent of this "brain-decision" is the **Selection Criteria Framework** for actuators and drives. ## 1.3 The Three Primary Actuator Families | Family | Energy Domain | Typical Devices | |---|---|---| | **Electrical** | Electrical → Mechanical | DC motor, Stepper, BLDC, Servo, Solenoid, Piezo | | **Hydraulic** | Fluid (oil) pressure → Mechanical | Hydraulic cylinder, Hydraulic motor | | **Pneumatic** | Compressed air → Mechanical | Pneumatic cylinder, Air motor, Muscle (PAM) | > [!NOTE] > **Fourth-generation smart actuators** (Shape Memory Alloys, Piezoelectric stacks, Electroactive polymers) are increasingly studied in KTU advanced modules, but the three classical families dominate industrial practice. ## 1.4 Fundamental Specifications That Define a Selection Problem Every selection problem begins with a clear specification sheet. The engineer must know: 1. **Type of motion required** — linear, rotary, or multi-axis. 2. **Magnitude of force/torque** — peak and continuous. 3. **Stroke length or angular range** — distance/angle to be moved. 4. **Speed / Velocity profile** — constant, variable, or point-to-point. 5. **Acceleration and bandwidth** — how fast the system must respond. 6. **Duty cycle** — continuous, intermittent, or pulse operation. 7. **Position accuracy and repeatability** — in mm or arc-minutes. 8. **Operating environment** — temperature, humidity, dust, explosive, cleanroom. 9. **Power source available** — battery, mains, compressor, pump. 10. **Cost, weight, size, and lifetime constraints.** > [!IMPORTANT] > If even **one** of these specifications is missing, the selection is **incomplete** and will fail at the integration stage — a common reason for KTU students losing marks in design questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hierarchical Selection Methodology (Step-by-Step Logic) A rigorous KTU-style answer follows this **top-down filtering process**: **Step 1 — Establish the Functional Requirement** Define the *purpose* of actuation: gripping, lifting, positioning, oscillating, valving, etc. This fixes the motion type (linear vs. rotary) and load class (force/torque class). **Step 2 — Quantify the Load** Compute the required **force (F)** or **torque (T)** using Newton's laws or static/dynamic equilibrium. Account for friction, gravity, and inertial loads. **Step 3 — Determine the Speed & Bandwidth** Calculate the required **linear velocity (v)** or **rotational speed (N)** and the **response time (t_r)**. Bandwidth dictates whether a high-dynamics actuator (servo) or low-dynamics (stepper, pneumatic) is suitable. **Step 4 — Match the Energy Domain** Filter candidates by available power source: - Mains/battery → **electrical** - High force, low speed → **hydraulic** - Light load, clean, fast cycling → **pneumatic** **Step 5 — Compute Power Density and Efficiency** Power density (W/kg) eliminates unsuitable candidates early. Hydraulic systems excel in power density; pneumatics are poor in efficiency. **Step 6 — Check Control Compatibility** Verify that the actuator accepts the controller's signal type (analog ±10 V, PWM, fieldbus, current loop, digital pulse train). **Step 7 — Verify Environmental & Safety Compliance** IP rating, temperature range, ATEX/explosion-proof rating, food-grade lubrication, EMI/EMC. **Step 8 — Economic Trade-off** Compare **Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)** = purchase + installation + energy + maintenance + disposal. ## 2.2 Selection Attributes — The Eight-Criterion Matrix Modern mechatronic selection uses a **weighted scoring model**. The eight canonical criteria are: | # | Criterion | Symbol | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Force/Torque Capacity | $F$ or $T$ | Maximum load the actuator can produce | | 2 | Speed / Bandwidth | $v$ or $\omega$ | Dynamic response capability | | 3 | Power Density | $P/m$ | Power per unit mass (W/kg) | | 4 | Precision / Resolution | $\delta x$ | Smallest controllable increment | | 5 | Stiffness | $k$ | Resistance to deflection under load | | 6 | Efficiency | $\eta$ | Useful output / input power | | 7 | Reliability / MTBF | $\lambda$ | Mean time between failures | | 8 | Cost | $C$ | Capital + operational expense | > [!NOTE] > In KTU examination answers, a **weighted sum model** is often used: > > $$\text{Score}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$$ > > where $w_j$ is the weight of criterion $j$ and $s_{ij}$ is the normalized score of actuator $i$ on criterion $j$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = m \cdot a + F_{\text{fric}} + F_{\text{gravity}}$ | Linear force balance (dynamic load) | N | | 2 | $T = J \cdot \alpha + T_{\text{fric}} + T_{\text{load}}$ | Torque balance (rotary load) | N·m | | 3 | $P = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power output | W | | 4 | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{60}$ | Hydraulic power from pressure $p$ (bar) and flow $Q$ (L/min) | W | | 5 | $P_{\text{pneu}} = p \cdot Q \cdot k$ | Pneumatic power (with cycle factor $k$) | W | | 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | Efficiency of energy conversion | dimensionless | | 7 | $k = \dfrac{F}{\Delta x}$ | Actuator stiffness | N/m | | 8 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency (defines bandwidth) | rad/s | | 9 | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{F}$ (for linear) | Max velocity from power limit | m/s | | 10 | $T_{\text{motor}} = K_t \cdot I$ | DC/BLDC torque-current constant | N·m/A | | 11 | $V_{\text{emf}} = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF in rotating machines | V | | 12 | $F_{\text{cyl}} = p \cdot A$ | Cylinder force from pressure and piston area | N | | 13 | $\dot{x} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Cylinder velocity from flow and area | m/s | | 14 | $L_i = \sum w_j s_{ij}$ | Weighted score for selection | — | | 15 | $LCC = C_{\text{cap}} + C_{\text{op}} \cdot t_{\text{life}}$ | Life-cycle cost model | ₹ / $ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU rule for tables:** No `|` character inside cells — use `\vert` for absolute value or "such that" in formulas. ## 2.4 Why the "Why" and "How" Matter - **Why** a weighted model? Because no actuator is universally "best"; trade-offs are **multi-objective**. - **How** does bandwidth enter? Through the natural-frequency formula $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, which must exceed the desired closed-loop bandwidth by a factor of 3–5 for stable control. - **Why** power density? It determines whether a system can be mobile (robotics, drones) or must be stationary (industrial press). - **How** is efficiency linked to heat? $P_{\text{loss}} = (1-\eta) P_{\text{in}}$ — this becomes heat that must be dissipated. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility | Industry | Selection Emphasis | Typical Choice | |---|---|---| | Robotics (industrial) | Precision, bandwidth, repeatability | BLDC servo + harmonic drive | | Aerospace flight control | Power density, reliability | Hydraulic / electro-mechanical | | Pick-and-place PCB assembly | Speed, precision, cleanroom | Linear servo motor | | Heavy press / injection moulding | High force, stiffness | Hydraulic cylinder | | Food packaging | Cleanliness, speed, low cost | Pneumatic cylinder | | Surgical robot | Sterility, fine motion, safety | Piezo / DC micromotor | | Mobile robot / AGV | Battery life, power density | BLDC + planetary gearbox | | CNC machine tool | Stiffness, accuracy, thermal stability | Servo motor + ballscrew | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Force and Power (Pneumatic / Hydraulic) **Starting point:** A fluid under pressure $p$ acting on a piston of effective area $A$ produces a force. The **theoretical force** is the product of pressure and area: $$F_{\text{theory}} = p \cdot A$$ This is derived directly from the definition of pressure: $p = F/A \Rightarrow F = pA$. The **effective (usable) force** accounts for friction and load direction: $$F_{\text{eff}} = p \cdot A \cdot \eta_m - F_{\text{fric}}$$ where $\eta_m$ is the mechanical efficiency of the cylinder (typically 0.85–0.95 for industrial cylinders) and $F_{\text{fric}}$ is the friction force. **The piston velocity** comes from continuity of flow: $$\dot{x} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$$ because the volume swept per second equals the flow rate. **The mechanical power** developed at the load: $$P_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{eff}} \cdot \dot{x} = (pA\eta_m - F_{\text{fric}}) \cdot \dfrac{Q}{A}$$ After algebraic simplification (cancelling $A$): $$P_{\text{out}} = p \cdot Q \cdot \eta_m - F_{\text{fric}} \cdot \dfrac{Q}{A}$$ **Step-by-step logic explanation:** - *Why pressure × area?* Because pressure is force per unit area; multiplying by total area gives total force on the piston face. - *Why divide flow by area?* Because volume flow rate is area × linear speed (continuity of fluid). - *Why multiply by $\eta_m$?* Because seals and friction in real cylinders dissipate some energy as heat. **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A hydraulic cylinder has bore diameter $D = 50$ mm, rod diameter $d = 30$ mm, supply pressure $p = 100$ bar, pump flow $Q = 20$ L/min, mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.9$, friction force $F_{\text{fric}} = 500$ N. Area of full bore (push direction): $$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$ Area of annulus (pull direction): $$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.050^2 - 0.030^2) = 1.178 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$ Push force: $$F_{\text{push}} = p \cdot A_1 \cdot \eta_m - F_{\text{fric}} = (100 \times 10^5)(1.963 \times 10^{-3})(0.9) - 500$$ $$F_{\text{push}} = 17{,}667 - 500 = 17{,}167\ \text{N}$$ Push velocity: $$\dot{x}_{\text{push}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3}/60}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.170\ \text{m/s}$$ Push power: $$P_{\text{push}} = F_{\text{push}} \cdot \dot{x}_{\text{push}} = 17{,}167 \times 0.170 = 2{,}918\ \text{W} \approx 2.92\ \text{kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > This exact form of computation is a **favourite KTU Part-B question pattern** under Module 2. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Torque and Speed Selection A mechatronic load requires torque $T_L$ at speed $N$ rpm. Convert rpm to angular speed: $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\ \text{rad/s}$$ The required motor power: $$P_{\text{out}} = T_L \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T_L}{60}$$ The required motor electrical input power (with efficiency $\eta_m$): $$P_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta_m}$$ For a DC/BLDC motor with torque constant $K_t$ (N·m/A) and back-EMF constant $K_e$ (V·s/rad), in SI units $K_t = K_e$. The motor current needed: $$I = \dfrac{T_L}{K_t \cdot \eta_g}$$ where $\eta_g$ is the gearbox efficiency (if any). **Step-by-step logic explanation:** - *Why $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$?* Because 1 revolution = $2\pi$ radians, and 1 minute = 60 seconds. - *Why divide by efficiency?* Real motors convert only a fraction of electrical input to mechanical output; the rest becomes heat. - *Why torque constant matters?* In DC machines, the electromagnetic torque is linear in armature current: $T = K_t I$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth and Stiffness Relation The closed-loop bandwidth of a position-controlled actuator is limited by its mechanical natural frequency: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$$ where $k$ is the actuator stiffness (N/m) and $m$ is the moving mass (kg). For stable control, the desired closed-loop bandwidth $f_{bw}$ must satisfy: $$f_{bw} \leq \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi \cdot 5} = \dfrac{1}{10\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$$ The factor of 5 ensures adequate phase margin (typically 45°–60°). **Numerical Example:** A pick-and-place system has moving mass $m = 2$ kg and requires closed-loop bandwidth $f_{bw} = 20$ Hz. Find the minimum required stiffness. First, find required natural frequency: $$\omega_n = 2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot f_{bw} = 2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 20 = 628.3\ \text{rad/s}$$ Then solve for stiffness: $$k = m \cdot \omega_n^2 = 2 \times (628.3)^2 = 7.9 \times 10^5\ \text{N/m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **standard KTU 14-mark derivation** in design problems. Memorise the formula and the factor-of-5 rule. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weighted Selection Score For three candidate actuators $A_1, A_2, A_3$ evaluated on four criteria with weights $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$: $$\text{Score}_{A_i} = w_1 s_{i1} + w_2 s_{i2} + w_3 s_{i3} + w_4 s_{i4}$$ Normalization ensures each $s_{ij} \in [0, 1]$. The candidate with the highest weighted score is selected: $$A^* = \arg\max_i \text{Score}_{A_i}$$ **Worked Example:** | Criterion | Weight | DC Motor Score | Stepper Score | Pneumatic Score | |---|---|---|---|---| | Precision | 0.30 | 0.9 | 0.8 | 0.4 | | Speed | 0.25 | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.7 | | Cost | 0.20 | 0.5 | 0.7 | 0.9 | | Cleanliness | 0.25 | 0.9 | 0.9 | 0.6 | Compute scores: $$\text{Score}_{DC} = 0.30(0.9) + 0.25(0.8) + 0.20(0.5) + 0.25(0.9)$$ $$= 0.270 + 0.200 + 0.100 + 0.225 = 0.795$$ $$\text{Score}_{Stepper}} = 0.30(0.8) + 0.25(0.5) + 0.20(0.7) + 0.25(0.9)$$ $$= 0.240 + 0.125 + 0.140 + 0.225 = 0.730$$ $$\text{Score}_{Pneu}} = 0.30(0.4) + 0.25(0.7) + 0.20(0.9) + 0.25(0.6)$$ $$= 0.120 + 0.175 + 0.180 + 0.150 = 0.625$$ **Conclusion:** DC motor scores highest (0.795) and is therefore selected. ## 3.5 Comparative Selection Matrix — Three Actuator Families | Parameter | Electrical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | |---|---|---|---| | Energy source | Electricity | Pressurized oil | Compressed air | | Force range | 1 N – 50 kN | 1 kN – 10 MN | 10 N – 50 kN | | Power density | High | **Highest** | Low | | Speed | High & precise | Medium, smooth | High, low load | | Precision | **Excellent** (sub-µm) | Good (mm) | Moderate (mm) | | Stiffness | Medium | **Very high** | Low (air is compressible) | | Bandwidth | **Highest** (>kHz for piezo) | Medium (10–100 Hz) | Low (<10 Hz) | | Efficiency | 70–95% | 50–70% | 10–30% | | Cleanliness | **Best** | Risk of oil leak | Risk of exhaust/contaminants | | Noise | Low | Medium | **High** (90–100 dB) | | Cost (capital) | Medium | High (pump, reservoir) | Low (compressor only) | | Cost (operating) | Low | High (energy, filtration) | Medium (air generation) | | Maintenance | Low | High | Medium | | Fire/Explosion safety | Spark risk | Oil fire risk | **Safest** | | Best application | Robotics, CNC, electronics | Press, aircraft, heavy lift | Packaging, clamping, low-cost automation | > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners **love** this kind of comparison table. Memorising it is worth 5–7 easy marks in any Part-B question. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Mechatronic Actuation Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR CTRL[Controller
Microcontroller / PLC] --> DRV[Drive System
Amplifier / Valve / Gear Train] DRV --> ACT[Actuator
Motor / Cylinder / Piezo] ACT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load
Joint / Tool / Gripper] LOAD --> FB[Feedback Sensor
Encoder / LVDT / Pressure] FB --> CTRL CTRL -.-> SRC[Power Source
Mains / Battery / Compressor] SRC -.-> DRV ``` **Explanation of flow:** - The **Controller** generates a low-power command signal. - The **Drive System** amplifies this signal (electronic amplifier, hydraulic servo valve, pneumatic proportional valve, or mechanical gear train). - The **Actuator** converts the conditioned energy into mechanical motion. - The **Motion** acts on the **Load**. - **Sensors** measure the output and feed it back to the controller, closing the loop. - The **Power Source** supplies the energy to the drive and actuator stages. ## 4.2 Hierarchical Selection Filter (Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Start: Define Specs] --> A1{Motion Type?} A1 -- Linear --> A2 A1 -- Rotary --> A3 A2{Force Level?} A3{Torque Level?} A2 -- High --> H1[Consider Hydraulic Cylinder] A2 -- Medium --> H2[Consider Pneumatic or Servo Linear] A2 -- Low / Precision --> H3[Consider Piezo or Voice Coil] A3 -- High & Slow --> R1[Consider Hydraulic Motor] A3 -- Medium --> R2[Consider Servo BLDC + Gearbox] A3 -- Precision Stepping --> R3[Consider Stepper Motor] A3 -- High Speed Low Load --> R4[Consider BLDC Direct Drive] H1 --> B1{Power Source Available?} H2 --> B1 H3 --> B1 R1 --> B1 R2 --> B1 R3 --> B1 R4 --> B1 B1 -- Mains / Battery --> E1[Refine: Electrical Subset] B1 -- Compressor --> E2[Refine: Pneumatic Subset] B1 -- Pump Station --> E3[Refine: Hydraulic Subset] E1 --> F1[Compute Weighted Score] E2 --> F1 E3 --> F1 F1 --> G1[Select Highest Score] G1 --> H0[Verify: Stiffness, Bandwidth, Cost] H0 --> Z[Final Selection] ``` **Explanation:** - The selection begins with a **specification** (top), cascades through binary filters, and converges on a **weighted-score optimisation** at the bottom. - Each diamond node represents a **decision gate** that prunes candidates. - The square nodes represent **filtering/optimisation** actions. ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT[INPUT SPECIFICATIONS] I1[Force and Torque] I2[Speed and Bandwidth] I3[Stroke and Range] I4[Duty Cycle] I5[Environment] I6[Cost and Lifetime] end subgraph FILTER[STAGE 1 DOMAIN FILTER] F1[Energy Source Check] F2[Motion Type Check] F3[Force Class Check] end subgraph EVAL[STAGE 2 PERFORMANCE EVAL] E1[Power Density] E2[Efficiency] E3[Stiffness] E4[Precision] end subgraph SCORE[STAGE 3 WEIGHTED SCORING] S1[Assign Weights to Criteria] S2[Normalize Scores 0 to 1] S3[Compute Weighted Sum] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT RECOMMENDATION] O1[Ranked Candidate List] O2[Final Selected Actuator plus Drive] end INPUT --> FILTER FILTER --> EVAL EVAL --> SCORE SCORE --> OUTPUT ``` **Explanation:** - **Stage 1** is a coarse filter that eliminates obviously unsuitable families. - **Stage 2** evaluates survivors against quantitative performance metrics. - **Stage 3** applies a multi-criteria decision algorithm to produce a **ranked list**, of which the top entry is the recommended actuator-drive pair. ## 4.4 Drive System Sub-Block (Detailed Internal Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DRIVE[DRIVE SYSTEM] D1[Signal Conditioner
DAC, Amplifier] D2[Power Stage
MOSFET, IGBT, Servo Valve] D3[Transmission
Gear, Belt, Ballscrew, Coupling] D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 end D3 --> A[Actuator Mechanical Output] FB2[Sensor] --> D1 A --> FB2 ``` **Explanation:** - The signal conditioner shapes the low-level control signal. - The power stage boosts current/voltage/flow to levels capable of driving the actuator. - The transmission adapts the output speed/torque/inertia to the load. - Feedback from the sensor closes the inner loop of the drive system. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *List any six criteria used for the selection of an actuator in a mechatronic system.* **Model Answer (6 points, 0.5 mark each):** 1. Force or torque capacity (continuous and peak) 2. Speed and bandwidth / response time 3. Precision, resolution, and repeatability 4. Power density and efficiency 5. Stiffness and dynamic performance 6. Operating environment (temperature, IP, cleanliness, hazardous area) 7. Cost (capital + operating + maintenance) 8. Compatibility with controller and power source 9. Weight, size, and packaging constraints 10. Reliability, MTBF, and service life *(Any six — full 3 marks)* ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *Compare hydraulic and pneumatic actuation systems on the basis of (i) working fluid, (ii) compressibility, (iii) stiffness, and (iv) typical application.* **Model Answer (tabular form expected):** | # | Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Working fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) | | 2 | Compressibility | Negligible (~0.5%) | High (~100% by volume) | | 3 | Stiffness | Very high (load holding inherent) | Low (requires check valves / accumulators) | | 4 | Typical application | Heavy press, injection moulding, aircraft | Packaging, clamping, low-cost pick-and-place | *(3 marks — 0.75 per row)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted] **(a)** Explain in detail the **eight-criterion weighted scoring model** used for the selection of actuators in mechatronic design. Mention how weights are assigned and scores normalized. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pick-and-place machine must move a 1.5 kg payload through 200 mm in 0.4 s, with a final positional accuracy of ±0.05 mm. Compare three candidate actuation systems — **BLDC servo with ballscrew**, **pneumatic cylinder**, and **hydraulic cylinder** — using the weighted scoring method with criteria and weights given below. Recommend the best choice. **(7 marks)** | Criterion | Weight | BLDC Servo | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | |---|---|---|---|---| | Precision | 0.35 | 0.95 | 0.50 | 0.70 | | Speed | 0.25 | 0.90 | 0.85 | 0.50 | | Cleanliness | 0.20 | 0.95 | 0.70 | 0.30 | | Cost | 0.20 | 0.60 | 0.90 | 0.40 | **Model Solution:** **(a) Eight-criterion weighted scoring model — explanation (7 marks):** The weighted scoring method is a **multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)** tool that converts qualitative preferences into a quantitative ranking. *Step 1 — Identify criteria.* Eight commonly used criteria are: (1) Force/torque capacity, (2) Speed/bandwidth, (3) Power density, (4) Precision, (5) Stiffness, (6) Efficiency, (7) Reliability, (8) Cost. *Step 2 — Assign weights $w_j$.* Weights reflect the **relative importance** of each criterion to the specific application. They are obtained from: $$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1, \quad w_j \in [0, 1]$$ For precision-critical applications (surgery, semiconductor), precision gets a high weight (e.g., 0.35). For cost-driven consumer products, cost weight rises. *Step 3 — Normalize raw scores.* Each actuator's raw performance on criterion $j$ is scaled to $s_{ij} \in [0, 1]$ using min-max or ideal-point normalization: $$s_{ij} = \dfrac{x_{ij} - x_{j,\min}}{x_{j,\max} - x_{j,\min}}$$ *Step 4 — Compute weighted score:* $$\text{Score}_{A_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$$ *Step 5 — Rank and select:* $$A^* = \arg\max_i \text{Score}_{A_i}$$ *Advantages:* Quantitative, transparent, repeatable. *Limitations:* Weights are subjective; sensitive to score normalization. **(b) Numerical scoring (7 marks):** $$\text{Score}_{BLDC} = 0.35(0.95) + 0.25(0.90) + 0.20(0.95) + 0.20(0.60)$$ $$= 0.3325 + 0.2250 + 0.1900 + 0.1200 = \mathbf{0.8675}$$ $$\text{Score}_{Pneu} = 0.35(0.50) + 0.25(0.85) + 0.20(0.70) + 0.20(0.90)$$ $$= 0.1750 + 0.2125 + 0.1400 + 0.1800 = \mathbf{0.7075}$$ $$\text{Score}_{Hyd} = 0.35(0.70) + 0.25(0.50) + 0.20(0.30) + 0.20(0.40)$$ $$= 0.2450 + 0.1250 + 0.0600 + 0.0800 = \mathbf{0.5100}$$ **Ranking:** BLDC Servo (0.8675) > Pneumatic (0.7075) > Hydraulic (0.5100). **Recommendation:** The **BLDC servo motor with ballscrew drive** is the best choice because it scores highest on precision (0.95) and cleanliness (0.95) — the two most heavily weighted criteria — while also providing excellent speed. The pneumatic system is a viable low-cost alternative if the precision requirement were relaxed. --- ### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted] **(a)** Explain the **drive system** in mechatronics. With neat block diagrams, describe the functions of (i) signal conditioner, (ii) power amplifier/stage, and (iii) mechanical transmission in an electrical drive system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 45$ mm, supply pressure $p = 140$ bar, pump flow rate $Q = 30$ L/min, and mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.88$. Friction force $F_{\text{fric}} = 800$ N. Compute: (i) effective pushing force, (ii) effective pulling force, (iii) pushing velocity, (iv) pushing power developed at the load. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Drive system explanation (7 marks):** A **drive system** is the intermediate stage between the controller and the actuator that conditions, amplifies, and shapes the energy delivered to the load. *Block diagram:* ``` [Controller] → [Signal Conditioner] → [Power Stage] → [Transmission] → [Actuator] → [Load] ↑ [Sensor] ↑ feedback ``` *Function of each block:* 1. **Signal Conditioner:** Converts the low-power controller output (e.g., 0–5 V, 4–20 mA, PWM) into a form suitable for the power stage. Includes filtering, isolation, dead-band compensation, and DAC stages. 2. **Power Amplifier/Stage:** Boosts the conditioned signal to the high-power level required by the actuator. Examples: MOSFET/IGBT inverter for a BLDC motor, servo valve for a hydraulic cylinder, proportional valve for pneumatics. 3. **Mechanical Transmission:** Adapts the actuator's raw output to the load. Examples: gear train (speed reduction, torque multiplication), belt-pulley (long-distance transmission), ballscrew (rotary to linear), rack-and-pinion (linear motion), harmonic drive (high-ratio, zero-backlash gear). *Design considerations:* stiffness, backlash, inertia matching, efficiency, and lubrication. **(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):** Bore area: $$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$ Annulus area (rod side): $$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.080^2 - 0.045^2) = 3.430 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$ Convert pressure: $p = 140\ \text{bar} = 140 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 1.4 \times 10^7\ \text{Pa}$. Convert flow: $Q = 30\ \text{L/min} = 30 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. **(i) Effective pushing force:** $$F_{\text{push}} = p \cdot A_1 \cdot \eta_m - F_{\text{fric}}$$ $$= (1.4 \times 10^7)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})(0.88) - 800$$ $$= 61{,}933 - 800 = \mathbf{61{,}133\ N} \approx 61.1\ \text{kN}$$ **(ii) Effective pulling force:** $$F_{\text{pull}} = p \cdot A_2 \cdot \eta_m - F_{\text{fric}}$$ $$= (1.4 \times 10^7)(3.430 \times 10^{-3})(0.88) - 800$$ $$= 42{,}258 - 800 = \mathbf{41{,}458\ N} \approx 41.5\ \text{kN}$$ **(iii) Pushing velocity:** $$\dot{x}_{\text{push}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{0.0995\ m/s} \approx 0.1\ \text{m/s}$$ **(iv) Pushing power developed at the load:** $$P_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{push}} \cdot \dot{x}_{\text{push}} = 61{,}133 \times 0.0995$$ $$= \mathbf{6{,}083\ W} \approx 6.08\ \text{kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losses:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Pressure in bar must be converted to Pa ($\times 10^5$); flow in L/min must be converted to m³/s (÷ 60 000). Forgetting either loses **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Confusing bore vs. annulus area** — Students often use $A_1$ for pull direction. Pull force is **always** smaller because rod reduces effective area. > 3. **Ignoring mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$** — A 1-mark deduction if omitted. > 4. **Forgetting friction force** — The problem statement explicitly provides it; ignoring it loses a mark. > 5. **Mixing up which velocity is asked** — Push velocity uses $A_1$, pull velocity uses $A_2$. > 6. **In weighted-score problems**, students often forget to **normalize** scores or forget the constraint $\sum w_j = 1$. Always check both. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Actuator & Drive Selection** - [x] **Actuator** = energy converter (signal → motion). **Drive** = power-conditioning stage in between. - [x] Three primary families: **Electrical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic**. - [x] **Eight canonical selection criteria**: Force/Torque, Speed, Power Density, Precision, Stiffness, Efficiency, Reliability, Cost. - [x] **Hydraulic** wins on power density and stiffness; **Pneumatic** wins on simplicity and safety; **Electrical** wins on precision, cleanliness, and efficiency. - [x] **Cylinder force:** $F = pA\eta_m - F_{\text{fric}}$; **Cylinder velocity:** $\dot{x} = Q/A$. - [x] **Motor power:** $P = T\omega = 2\pi N T / 60$. - [x] **DC motor relation:** $T = K_t I$; back-EMF $V = K_e \omega$; in SI $K_t = K_e$. - [x] **Bandwidth rule:** Required stiffness $k = m \omega_n^2$, where $\omega_n \geq 5 \cdot 2\pi f_{bw}$ for stable control. - [x] **Weighted-score formula:** $\text{Score}_i = \sum w_j s_{ij}$, with $\sum w_j = 1$. - [x] **Unit conversions to memorise:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa; 1 L/min = $10^{-3}/60$ m³/s; rpm to rad/s multiply by $2\pi/60$. - [x] **Selection is multi-objective** — never pick an actuator on a single parameter. - [x] **Life-cycle cost** = capital + operating + maintenance — total cost of ownership, not purchase price. - [x] **Environment** matters: IP rating, ATEX, cleanroom, temperature, EMI/EMC. - [x] **Drive system components**: Signal conditioner → Power stage → Mechanical transmission. - [x] **Transmission types**: Gear, belt, chain, ballscrew, lead screw, rack-pinion, harmonic drive, CVT. - [x] **Inertia matching** — for high-bandwidth servos, reflected load inertia to motor should be $\leq 5\times$ rotor inertia. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute exam strategy for this topic:** > 1. Always draw the **block diagram** of the actuation chain first — it earns 2 marks for free. > 2. Always state the **assumed efficiency** and **unit conversion** — examiners reward engineering discipline. > 3. Always compare **at least two alternatives** in a tabular form before recommending one. > 4. Always end the answer with a **one-line justification** of the chosen option.

topics.title: Overview of Proportional, Integral, and Derivative (PID) Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Proportional, Integral, and Derivative (PID) Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PID Controller (Proportional-Integral-Derivative Controller):** A generic **closed-loop feedback control mechanism** widely used in industrial control systems. It continuously calculates an **error value** $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired **setpoint (SP)** and a measured **process variable (PV)**, and applies a correction based on three distinct terms — **Proportional**, **Integral**, and **Derivative** — to minimize the error over time.

Mathematically, the **PID control law** in the continuous-time domain is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = Control signal (actuator command)
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ = Error signal (setpoint minus measured output)
- $K_p$ = **Proportional gain** (unitless or in %/%)
- $K_i$ = **Integral gain** (in $\text{sec}^{-1}$)
- $K_d$ = **Derivative gain** (in seconds)
- $r(t)$ = Reference / setpoint
- $y(t)$ = Process variable (measured output)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Cruise Control" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant speed of **80 km/h** on a highway. This is precisely what a PID controller does in a mechatronic system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
>
> - **P (Proportional):** "The faster I am below 80, the harder I press the accelerator." The harder you push the pedal is *proportional* to how far you are from the target speed.
> - **I (Integral):** "If I have been slow for the last 2 minutes, I should press the accelerator a little extra." The integral action compensates for *accumulated past errors* — like a small uphill slope that keeps slowing you down.
> - **D (Derivative):** "If I am approaching 80 too fast, I should ease off the accelerator to avoid overshooting." The derivative action looks at the *rate of change* of error and damps rapid movements.

## 1.3 Why PID? The Core Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** A PID controller is the **most widely deployed** control algorithm in the world — used in over **95% of industrial process control loops** (temperature, pressure, flow, level, motion, robotics).

| Property | Engineering Benefit |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Structureless model requirement** | No precise mathematical model of the plant is needed for tuning |
| **Robustness** | Tolerates moderate plant parameter variations |
| **Versatility** | P, PI, PD, and PID modes cover most practical control needs |
| **Tunability** | Three intuitive knobs $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$ adjust behavior |

## 1.4 The Error Signal — Heart of the Controller

The **error** $e(t)$ is the single most important signal inside any feedback controller. It represents the *deviation* between the **commanded (desired)** state and the **actual (measured)** state.

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

The controller's entire job is to drive $e(t) \rightarrow 0$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$, which is called **asymptotic setpoint tracking** or **zero steady-state error**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Control Actions — Decoded

### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Control
The proportional term produces a control action that is **directly proportional** to the instantaneous error.

$$u_P(t) = K_p \, e(t)$$

- **Effect:** Increases the **speed of response** and reduces (but never fully eliminates) the steady-state error.
- **Drawback:** Too high $K_p$ causes **overshoot and oscillation**; a pure P-controller always leaves a residual **steady-state offset** in most plants (a phenomenon called * droop* or *load error*).

### 2.1.2 Integral (I) Control
The integral term accumulates all past errors and produces a control action proportional to the **time integral of error**.

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

- **Effect:** **Eliminates steady-state error** completely (for a step input in a stable plant). Acts as the system's "memory."
- **Drawback:** Causes **integral windup** if the actuator saturates; slows down transient response.

### 2.1.3 Derivative (D) Control
The derivative term reacts to the **rate of change of error** — predicting future error behavior.

$$u_D(t) = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **Effect:** Adds **damping**, suppresses overshoot, improves **stability margins**, and anticipates error growth.
- **Drawback:** Highly sensitive to **measurement noise** (differentiation amplifies high-frequency noise); never used alone.

## 2.2 Tuning Variants of PID

| Controller Type | Control Law | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P-only** | $u(t) = K_p e(t)$ | Simple level control, slow processes |
| **PI** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt$ | Motor speed, flow, temperature |
| **PD** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | Servo positioning, motion control |
| **PID** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | Universal industrial process control |

## 2.3 Alternative Standard Form — The ISA / Parallel Form
