> [!NOTE]
> The **parallel form** (shown above) treats $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ as independent gains. The **standard (ISA) form** uses $K_c$, $T_i$ (integral time), and $T_d$ (derivative time) — these are equivalent and related by:
>
> $K_p = K_c$,  $K_i = \dfrac{K_c}{T_i}$,  $K_d = K_c T_d$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Error definition | Same units as PV |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law (time domain) | $K_p$: unitless; $K_i$: $\text{s}^{-1}$; $K_d$: s |
| 3 | $\frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID transfer function (Laplace) | S-domain equivalent |
| 4 | $\frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | PID transfer function (factored) | Useful for root locus |
| 5 | $K_p = K_c$, $\;K_i = K_c/T_i$, $\;K_d = K_c T_d$ | Parallel-to-Standard conversion | ISA form mapping |
| 6 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ | Steady-state error (Final Value Theorem) | $e_{ss} \to 0$ with integral action |
| 7 | $K_u$, $P_u$ (Ziegler-Nichols) | Ultimate gain and period | Tuning input parameters |
| 8 | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$ | Z-N tuning rules (classic) | For PID only |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** PID controllers are embedded in:
> - **Automotive:** Cruise control, ABS braking, engine fuel injection (ECU)
> - **Robotics:** Joint position, torque, and trajectory controllers
> - **Process Industries:** Temperature ovens, pH control, distillation columns
> - **Aerospace:** Flight control surfaces, autopilot, drone stabilization
> - **Consumer Electronics:** Camera autofocus, drone gimbals, 3D printer hot-ends

## 2.6 Performance Criteria Used to Judge a PID

- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from 10% to 90% of setpoint — *want small*.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to stay within ±2% (or ±5%) of final value — *want small*.
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** Peak overshoot above setpoint — *want small*.
- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** Final residual error — *want zero*.
- **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE):** $\int \vert e \vert \, dt$ — *want small*.
- **Integral of Squared Error (ISE):** $\int e^2 \, dt$ — *want small*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Control Law to Transfer Function

### Step 1 — Start with the time-domain PID control law

We begin with the fundamental PID equation acting on the error signal $e(t)$:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{d e(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Laplace Transform term-by-term

Recall the standard Laplace pairs:
- $\mathcal{L}\{e(t)\} = E(s)$
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = s E(s) - e(0^+)$

Assuming **zero initial conditions** ($e(0^+) = 0$):

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + K_i \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_d \cdot s E(s)$$

### Step 3 — Factor out $E(s)$

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) E(s)$$

### Step 4 — Define the controller transfer function

The PID controller transfer function $G_c(s)$ is defined as the ratio $\dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)}$:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

### Step 5 — Express as a single rational function

Multiply through by $s$ in both numerator and denominator form:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

This form is particularly useful for **root-locus analysis** since the controller adds a **pole at the origin** (which is why integral action eliminates steady-state error) and **two zeros** that the designer can place via $K_p, K_i, K_d$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error for a Unit Step Input

### Step 1 — Consider a unity-feedback system

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

The error $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s) = R(s) \left[1 - T(s)\right]$, which simplifies to:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Final Value Theorem

For a unit step input, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot (1/s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the loop gain for a P-controller

For a pure P-controller, $G_c(s) = K_p$, and a typical first-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) G_p(s) = K_p \cdot K = K_p K$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p K} \neq 0$$

> **Conclusion:** A pure P-controller **cannot eliminate** steady-state error for a step input — there is always a residual offset.

### Step 4 — Evaluate the loop gain for a PI-controller

For a PI-controller, $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$. As $s \to 0$:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} G_c(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \to \infty$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> **Conclusion:** The integral term introduces a **pole at the origin**, making the system **Type 1** (or higher) and **eliminating step-input steady-state error** completely. This is the analytical proof of the integral action's power.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Gain on System Performance

| Tuning Knob | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error | Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Increase $K_p$** | Decreases $\downarrow$ | Increases $\uparrow$ | Small change | Decreases $\downarrow$ (but not zero) | Worsens $\downarrow$ |
| **Increase $K_i$** | Small decrease | Increases $\uparrow$ | Increases $\uparrow$ | Eliminates $\rightarrow 0$ | Worsens $\downarrow$ |
| **Increase $K_d$** | Minor change | Decreases $\downarrow$ | Decreases $\downarrow$ | No effect | Improves $\uparrow$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** "**P** makes it **push**, **I** makes it **integrate** (forget nothing), **D** makes it **damp** (predict the future)."

## 3.4 Worked Example — Numerical Tuning Verification

**Problem:** A first-order plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+1}$ is controlled by a P-controller with $K_p = 3$. Find the steady-state error for a unit step input.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Open-loop transfer function:
$$G(s) = K_p \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{3 \cdot 2}{s+1} = \frac{6}{s+1}$$

Step 2: Position constant:
$$K_{pos} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \frac{6}{1} = 6$$

Step 3: Steady-state error for unit step:
$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_{pos}} = \frac{1}{1 + 6} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429$$

Step 4: Interpretation:
The P-controller with $K_p = 3$ leaves a residual error of about **14.29%** in the steady-state. To eliminate this, an integral term must be added.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop PID Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Reference Setpoint r of t"] --> nodeSum1(("Summing Junction e = r minus y"))
    nodeN["Measurement Noise n of t"] --> nodeSum1
    nodeSum1 --> nodeGc["PID Controller Gc of s = Kp plus Ki over s plus Kd s"]
    nodeGc --> nodeU["Control Signal u of t"]
    nodeU --> nodeSat["Actuator with Saturation Limits"]
    nodeSat --> nodeGp["Plant Process Gp of s"]
    nodeGp --> nodeY["Process Variable y of t Output"]
    nodeY --> nodeFB["Feedback Sensor H of s equal to 1"]
    nodeFB --> nodeSum1
    nodeY --> nodeOut["Measured Output"]

    classDef ctrl fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class nodeGc,nodeSat ctrl
    class nodeGp,nodeFB plant
    class nodeR,nodeY,nodeU,nodeOut,nodeN sig
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal PID Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeE["Error e of t"] --> nodeP["Proportional Block multiplied by Kp"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Integral Block Ki divided by s"]
    nodeE --> nodeD["Derivative Block Kd times s"]
    nodeP --> nodeAdd(("Summing Junction"))
    nodeI --> nodeAdd
    nodeD --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeOut["Total Control Output u of t"]

    classDef term fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class nodeP,nodeI,nodeD term
    class nodeE,nodeOut sig
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — PID in a Mechatronic Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL LAYER"]
        c1["Microcontroller or PLC or DSP"]
        c2["PID Algorithm Software"]
        c3["DAC and PWM Generator"]
        c1 --> c2 --> c3
    end

    subgraph POWER["POWER AND DRIVE LAYER"]
        p1["Motor Driver or H Bridge"]
        p2["DC Motor or Servo or Actuator"]
        p3["Power Supply Unit"]
        p3 --> p1 --> p2
    end

    subgraph SENSE["SENSING LAYER"]
        s1["Encoder or Potentiometer"]
        s2["Signal Conditioning and ADC"]
        s1 --> s2
    end

    subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL LAYER"]
        m1["Gears and Linkages"]
        m2["Load or End Effector"]
        m1 --> m2
    end

    c3 --> p1
    p2 --> m1
    m2 --> s1
    s2 --> c1

    classDef ctrl fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pwr fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sns fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mec fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class c1,c2,c3 ctrl
    class p1,p2,p3 pwr
    class s1,s2 sns
    class m1,m2 mec
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Tuning Loop — Ziegler-Nichols Closed-Loop Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start with Ki and Kd set to zero"] --> nodeB["Increase Kp gradually until sustained oscillation occurs"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Record the Ultimate Gain Ku and Oscillation Period Pu"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Choose Controller Type"}
    nodeD -->|P only| nodeE["Set Kp equal to 0 point 5 Ku"]
    nodeD -->|PI| nodeF["Set Kp equal to 0 point 45 Ku, Ti equal to 0 point 8 Pu"]
    nodeD -->|PID| nodeG["Set Kp equal to 0 point 6 Ku, Ti equal to 0 point 5 Pu, Td equal to 0 point 125 Pu"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Apply to Plant and Validate"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH

    classDef step fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeH step
    class nodeE,nodeF,nodeG dec
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a PID controller. Write down its control law in the time domain and identify the role of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is a feedback controller that computes a control action based on the present error, the accumulation of past errors, and the rate at which the error is changing.

The control law is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

| Term | Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **P-term** $K_p e(t)$ | Reacts to **current** error — speeds up response |
| **I-term** $K_i \int e \, dt$ | Reacts to **past accumulated** error — eliminates steady-state error |
| **D-term** $K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Reacts to **future trend** of error — adds damping, reduces overshoot |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for role of each term)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain why a pure proportional controller cannot give zero steady-state error for a step input. What modification overcomes this?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pure P-controller** has transfer function $G_c(s) = K_p$. For a unity-feedback system, the steady-state error to a unit step is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} K_p G_p(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p K_{plant}}$$

Since $K_p$ and $K_{plant}$ are **finite**, the denominator is finite, so $e_{ss} \neq 0$. There is always a residual offset.

**Modification:** Add an **Integral (I) action** to form a PI-controller. The I-term introduces a pole at the origin, making the system type increase by one. The loop gain $K_c(s)G_p(s) \to \infty$ as $s \to 0$, which drives $e_{ss} \to 0$.

*(2 marks for the analytical justification, 1 mark for the modification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Derive the transfer function of a PID controller in the s-domain, starting from its time-domain control law. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A unity-feedback system has plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{10}{(s+2)(s+5)}$ and uses a P-controller with $K_p = 1.5$. Find the closed-loop transfer function, and comment on the steady-state error for a unit step input. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Write the time-domain PID control law (1 mark):

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Laplace transform to each term, assuming **zero initial conditions** (2 marks):

$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + K_i \cdot \frac{E(s)}{s} + K_d \cdot s E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $E(s)$ (1 mark):

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Define the controller transfer function and write in standard form (2 marks):

$$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion (1 mark): The PID controller introduces a pole at the origin (giving integral action) and two zeros that the designer can place for desired closed-loop behavior.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{10}{(s+2)(s+5)}$, $K_p = 1.5$, unity feedback.

**Step 1:** Open-loop transfer function (1 mark):

$$G(s) = K_p G_p(s) = \frac{1.5 \times 10}{(s+2)(s+5)} = \frac{15}{s^2 + 7s + 10}$$

**Step 2:** Closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ (2 marks):

$$T(s) = \frac{15}{s^2 + 7s + 10 + 15} = \frac{15}{s^2 + 7s + 25}$$

**Step 3:** Steady-state error calculation using Final Value Theorem (2 marks):

Position constant $K_{pos} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \dfrac{15}{10} = 1.5$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_{pos}} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.5} = \frac{1}{2.5} = 0.4$$

**Step 4:** Comment (2 marks):
The steady-state error of **0.4 (or 40%)** is unacceptably large. To eliminate it, an **integral term** must be added, converting the controller from P to **PI**. This will also affect the transient response — settling time and overshoot may worsen, requiring careful tuning (e.g., using Ziegler-Nichols) and possibly a derivative term.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop PID control system. Discuss the contribution of each term ($K_p, K_i, K_d$) on:
  (i) Steady-state error
  (ii) Transient response
  (iii) Stability **(7 marks)**

**b)** Using the Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop (ultimate-cycle) tuning method, determine $K_p, T_i, T_d$ for a PID controller given that the ultimate gain $K_u = 8$ and the ultimate period $P_u = 2$ seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Term Effects (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Block diagram (2 marks):**

```
r(t) ──►(+)──────►[ PID Controller Gc(s) ]──►[ Plant Gp(s) ]──► y(t)
         ▲(-)                                              │
         └──────────────[ Sensor H(s) = 1 ]──────────────────┘
```

**Step 2 — Effect of $K_p$ (1.5 marks):**
- (i) Reduces (but never eliminates) steady-state error
- (ii) Decreases rise time, increases overshoot
- (iii) Too high $K_p$ destabilizes the system

**Step 3 — Effect of $K_i$ (1.5 marks):**
- (i) **Eliminates** steady-state error (the key benefit)
- (ii) Slows down response, may increase overshoot
- (iii) Can cause oscillations and reduce stability margin; risk of integral windup

**Step 4 — Effect of $K_d$ (2 marks):**
- (i) No direct effect on steady-state error
- (ii) Reduces overshoot, improves settling time
- (iii) Adds damping, improves stability margin; sensitive to noise

#### Part (b) — Ziegler-Nichols Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** State the Z-N closed-loop tuning formula for PID (2 marks):

$$K_p = 0.6 K_u, \quad T_i = 0.5 P_u, \quad T_d = 0.125 P_u$$

**Step 2:** Substitute given values $K_u = 8$, $P_u = 2$ s (2 marks):

$$K_p = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0 \text{ s}$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \times 2 = 0.25 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to parallel-form gains (2 marks):

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{1.0} = 4.8 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = K_p T_d = 4.8 \times 0.25 = 1.2 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4:** Final controller (1 mark):

$$u(t) = 4.8 \, e(t) + 4.8 \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + 1.2 \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting zero initial conditions** when applying the Laplace transform — students often write $\mathcal{L}\{de/dt\} = sE(s)$ without stating the assumption, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing parallel and standard form** of the PID equation — the relationship $K_i = K_c/T_i$ and $K_d = K_c T_d$ is frequently swapped; examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **Skipping units** in the numerical — $K_i$ has units of $\text{s}^{-1}$ and $K_d$ has units of s; this is a typical 0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Not commenting on the result** in numerical problems — KTU examiners award marks for *interpretation*, not just computation. A 40% steady-state error must be called out as "unacceptable," and the remedy must be suggested.
> 5. **Mixing up "P-only" Z-N formulas** — using $K_p = 0.6 K_u$ for a P-only controller (correct value is $0.5 K_u$) is a classic error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PID Control Overview**

- **Definition:** A PID controller is a feedback controller with three terms: **Proportional** (present), **Integral** (past), **Derivative** (future). Control law: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$.
- **Error signal:** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$; goal is $e(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.
- **Transfer function:** $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ — adds a **pole at origin** (Type-1 system) and **two zeros**.
- **P-action:** Speeds up response, reduces (not eliminates) steady-state error; risk of overshoot and oscillation.
- **I-action:** **Eliminates** steady-state error for step input; risk of integral windup and slower transient.
- **D-action:** Adds damping, reduces overshoot, predicts future error; sensitive to noise; never used alone.
- **Forms of PID:** P, PI, PD, PID — chosen based on process requirements.
- **Form conversions:** Parallel form $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$ ↔ Standard/ISA form $(K_c, T_i, T_d)$ via $K_p = K_c$, $K_i = K_c/T_i$, $K_d = K_c T_d$.
- **Tuning methods:** Ziegler-Nichols (ultimate cycle and reaction curve), Cohen-Coon, Tyreus-Luyben, software auto-tuning.
- **Z-N PID formulas:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$.
- **Performance metrics:** Rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$, overshoot $M_p$, steady-state error $e_{ss}$, IAE, ISE, ITAE.
- **Real-world use:** Over 95% of industrial control loops use some form of PID — DC motor speed, temperature ovens, cruise control, robotic joints.
- **Exam mantra:** "**P**ushes, **I**ntegrates, **D**amps." Always state units of gains. Always state zero initial conditions for Laplace.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Mechatronic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Example Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Mechatronic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Practical Implementation of Control Algorithms on Mechatronic Systems** refers to the systematic procedure of designing, simulating, tuning, and physically realizing feedback control laws (most commonly **PID — Proportional-Integral-Derivative** control) on a real mechatronic plant such as a **DC servomotor**, **inverted pendulum**, **robotic manipulator joint**, or **thermal process**. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this includes deriving the plant transfer function, applying a control law in the **s-domain / z-domain**, computing controller gains using **stability criteria** (Routh–Hurwitz, root locus, Bode), and deploying the algorithm on a **microcontroller (Arduino/STM32)**, **PLC**, or **DSP platform**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The emphasis is on *closing the loop* — meaning the student must connect a **mathematical model** of the physical system to a **computational algorithm** and verify **transient response specifications** (rise time, peak overshoot, settling time, steady-state error).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle** and want to maintain a perfectly straight path. Your **eyes** sense the deviation (error), your **brain** computes a correction (control action), and your **hands** turn the handlebar (actuator). This is a closed-loop control system. A **PID controller** does exactly this:

- **P (Proportional)** → "How far am I from the path?" — Big deviation → Big correction.
- **I (Integral)** → "How long have I been off the path?" — Removes persistent drift.
- **D (Derivative)** → "How fast am I drifting?" — Predicts and damps oscillation.

In a mechatronic system, the *eyes* become a **sensor** (encoder, tachogenerator, thermistor), the *brain* becomes a **microcontroller running the algorithm**, and the *hands* become an **actuator** (DC motor, servo, heater).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Rise Time (t_r):** Time to reach 90% of final value. Target = **0.5 – 1.5 s** (typical motor control).
> - **Peak Overshoot (M_p):** Maximum excursion beyond setpoint. Target ≤ **5–10%**.
> - **Settling Time (t_s):** Time to remain within ±2% of final value.
> - **Steady-State Error (e_ss):** Persistent error at infinity. Target → **0**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Motor back-EMF constant | K_b | 0.01 – 0.1 | V·s/rad |
| Motor torque constant | K_t | 0.01 – 0.1 | N·m/A |
| Armature resistance | R_a | 1 – 10 | Ω |
| Armature inductance | L_a | 1 – 100 | mH |
| Rotor inertia | J | 1×10⁻⁶ – 1×10⁻³ | kg·m² |
| Viscous damping | B | 1×10⁻⁶ – 1×10⁻⁴ | N·m·s/rad |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Mechatronic Control System

A mechatronic control loop consists of the following interconnected blocks, all of which the student must identify in a KTU exam diagram:

1. **Reference Input (r(t))** — The desired value (e.g., setpoint speed 1500 RPM).
2. **Comparator / Summing Junction** — Computes error: $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
3. **Controller C(s)** — Generates control signal u(t) from the error.
4. **Actuator (Power Amplifier + Motor)** — Converts low-power signal into mechanical motion.
5. **Plant G(s)** — The physical process (mechanical, thermal, or fluid).
6. **Sensor H(s)** — Measures output and feeds it back (typically H(s) = 1 for unity feedback).
7. **Output y(t)** — The actual measured variable (position, speed, temperature).

## 2.2 The Plant Model: Armature-Controlled DC Motor

The DC motor is the **canonical mechatronic plant** used in nearly every KTU question on this topic. Its transfer function (relating armature voltage $V_a(s)$ to angular position $\theta(s)$) is derived from the following physical laws:

**Step 1 — Electrical (Armature) Equation:**

$$V_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

$$e_b(t) = K_b \, \omega(t) = K_b \, \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical (Torque) Equation:**

$$J \, \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \, \omega(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)$$

**Step 3 — Laplace Transform and Elimination:**

After taking the Laplace transform of both equations, eliminating the armature current $I_a(s)$, and expressing output as $\Theta(s)$, we obtain the **standard KTU result**:

$$G(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \, \big[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_t \big]}$$

For KTU examinations, $L_a$ is **typically neglected** ($L_a \approx 0$) because it is much smaller than $R_a$ in the operating frequency range, simplifying the transfer function to a **second-order system**:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

where the **motor gain constant** and **mechanical time constant** are:

$$K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_b K_t}$$

$$\tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Double Integrator (1/s) Appears:** The factor $1/s$ arises because voltage is integrated *twice* to yield position — once via the motor's mechanical inertia ($1/s$ from velocity to position) and once via the electrical dynamics. This makes the open-loop plant a **Type-1 system**, which inherently produces **zero steady-state error to a step input** when closed-loop unity feedback is used — a critical KTU concept.

## 2.3 The PID Control Law

The continuous-time PID control law in the **time domain** is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

In the **s-domain**, the corresponding transfer function is:

$$C(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

The three terms have distinct physical roles:

- **Proportional term ($K_p e$):** Provides *immediate* corrective action proportional to current error. Increases **bandwidth and response speed**, but excessive $K_p$ causes **oscillation and overshoot**.
- **Integral term ($K_i \int e\, dt$):** Accumulates past errors to eliminate **steady-state error**. Too much $K_i$ leads to **integrator windup** and slow response.
- **Derivative term ($K_d \, de/dt$):** Anticipates future error based on its rate of change. Acts as a **damper**, reducing overshoot and improving stability.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Error signal in feedback loop | Same as plant output |
| 2 | $G_{cl}(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function (unity feedback) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $G(s) = \frac{K_t}{s[(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_b K_t]}$ | DC motor transfer function (voltage → position) | rad/V |
| 4 | $G(s) = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$ | Simplified DC motor (neglecting $L_a$) | rad/V |
| 5 | $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID controller transfer function | V/V |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Time-domain PID law | V (or A) |
| 7 | $M_p = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage peak overshoot from damping ratio $\zeta$ | % |
| 8 | $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2% settling time approximation | s |
| 9 | $t_r \approx \frac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | 10–90% rise time approximation | s |
| 10 | $K_p = \frac{\tau_m}{K_m \cdot t_r}$ (Ziegler rule) | Approximate P-gain for desired rise time | A/rad |
| 11 | $K_d = 2\zeta \sqrt{\frac{K_p}{K_m \tau_m}} - 1$ | Approximate D-gain using damping ratio | V·s/rad |
| 12 | $K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i}$, $T_i = \frac{\tau_m}{0.5}$ | Integral gain from integral time | 1/s |
| 13 | $K_u, T_u$ (Ziegler–Nichols Ultimate) | $K_p^* = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i^* = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d^* = 0.125 T_u$ | – |
| 14 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ | Final Value Theorem for steady-state error | – |
| 15 | $\text{RMS Error} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} e^2(k)}$ | Digital implementation performance metric | – |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The control algorithms discussed here form the **backbone of nearly every modern mechatronic product**:

- **CNC Machine Tool:** PID position control on each axis → precision of **±1 µm**.
- **Hard Disk Drive Head Positioning:** Voice-coil motor with closed-loop bandwidth > **1 kHz**.
- **Quadcopter / Drone:** Cascaded PID for attitude (inner loop) and altitude/position (outer loop).
- **Automotive Cruise Control:** PI control of throttle to maintain vehicle speed.
- **HVAC Thermostat:** PI temperature control with anti-windup.
- **Industrial Robotic Arm:** Computed-torque control (which uses PID-like feedback linearization) on each joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In practice, the *theoretical* PID gains obtained from the Bode or root-locus method must be **fine-tuned empirically** on the real plant using **Ziegler–Nichols** or **Tyreus–Luyben** tuning, because the mathematical model always omits nonlinearities (friction, saturation, backlash, dead-zone).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a DC Motor with PID

### 3.1.1 Given Plant (Type-1 Second-Order)

Assume the simplified DC motor position transfer function (a standard KTU assumption):

$$G(s) = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

with numerical values $K_m = 2.0$ rad/V·s and $\tau_m = 0.5$ s. A PID controller $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ is to be designed.

### 3.1.2 Forming the Open-Loop Transfer Function

The forward path transfer function $L(s)$ is the product of controller and plant:

$$L(s) = C(s) \cdot G(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) \cdot \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator:

$$L(s) = \frac{K_m \left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right)}{s^2 (\tau_m s + 1)}$$

### 3.1.3 Applying Unity Negative Feedback

The closed-loop transfer function $T(s)$ under unity negative feedback is:

$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)}$$

Substituting:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_m \left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right)}{s^2 (\tau_m s + 1) + K_m \left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right)}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$s^2 (\tau_m s + 1) = \tau_m s^3 + s^2$$

$$K_m(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) = K_m K_d s^2 + K_m K_p s + K_m K_i$$

Summing both:

$$\text{Denominator} = \tau_m s^3 + (1 + K_m K_d) s^2 + K_m K_p s + K_m K_i$$

Thus, the complete closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_m K_d s^2 + K_m K_p s + K_m K_i}{\tau_m s^3 + (1 + K_m K_d) s^2 + K_m K_p s + K_m K_i}$$

### 3.1.4 Comparing to a Standard Third-Order Characteristic Polynomial

The desired characteristic equation (denominator = 0) should match a **standard form** for a third-order ITAE-optimized system:

$$s^3 + 1.75 \omega_n s^2 + 2.15 \omega_n^2 s + \omega_n^3 = 0$$

Dividing our denominator by $\tau_m$:

$$s^3 + \frac{(1 + K_m K_d)}{\tau_m} s^2 + \frac{K_m K_p}{\tau_m} s + \frac{K_m K_i}{\tau_m} = 0$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for the PID Gains

Matching coefficients term by term gives a system of three equations in three unknowns:

$$\frac{1 + K_m K_d}{\tau_m} = 1.75 \, \omega_n$$

$$\frac{K_m K_p}{\tau_m} = 2.15 \, \omega_n^2$$

$$\frac{K_m K_i}{\tau_m} = \omega_n^3$$

Solving for the gains in terms of $\omega_n$:

$$K_d = \frac{1.75 \, \omega_n \, \tau_m - 1}{K_m}$$

$$K_p = \frac{2.15 \, \omega_n^2 \, \tau_m}{K_m}$$

$$K_i = \frac{\omega_n^3 \, \tau_m}{K_m}$$

### 3.1.6 Numerical Example with $\omega_n = 5$ rad/s

Substituting $K_m = 2.0$, $\tau_m = 0.5$, $\omega_n = 5$:

$$K_d = \frac{1.75 \times 5 \times 0.5 - 1}{2.0} = \frac{4.375 - 1}{2.0} = \frac{3.375}{2.0} = 1.6875$$

$$K_p = \frac{2.15 \times 25 \times 0.5}{2.0} = \frac{26.875}{2.0} = 13.4375$$

$$K_i = \frac{125 \times 0.5}{2.0} = \frac{62.5}{2.0} = 31.25$$

These gains are then verified in simulation (MATLAB/Simulink) and fine-tuned on the actual hardware.

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Error for a Unit Step Input

The error transfer function in unity feedback is:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + L(s)}$$

For a unit step input, $R(s) = 1/s$. Using the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + L(s)}$$

Substituting $L(s) = \frac{K_m(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s^2(\tau_m s + 1)}$:

$$L(0) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K_m K_i}{s^2(1)} = \infty$$

Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

This rigorously confirms that **a Type-1 system with PID controller has zero steady-state error to a step input** — a frequently asked KTU question.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Discrete-Time (Digital) Implementation

In practical mechatronic systems, the PID algorithm must be **digitally implemented** on a microcontroller using a sampled-data approach. The continuous derivative $\frac{de(t)}{dt}$ is replaced by the **backward difference approximation**:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

The continuous integral $\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau$ is replaced by the **trapezoidal (Tustin) approximation**:

$$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau) d\tau \approx T_s \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{e(j) + e(j-1)}{2}$$

The resulting **discrete-time velocity-form PID** (which avoids integrator windup via the $K_d \cdot \dot{y}$ formulation) is:

$$u(k) = K_p \, e(k) + K_i \sum_{j=1}^{k} \frac{T_s [e(j) + e(j-1)]}{2} + K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

| Component | Continuous Form | Discrete Form (Backward Difference) | Sampling Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| P-action | $K_p e(t)$ | $K_p e(k)$ | Instantaneous correction |
| I-action | $K_i \int e \, dt$ | $K_i T_s \sum e(k)$ | Eliminates steady-state error |
| D-action | $K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | $K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$ | Damps oscillations |
| Anti-windup | – | Conditional integration (clamp / back-calculate) | Prevents actuator saturation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist Sampling Rule (KTU 2024):** The sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$ must satisfy $f_s \geq 10 \, f_{BW}$ where $f_{BW}$ is the closed-loop bandwidth. For a DC motor with $t_s \approx 0.4$ s, $f_{BW} \approx 2.5$ Hz, so $T_s \leq 40$ ms. Arduino Uno (with $\sim$ 1 ms loop time) easily satisfies this.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Stability Check Using Routh–Hurwitz

The characteristic equation for the PID-controlled DC motor is:

$$\tau_m s^3 + (1 + K_m K_d) s^2 + K_m K_p s + K_m K_i = 0$$

Substituting numerical values $\tau_m = 0.5$, $K_m = 2.0$, $K_p = 13.44$, $K_d = 1.69$, $K_i = 31.25$:

$$0.5 s^3 + (1 + 2 \times 1.69) s^2 + 2 \times 13.44 \, s + 2 \times 31.25 = 0$$

$$0.5 s^3 + 4.38 \, s^2 + 26.88 \, s + 62.5 = 0$$

**Routh Array Construction:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $0.5$ | $26.88$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $4.38$ | $62.5$ | $0$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{(4.38)(26.88) - (0.5)(62.5)}{4.38}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $62.5$ | $0$ | $0$ |

Computing the $s^1$ entry:

$$b_1 = \frac{(4.38)(26.88) - (0.5)(62.5)}{4.38} = \frac{117.7344 - 31.25}{4.38} = \frac{86.4844}{4.38} = 19.745$$

All first-column entries are **positive** ($0.5, 4.38, 19.745, 62.5$), therefore the system is **stable**. No sign changes in the first column ⇒ **no right-half-plane poles** ⇒ closed-loop stable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete Closed-Loop Mechatronic Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref1[Reference Setpoint r of t] --> sum1((Summing Junction + -))
    sum1 --> ctrl1[PID Controller C of s]
    ctrl1 --> amp1[Power Amplifier / PWM Driver]
    amp1 --> dcm1[DC Servo Motor G of s]
    dcm1 --> out1[Mechanical Output theta of t]
    out1 --> load1[Mechanical Load Robotic Joint Disk Drive]
    load1 --> fb1[Encoder / Tachogenerator]
    fb1 --> fbcond1[Signal Conditioner Anti aliasing Filter]
    fbcond1 --> sum1
    out1 --> perf1[Performance Monitor Scope DAQ]
    style ctrl1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:2px
    style dcm1 fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style sum1 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Software Implementation Flow on a Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA([Power On Reset]) --> initA[Initialise GPIO ADC Timer PWM UART]
    initA --> calA[Calibrate Encoder Zero Position]
    calA --> loopStartA{Loop Forever}
    loopStartA --> readA[Read Encoder Counts Compute theta_meas]
    readA --> errA[Compute e of k = r of k - theta_meas of k]
    errA --> pidA[Compute P I D terms and Sum]
    pidA --> satA{Clamp u to PWM Limits}
    satA -->|Within Limits| pwmA[Write PWM Duty to Motor Driver]
    satA -->|Saturated| antiA[Apply Anti windup Back Calculation]
    antiA --> pwmA
    pwmA --> logA[Log Data to Serial / SD Card]
    logA --> loopStartA
    style pidA fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:2px
    style satA fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
    style pwmA fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: PID Tuning Workflow (Ziegler–Nichols Method)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state "Set Ki and Kd to Zero" as s1
    state "Increase Kp Until Sustained Oscillation" as s2
    state "Record Ku Ultimate Gain and Tu Period" as s3
    state "Apply Ziegler Nichols Table Formulas" as s4
    state "Test Step Response for M_p t_r t_s" as s5
    state "Fine tune Gains on Actual Plant" as s6
    state "Final Implementation Deployed" as s7
    [*] --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s5: Adjust if M_p too high
    s6 --> s7: Acceptable Response
    s7 --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hardware Interconnection (Practical Lab Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    psu1[24V DC Power Supply] --> drv1[Motor Driver L298N BTS7960]
    drv1 --> m1[DC Geared Motor with Encoder]
    m1 --> enc1[Quadrature Encoder A B Channels]
    enc1 --> mcu1[Arduino Mega / STM32 Nucleo]
    psu1 -->|5V Logic| mcu1
    mcu1 --> pwm1[PWM Output Pin to Driver]
    mcu1 --> uart1[USB UART to PC]
    pcp1[Host PC with MATLAB Python Serial Monitor] --> uart1
    uart1 --> pcp1
    m1 --> loadMech1[Inertia Disk or Lead Screw Slide]
    style m1 fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style mcu1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,stroke-width:2px
    style pcp1 fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#1A5490,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three advantages of closed-loop control over open-loop control in a mechatronic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Improved Accuracy:** Closed-loop control continuously measures the output and corrects deviations, drastically reducing steady-state error. For a DC motor, open-loop speed varies with load torque, but closed-loop feedback holds the setpoint regardless of disturbance (1 mark).
2. **Disturbance Rejection:** External disturbances (e.g., a sudden load on a robotic arm) are automatically compensated by the feedback action, which is impossible in open-loop systems (1 mark).
3. **Reduced Sensitivity to Plant Variations:** Component ageing, temperature drift, and manufacturing tolerances cause the open-loop gain to vary. Feedback reduces this sensitivity, making performance repeatable across units (1 mark).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the role of the derivative term in a PID controller. Why is the pure derivative action not implemented directly in a microcontroller?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **derivative term** $K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ acts on the *rate of change* of error. It provides **anticipatory** control — if the error is growing rapidly, the derivative term produces a large corrective signal even before the error becomes large. This **damps oscillations** and **reduces peak overshoot** (2 marks).

In a microcontroller, pure differentiation is **not implemented directly** because (i) it amplifies high-frequency noise present in the encoder/tachogenerator signal, and (ii) a sudden step change in the setpoint causes an **impulse-like spike** in the derivative output, which can damage the actuator. Hence, a **filtered derivative** $K_d \frac{s}{\tau_f s + 1} E(s)$ with $\tau_f \approx 0.01$–$0.1$ s is used (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Derive the transfer function of an armature-controlled DC servomotor relating the armature voltage to the angular position. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Linear magnetic circuit (no saturation).
- Constant field current.
- Viscous friction is the only opposing torque; Coulomb friction is neglected.
- $L_a$ may be neglected for low-frequency operation (commonly assumed in KTU problems).

**Electrical Armature Circuit (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law) (2 marks):**

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

The back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity:

$$e_b(t) = K_b \omega(t) = K_b \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$$

**Mechanical Torque Balance (Newton's Second Law for rotation) (2 marks):**

$$J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \omega(t) = T_m(t) = K_t i_a(t)$$

**Taking Laplace Transforms and Eliminating $I_a(s)$ (2 marks):**

From the electrical equation:

$$I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \Theta(s)}{L_a s + R_a}$$

Substituting into the mechanical equation (using $\Omega(s) = s\Theta(s)$):

$$J s^2 \Theta(s) + B s \Theta(s) = K_t \cdot \frac{V_a(s) - K_b s \Theta(s)}{L_a s + R_a}$$

Collecting $\Theta(s)$ terms on the left and $V_a(s)$ on the right:

$$\Theta(s) \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s^2 + B s) + K_b K_t s \right] = K_t V_a(s)$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\Theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_t \right]}$$

Neglecting $L_a$, this reduces to:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_t / (R_a B + K_b K_t)}{s \left[ \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_t} s + 1 \right]} = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

with $K_m = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_b K_t}$ and $\tau_m = \frac{R_a J}{R_a B + K_b K_t}$.

---

**(b) For the DC motor with $K_m = 2.0$ rad/V·s and $\tau_m = 0.5$ s, design a PID controller so that the closed-loop system is critically damped with natural frequency $\omega_n = 4$ rad/s. Verify stability using the Routh–Hurwitz criterion. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Form the Open-Loop Transfer Function (1 mark):**

$$L(s) = \frac{K_m (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{s^2(\tau_m s + 1)}$$

**Step 2: Closed-Loop Characteristic Polynomial (2 marks):**

$$\tau_m s^3 + (1 + K_m K_d) s^2 + K_m K_p s + K_m K_i = 0$$

**Step 3: Apply Design Specifications (2 marks):**

For a critically damped system with $\omega_n = 4$ rad/s, the dominant poles must be at $s = -4$ (double real pole). A suitable target polynomial that places both a third pole further left is:

$$(s + 4)^2 (s + a) = s^3 + (a + 8) s^2 + (8a + 16) s + 16a = 0$$

Choosing $a = 6$ (pole at $s = -6$ further left, ensuring critical damping):

$$s^3 + 14 \, s^2 + 64 \, s + 96 = 0$$

**Step 4: Match Coefficients (1 mark):**

Dividing our characteristic equation by $\tau_m = 0.5$:

$$s^3 + \frac{1 + K_m K_d}{0.5} s^2 + \frac{K_m K_p}{0.5} s + \frac{K_m K_i}{0.5} = 0$$

$$s^3 + 2(1 + 2 K_d) s^2 + 4 K_p s + 4 K_i = 0$$

Matching:

- $2(1 + 2 K_d) = 14 \Rightarrow K_d = 3.0$
- $4 K_p = 64 \Rightarrow K_p = 16.0$
- $4 K_i = 96 \Rightarrow K_i = 24.0$

**Step 5: Routh–Hurwitz Verification (1 mark):**

The characteristic polynomial with computed gains is:

$$0.5 s^3 + (1 + 2 \times 3) s^2 + 2 \times 16 \, s + 2 \times 24 = 0$$

$$0.5 s^3 + 7 s^2 + 32 s + 48 = 0$$

Routh array:

| | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $0.5$ | $32$ |
| $s^2$ | $7$ | $48$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{7 \cdot 32 - 0.5 \cdot 48}{7} = \frac{224 - 24}{7} = 28.57$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $48$ | $0$ |

All first-column entries are positive ⇒ system is **stable**.

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the procedure for implementing a digital PID controller on a microcontroller for a DC motor position control application. Include the pseudo-code structure and anti-windup mechanism. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Hardware Initialization (1 mark):**

- Configure a **quadrature encoder input** (Timer in encoder mode) to measure position.
- Configure a **PWM output** at 1 kHz or higher to drive the H-bridge motor driver.
- Set ADC reference and sampling rate.
- Set the control loop period $T_s$ using a **timer interrupt** (e.g., 10 ms).

**Step 2: Sampling the Error (1 mark):**

In each interrupt:
- Read current position $\theta_{meas}(k)$ from the encoder counter.
- Compute error: $e(k) = \theta_{ref}(k) - \theta_{meas}(k)$.

**Step 3: PID Computation (2 marks):**

Using the **velocity-form** to avoid large setpoint-induced derivative kicks:

$$P(k) = K_p \cdot e(k)$$

$$I(k) = I(k-1) + K_i \cdot T_s \cdot e(k)$$

$$D(k) = -K_d \cdot \frac{\theta_{meas}(k) - \theta_{meas}(k-1)}{T_s}$$

Note: derivative acts on the **measurement** (not the error) to avoid derivative kick on setpoint change.

$$u(k) = P(k) + I(k) + D(k)$$

**Step 4: Saturation and Anti-Windup (2 marks):**

- Clamp $u(k)$ to $[u_{min}, u_{max}]$ (e.g., $[-255, +255]$ for an 8-bit PWM).
- If saturation occurs, freeze the integrator: $I(k) = I(k-1)$ (conditional integration).
- Alternatively, use **back-calculation**: $I_{corrected} = I(k) + K_{aw} [u_{sat} - u(k)]$ with $K_{aw} = \sqrt{K_i / K_d}$.

**Step 5: Output Update (1 mark):**

Write the clamped $u(k)$ to the PWM register. Increment $k$ and return to the main loop.

---

**(b) A temperature control system has plant transfer function $G(s) = \frac{1}{(10s + 1)(50s + 1)}$. Design a PI controller using the Ziegler–Nichols open-loop method and compute the step response specifications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) Model (2 marks):**

The plant has no time delay, so we approximate it as a single first-order system with equivalent time constant. The dominant time constant is the larger one, $T = 50$ s, with process gain $K_p = 1$ and $L = 0$ s. Strict FOPDT form:

$$G(s) \approx \frac{K_p \, e^{-Ls}}{Ts + 1} = \frac{1 \cdot e^{0}}{50s + 1} = \frac{1}{50s + 1}$$

**Step 2: Apply Ziegler–Nichols Open-Loop PI Tuning Rules (2 marks):**

For a PI controller, the Z–N rules from the FOPDT parameters are:

$$K_c = \frac{0.9 T}{K_p L}$$

Since $L = 0$, this formula is singular. In practice, a small artificial delay $L = 0.1 T$ is introduced, or the **closed-loop ultimate gain** method is used instead. Assuming the closed-loop ultimate gain has been experimentally determined as $K_u = 8.0$ and the oscillation period $T_u = 30$ s, the Z–N PI settings are:

$$K_p = 0.45 K_u = 0.45 \times 8.0 = 3.6$$

$$T_i = 0.8 T_u = 0.8 \times 30 = 24 \text{ s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{3.6}{24} = 0.15 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (1 mark):**

With $C(s) = 3.6 \left(1 + \frac{1}{24s}\right) = \frac{3.6 (24s + 1)}{24 s} = \frac{0.15 (24s + 1)}{s} = \frac{3.6 s + 0.15}{s}$:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)} = \frac{(3.6 s + 0.15)/s}{1 + (3.6 s + 0.15)/[s(50s+1)]} = \frac{3.6 s + 0.15}{50 s^2 + 4.6 s + 0.15}$$

**Step 4: Step Response Specifications (2 marks):**

The denominator roots are:

$$s = \frac{-4.6 \pm \sqrt{21.16 - 30}}{100} = \frac{-4.6 \pm j \, 2.98}{100} = -0.046 \pm j \, 0.0298$$

The system is **underdamped** with:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{(-0.046)^2 + (0.0298)^2} = 0.0548 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{0.046}{0.0548} = 0.84$$

$$M_p = e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\% = e^{-\pi (0.84) / \sqrt{1 - 0.706}} \times 100\% = e^{-3.86} \times 100\% \approx 2.1\%$$

$$t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \frac{4}{0.046} = 87 \text{ s}$$

$$e_{ss}(\text{step}) = 0 \text{ (Type-1 system, integrator present)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing $s$ in denominator:** Students often write $G(s) = \frac{K_m}{\tau_m s + 1}$ for a motor — forgetting the integrator $1/s$ that arises because voltage-to-position integration occurs twice. **Always include $1/s$ in a position transfer function.** (-2 marks)
> 2. **Sign error in feedback loop:** In a summing junction, the convention is $r - y$, not $r + y$. Writing the wrong sign will give positive feedback and an unstable system. (-3 marks)
> 3. **Ziegler–Nichols unit confusion:** $K_p$ and $T_i$ from the table are in their respective engineering units; converting $K_i = K_p / T_i$ is often missed. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Routh array arithmetic slip:** A single sign error in the Routh array $b_1$ calculation makes the entire stability conclusion wrong. Double-check the cross-multiplication: $b_1 = (a_{21} a_{12} - a_{31} a_{22})/a_{21}$. (-2 marks)
> 5. **Anti-windup omission:** Practical PI/PID code must include anti-windup. Writing an integrator without saturation clamping loses 1–2 marks. (-1 mark)
> 6. **S-domain vs z-domain confusion:** When asked for a *digital* implementation, do not present only the continuous transfer function. Show the difference equation and sampling period explicitly. (-2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **DC motor position transfer function** is a **Type-1 system** with a pole at the origin: $G(s) = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau_m s + 1)}$.
- ✅ **Closed-loop transfer function** under unity negative feedback: $T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$.
- ✅ **PID gains** in time domain: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$; in s-domain: $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$.
- ✅ **Proportional** $K_p$ → faster response, smaller steady-state error; but causes overshoot.
- ✅ **Integral** $K_i$ → **eliminates** steady-state error; but causes windup and slow recovery.
- ✅ **Derivative** $K_d$ → damps oscillations, reduces overshoot; amplifies noise → use **filtered derivative** $K_d s / (\tau_f s + 1)$.
- ✅ **Type-1 system with step input** ⇒ $e_{ss} = 0$ by Final Value Theorem.
- ✅ **Routh–Hurwitz stability** requires all first-column entries of the Routh array to be **strictly positive**.
- ✅ **Ziegler–Nichols ultimate gain method** (closed-loop): set $K_i = K_d = 0$, increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation at gain $K_u$ and period $T_u$. Then apply the table: P: $K_p = 0.5 K_u$; PI: $K_p = 0.45 K_u, T_i = 0.8 T_u$; PID: $K_p = 0.6 K_u, T_i = 0.5 T_u, T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- ✅ **ITAE-optimized** third-order polynomial: $s^3 + 1.75\omega_n s^2 + 2.15\omega_n^2 s + \omega_n^3 = 0$.
- ✅ **Peak overshoot** formula: $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Settling time (2%):** $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$.
- ✅ **Anti-windup methods:** conditional integration (clamping) or back-calculation with $K_{aw} = \sqrt{K_i/K_d}$.
- ✅ **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \geq 10 f_{BW}$ for digital control; $T_s = 1/f_s$ must be set by a **timer interrupt** (not a `delay()` loop).
- ✅ **Derivative kick** is avoided by differentiating the **measurement** $y$, not the error $e$.
- ✅ **Hardware components** in a typical lab: DC motor + encoder, H-bridge driver (L298N / BTS7960), microcontroller (Arduino / STM32), regulated power supply, PC for data logging.
- ✅ **Performance metrics** in a KTU answer must be reported quantitatively: rise time, settling time, overshoot, steady-state error — never qualitative statements like "the system responds well."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Actuation Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Actuation System (KTU Syllabus Definition):** > An **actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into **physical motion** — linear or rotary — thereby performing work on a load. In a mechatronic system, the actuator is the *output power element* that physically executes the decision made by the controller. In any closed-loop mechatronic chain: $$\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Load}$$ The **actuation system** is the "muscle" of the mechatronic product. Without it, sensing and processing are useless. ### 1.2 Classification of Actuators | Energy Domain | Actuator Type | Typical Output | |---|---|---| | Electrical | DC/AC Servomotor, Stepper, Solenoid, Relay | Rotary / Linear | | Fluid Power – Pneumatic | Air Cylinder, Air Motor | Linear / Rotary | | Fluid Power – Hydraulic | Hydraulic Cylinder, Hydraulic Motor | Linear / Rotary | | Mechanical | Cam, Spring, Rack-Pinion, Lead Screw | Linear / Rotary | | Thermal / Smart | Shape Memory Alloy (SMA), Piezoelectric | Micro-displacement | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body:** > Think of an actuator as **human muscle**. Your brain (controller) sends electrical signals through nerves (signal lines) to your bicep (actuator), which then contracts and pulls the forearm (load) up. Replace the bicep with a pneumatic cylinder, an electric motor, or a hydraulic ram — same principle, different "fuel." > > - **Pneumatic** = like using compressed breath to push a piston (think syringe). > - **Hydraulic** = like a car jack — small force, big output. > - **Electric** = like a fan motor spinning blades. ### 1.4 Why Actuators Matter in Mechatronics > [!IMPORTANT] > - They determine the **speed, force, accuracy, and power** of the system. > - They interface between the *electronic control domain* and the *mechanical domain*. > - The choice of actuator **dictates the entire downstream design** (power supply, sensors, mounting). The **Force ($F$)** and **Power ($P$)** capability of an actuator are governed by: $$F = P \cdot A \quad \text{and} \quad P = F \cdot v = T \cdot \omega$$ where $P$ = pressure, $A$ = piston area, $v$ = velocity, $T$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular velocity. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Pneumatic Actuation Systems A pneumatic system uses **compressed air** (typically at 4–8 bar) as the working fluid. **Standard Block Sequence:** $$\text{Compressor} \rightarrow \text{Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL)} \rightarrow \text{Valves} \rightarrow \text{Cylinder/Motor} \rightarrow \text{Exhaust}$$ **Components & Their Roles:** - **Compressor** – Generates compressed air. - **FRL Unit** – Removes moisture (Filter), regulates pressure (Regulator), adds oil mist (Lubricator) for smooth piston motion. - **Direction Control Valve (DCV)** – 5/2 or 3/2 valves that direct air to either side of the cylinder. - **Cylinder** – Double-acting (DA) gives motion in both directions; single-acting (SA) uses a spring return. - **Flow Control Valve (FCV)** – Controls speed by throttling exhaust air. **Force produced by a pneumatic cylinder:** $$F_{\text{pneumatic}} = P \cdot A_{\text{piston}} - P_{\text{back}} \cdot A_{\text{rod}}$$ For double-acting, extending force is greater than retracting force because the rod-side area is reduced. **Pneumatic System Advantages & Disadvantages:** > [!NOTE] > ✅ Clean, fast, low cost, simple control > ❌ Low force density, air is compressible (poor stiffness), noisy exhaust ### 2.2 Hydraulic Actuation Systems Uses **incompressible oil** (typically at 50–350 bar) — far higher pressures than pneumatics. **Standard Block Sequence:** $$\text{Oil Tank} \rightarrow \text{Pump} \rightarrow \text{Valves} \rightarrow \text{Cylinder/Motor} \rightarrow \text{Reservoir}$$ **Components & Their Roles:** - **Hydraulic Pump** – Gear, vane, or piston type; delivers flow at high pressure. - **Pressure Relief Valve** – Safety device; opens if pressure exceeds set limit. - **DCV** – 4/3 or 4/2 valves for direction control. - **Cylinder / Motor** – Linear or rotary output. - **Accumulator** – Stores pressurized fluid for peak demand. **Force produced by a hydraulic cylinder:** $$F_{\text{hydraulic}} = P \cdot A_{\text{piston}} - P \cdot A_{\text{rod}} \quad (\text{extending})$$ **Speed of piston extension:** $$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$ where $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s) and $A$ = piston area (m²). **Hydraulic System Advantages & Disadvantages:** > [!IMPORTANT] > ✅ Very high force & power density, precise positioning, rigid (incompressible) > ❌ Expensive, leakage risk, needs cooling, environmental concerns ### 2.3 Electrical Actuation Systems #### A) DC Servomotor A **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motor** is the workhorse of mechatronic position control. **Back-EMF Equation:** $$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$ **Torque Equation:** $$T = K_t \cdot I_a$$ **Armature Circuit (KVL):** $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ **Transfer Function (angular velocity to input voltage):** $$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}$$ #### B) Stepper Motor Moves in **discrete steps** (e.g., 1.8°/step = 200 steps/revolution) without needing a position sensor (open-loop). - **Holding Torque** – torque resisting rotation when energized. - **Step Resolution** – controlled by drive electronics (full-step, half-step, micro-stepping). #### C) Solenoid Converts electrical energy to **linear push/pull motion** via magnetic field. Used in valves, relays, door locks. **Force on plunger:** $$F \propto \frac{N^2 I^2 \mu_0 A}{2 g^2}$$ where $N$ = turns, $I$ = current, $g$ = air gap, $A$ = cross-section area. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Board Exams** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Pneumatic / Hydraulic force | N | | 2 | $v = Q/A$ | Cylinder velocity | m/s | | 3 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Hydraulic power | W | | 4 | $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Motor torque | N·m | | 5 | $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF | V | | 6 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL (steady) | V | | 7 | $\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_{\text{steps}}}$ | Stepper step angle | degrees | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = Q_{\text{actual}}/Q_{\text{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | > **Standard Metrics Bolded:** > - **Pneumatic pressure: 4 – 8 bar** > - **Hydraulic pressure: 50 – 350 bar** > - **Stepper common resolution: 1.8° / 200 steps per revolution** > - **PMDC motor back-EMF constant: $K_b$ in V·s/rad; torque constant: $K_t$ in N·m/A** (with $K_b = K_t$ in SI units) ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pneumatic** → Pick-and-place robots, dental drills, automatic doors, food packaging. - **Hydraulic** → Excavators, injection moulding, aircraft flaps, vehicle brakes. - **Stepper** → 3D printers, CNC feed drives, camera autofocus. - **Servo** → CNC spindle, robotic arms, conveyor speed control. - **Solenoid** → Fuel injectors, washing-machine valves, pinball flippers. > [!NOTE] > **Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU-favorite exam tip):** > - **High force, slow, rigid** → Hydraulic > - **Low force, fast, clean** → Pneumatic > - **Precise position/speed, electronic control** → Servo/Stepper ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Force and Velocity of a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder **Given:** - Piston diameter: $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Rod diameter: $d = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$ - Supply pressure: $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ - Pump flow rate: $Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = 30 \times 10^{-3} / 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ **Step 1 — Calculate full bore area (piston side):** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Calculate annular area (rod side):** $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.05)^2 - (0.03)^2) = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Calculate extending force (high-pressure side is full bore):** $$F_{\text{ext}} = P \cdot A_1 = (10 \times 10^6)(1.963 \times 10^{-3}) = 19{,}635 \text{ N} \approx 19.63 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate retracting force (high-pressure side is annular):** $$F_{\text{ret}} = P \cdot A_2 = (10 \times 10^6)(1.257 \times 10^{-3}) = 12{,}566 \text{ N} \approx 12.57 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate extending velocity:** $$Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$v_{\text{ext}} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.255 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 6 — Calculate retracting velocity:** $$v_{\text{ret}} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.257 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.398 \text{ m/s}$$ > **Key Insight:** Retracting is *faster but weaker* because the same flow enters a smaller area. ### 3.2 Derivation: DC Servomotor Transfer Function **Starting from the mechanical and electrical equations:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Electrical:} \quad & V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b \\ \text{Back-EMF:} \quad & E_b = K_b \omega \\ \text{Mechanical:} \quad & T = J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega + T_L \\ \text{Torque:} \quad & T = K_t I_a \end{aligned}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute $T = K_t I_a$ into mechanical equation:** $$K_t I_a = J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega + T_L$$ **Step 2 — Take Laplace transform (assuming $T_L = 0$ for free response):** $$K_t I_a(s) = (J s + B)\omega(s)$$ $$\Rightarrow I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B)\omega(s)}{K_t}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Laplace to electrical equation:** $$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s)I_a(s) + K_b \omega(s)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $I_a(s)$ from Step 2 into Step 3:** $$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B)\omega(s)}{K_t} + K_b \omega(s)$$ **Step 5 — Factor $\omega(s)$:** $$V_a(s) = \omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$ **Step 6 — Multiply through by $K_t$:** $$K_t V_a(s) = \omega(s) \left[ (R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t \right]$$ **Step 7 — Final Transfer Function:** $$\boxed{\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}}$$ **Step 8 — Steady-state gain (set $s = 0$):** $$\frac{\omega(0)}{V_a(0)} = \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_b K_t}$$ > **KTU Exam Insight:** If the motor is unloaded, $T_L = 0$ and steady-state speed is approximately $\omega_{ss} \approx V_a / K_b$ (since $R_a B \ll K_b K_t$). ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Electric (Servo) | |---|---|---|---| | Working fluid | Air (compressible) | Oil (incompressible) | N/A (electrons) | | Pressure | 4–8 bar | 50–350 bar | N/A | | Force density | Low | **Very high** | Medium | | Stiffness | Poor | **Excellent** | Good (with feedback) | | Speed | Fast | Slow–Medium | Wide range | | Cost | Low | High | Medium | | Cleanliness | Very clean | Messy (leakage) | Clean | | Position accuracy | Poor (no feedback) | Good (with valves) | **Excellent (with encoder)** | | Typical application | Packaging, robotics grippers | Excavators, presses | CNC, robotic arms | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Pneumatic Actuation System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Mains Supply] --> B[Compressor] B --> C[Receiver Tank] C --> D[FRL Unit
Filter Regulator Lubricator] D --> E[Direction Control Valve
5 by 2 DCV] E -->|Air Push| F[Double Acting Cylinder] F --> G[Mechanical Load] F -->|Position Sensor| H[Limit Switches] H --> I[Controller PLC] I --> E ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Actuation System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Electric Motor] --> B[Hydraulic Pump] B --> C[Reservoir Tank] B --> D[Pressure Relief Valve] D --> E[Direction Control Valve
4 by 3 DCV] E -->|High Pressure Oil| F[Hydraulic Cylinder] F --> G[Heavy Load] F -->|LVDT Feedback| H[Controller] H --> E C -->|Return Line| E ``` ### 4.3 Classification of Actuators (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Actuators] P --> P1[Electrical] P --> P2[Fluid Power] P --> P3[Mechanical] P1 --> P1A[DC Servo Motor] P1 --> P1B[Stepper Motor] P1 --> P1C[Solenoid] P1 --> P1D[Relay] P2 --> P2A[Pneumatic Cylinder] P2 --> P2B[Pneumatic Motor] P2 --> P2C[Hydraulic Cylinder] P2 --> P2D[Hydraulic Motor] P3 --> P3A[Cam Follower] P3 --> P3B[Rack and Pinion] P3 --> P3C[Lead Screw] ``` ### 4.4 Closed-Loop Position Control (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Reference Position] --> S1[Summing Junction] S1 --> C[Controller PID] C --> AMP[Power Amplifier] AMP --> M[Motor Actuator] M --> L[Mechanical Load] L --> F[Position Sensor Encoder] F --> S1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Always draw block diagrams with *arrows* showing signal/power flow direction. Mark feedback loops clearly. Examiners award marks for labeled arrows and proper summing junctions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define an actuator. List the four major categories of actuators used in mechatronic systems.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **actuator** is a device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into physical motion — either linear or rotary — to perform mechanical work on a load. The four major categories are: 1. **Electrical actuators** (e.g., DC servomotor, stepper motor, solenoid) 2. **Hydraulic actuators** (e.g., hydraulic cylinder, hydraulic motor) 3. **Pneumatic actuators** (e.g., air cylinder, air motor) 4. **Mechanical actuators** (e.g., cam, lead screw, rack and pinion) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the function of the FRL unit in a pneumatic system.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **FRL unit** stands for **Filter–Regulator–Lubricator**, and is fitted immediately after the compressor receiver. Its functions are: 1. **Filter** – Removes dust, dirt, and condensed moisture from compressed air to protect downstream components. 2. **Regulator** – Maintains a constant downstream pressure despite variations in supply pressure. 3. **Lubricator** – Adds a controlled mist of oil into the air stream to lubricate moving parts (seals, pistons) and extend cylinder life. --- ### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Always **state assumptions** (e.g., "neglecting friction," "steady-state"). > - In derivations, **never skip algebraic steps** — partial marks are awarded for intermediate equations. > - Always include **units** in the final answer. A numerical answer without unit = 0 marks in calculation parts. > - For comparison-type questions, use a **table** — it earns easy structure marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a **pneumatic actuation system**. List its main components. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of **80 mm** and a rod diameter of **50 mm**. The pump delivers oil at a pressure of **80 bar** with a flow rate of **40 L/min**. Calculate: (i) Extending force (ii) Retracting force (iii) Extending velocity (iv) Retracting velocity *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Working of a Pneumatic Actuation System (7 marks):** A pneumatic actuation system uses **compressed air** as the working medium to produce linear or rotary motion. **Block Diagram:** ``` [Compressor] → [Receiver] → [FRL Unit] → [DCV] → [Cylinder] → [Load] ↑ [FCV controls speed] ↑ [Sensors feedback to PLC] ``` **Main Components & Functions (each 1 mark):** | Component | Function | |---|---| | Compressor | Converts electrical/mechanical energy into compressed air | | Receiver Tank | Stores compressed air to smooth pressure fluctuations | | FRL Unit | Filters, regulates, lubricates the air | | Direction Control Valve (DCV) | Routes air to either side of the cylinder | | Double-Acting Cylinder | Converts air pressure into linear motion (both directions) | | Flow Control Valve (FCV) | Controls piston speed by throttling airflow | | Sensors + PLC | Provide feedback and sequencing for automation | **Working:** The compressor compresses atmospheric air into the receiver. The FRL conditions this air (clean, dry, lubricated, regulated). On receiving an electrical signal from the PLC, the DCV directs the air into one side of the cylinder, pushing the piston and moving the load. Exhaust air from the opposite side is released (often through an FCV for speed control). When the DCV switches, air is sent to the other side, retracting the piston. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$ - $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - $P = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ - $Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ **Step 1 — Areas:** $$\begin{aligned} A_1 &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\ A_2 &= \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.08)^2 - (0.05)^2) = 3.068 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Forces:** $$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{ext}} &= P \cdot A_1 = (8 \times 10^6)(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) = 40{,}212 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{40.21 \text{ kN}} \\ F_{\text{ret}} &= P \cdot A_2 = (8 \times 10^6)(3.068 \times 10^{-3}) = 24{,}544 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{24.54 \text{ kN}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Velocities:** $$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{ext}} &= \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx \mathbf{0.133 \text{ m/s}} \\ v_{\text{ret}} &= \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{3.068 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2173 \text{ m/s} \approx \mathbf{0.217 \text{ m/s}} \end{aligned}$$ **Final Tabulated Answer (1 mark for clarity):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Extending force | **40.21 kN** | | Retracting force | **24.54 kN** | | Extending velocity | **0.133 m/s** | | Retracting velocity | **0.217 m/s** | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the working principle of a **DC servomotor** as an actuator in mechatronic systems. Derive its **transfer function** relating input voltage to output angular velocity. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Compare **pneumatic, hydraulic, and electric actuators** in terms of working fluid, pressure range, force density, stiffness, cleanliness, and typical applications. State which actuator is most suitable for: (i) an excavator arm, (ii) a food packaging pick-and-place robot, and (iii) a CNC spindle drive. Justify each choice. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) DC Servomotor — Working Principle & Transfer Function (7 marks):** A **DC servomotor** is a permanent-magnet DC motor used as a precise positioning actuator. When a DC voltage $V_a$ is applied to the armature, current $I_a$ flows producing torque $T = K_t I_a$. The rotor accelerates the connected load. As the rotor turns, it cuts magnetic flux, generating a **back-EMF** $E_b = K_b \omega$ that opposes the applied voltage. This back-EMF provides inherent **self-regulation** — making the servo ideal for closed-loop position control. **Working Principle (2 marks):** - Armature current → magnetic field → torque - Rotation → back-EMF → limits current and stabilizes speed - With feedback (encoder/LVDT), position is precisely controlled **Governing Equations:** $$\begin{aligned} V_a &= I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + K_b \omega \\ T &= K_t I_a = J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega \end{aligned}$$ **Transfer Function Derivation (full steps shown — 5 marks):** **Step 1 — Take Laplace transform of both equations:** $$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b \omega(s)$$ $$K_t I_a(s) = (J s + B) \omega(s)$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $I_a(s)$ from the mechanical equation:** $$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B) \omega(s)}{K_t}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the electrical equation:** $$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t} \cdot \omega(s) + K_b \omega(s)$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange:** $$V_a(s) = \omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}{K_t} \right]$$ **Step 5 — Final Transfer Function:** $$\boxed{\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}}$$ --- **(b) Comparison Table + Application Justification (7 marks):** | Property | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | Electric (Servo) | |---|---|---|---| | Working fluid | Compressed air | Hydraulic oil | Electrical current | | Pressure range | 4–8 bar | 50–350 bar | N/A | | Force density | Low | **Very high** | Medium | | Stiffness | Poor (air is compressible) | **Excellent (incompressible)** | Good with closed-loop | | Cleanliness | Very clean | Messy (leakage) | Clean | | Speed | Very fast | Slow–Medium | Wide range | | Cost | Low | High | Medium | | Typical applications | Packaging, grippers | Excavators, presses | CNC, robotics | **Application-Specific Justification (3 marks):** (i) **Excavator arm** → **Hydraulic actuator** ✓ *Justification:* Requires very high force to lift tons of earth, must be rigid and position-stable, and operates in harsh outdoor environments where high force density and stiffness are critical. (ii) **Food packaging pick-and-place robot** → **Pneumatic actuator** ✓ *Justification:* Operates in a clean-room/hygienic environment, requires fast repetitive motion, and oil leakage is unacceptable near food. Low cost is also a key benefit. (iii) **CNC spindle drive** → **Electric DC servomotor (or AC servo)** ✓ *Justification:* Needs very precise speed/position control, smooth variable speed, and easy integration with computer-based CNC controller. High accuracy and feedback are essential. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board Exam** ✅ **Actuator = Output Power Element** of any mechatronic system. Converts control signal → mechanical motion. ✅ **Three Main Categories:** Electrical, Fluid Power (Pneumatic/Hydraulic), Mechanical. ✅ **Pneumatic:** Air at **4–8 bar**; clean, fast, low force. Uses FRL unit + DCV + cylinder. ✅ **Hydraulic:** Oil at **50–350 bar**; very high force, rigid, expensive, prone to leakage. ✅ **DC Servomotor:** Back-EMF equation $E_b = K_b \omega$; torque equation $T = K_t I_a$; self-regulating. ✅ **Transfer Function of DC Servo:** $$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}$$ ✅ **Stepper Motor:** Open-loop positioning; step angle $\theta_s = 360°/N_{\text{steps}}$; common is **1.8°/step**. ✅ **Hydraulic Cylinder Force:** $F = P \cdot A$ where $A$ is the bore area (extending) or annular area (retracting). ✅ **Cylinder Velocity:** $v = Q/A$ — retracting is always faster than extending (smaller area). ✅ **Solenoid:** Linear actuator based on electromagnetism; used in valves and relays. ✅ **Selection Rule:** - High force + rigid → **Hydraulic** - Fast + clean + low cost → **Pneumatic** - Precise electronic control → **Servo/Stepper** ✅ **KTU Pitfall:** Always show the **back-EMF effect** in DC motor derivations; forgetting $K_b$ loses 2 marks. ✅ **KTU Pitfall:** Always specify whether the calculation is for *extending* or *retracting* in cylinder problems. ✅ **KTU Pitfall:** Use **proper SI units** in numerical answers: N, m/s, Pa, W — not bar, mm/s, or kgf. ✅ **Examiner's Bonus:** Drawing **neat block diagrams with labeled arrows** can earn 1–2 extra marks even in theory questions.

topics.title: Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuation Systems (Basics Only): Introduction, Reciprocating Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuation Systems (Basics Only): Introduction, Reciprocating Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Fluid Power Actuation Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Fluid Power Systems (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):**
> Fluid power is the use of a fluid (liquid or gas) under pressure to generate, control, and transmit power. **Hydraulic systems** use incompressible liquids (typically mineral oil), while **pneumatic systems** use compressible gases (typically atmospheric air). The actuation element that converts this fluid energy into mechanical linear or rotary motion is called an **actuator**.

A **Hydraulic System** transmits power using pressurized hydraulic fluid, while a **Pneumatic System** uses compressed air. The principal actuation element in both is the **reciprocating cylinder** (also called a linear actuator), which converts the pressure energy of the fluid into a straight-line pushing/pulling force on a piston connected to a piston rod.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscles of a Machine"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders as the **"muscles"** of an industrial machine, while the electric motor is its **"heart."** The motor pumps pressurized fluid (the "blood") through pipelines (the "arteries") to the cylinder, which then pushes a load (the "limb") to perform work — exactly like how a bicep muscle contracts to lift your hand.

If you have ever used a dentist's chair, a hydraulic jack to lift a car, or seen the brakes of a truck engage — you have witnessed fluid power in action. The air-brake system of a KSRTC bus, the lift mechanism of an earth-mover (JCB), and the robotic arms in automotive assembly lines all rely on these actuation principles.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> - **Atmospheric Pressure** = **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar** = **14.7 psi**
> - **1 bar** = **100 kPa** = **10⁵ N/m²**
> - **Standard Hydraulic Working Pressure:** **70 – 210 bar** (typical industrial range)
> - **Standard Pneumatic Working Pressure:** **4 – 8 bar** (typical industrial range)
> - **Density of Hydraulic Oil (ISO VG 46):** ≈ **870 kg/m³** at 40 °C

### 1.4 Comparison: Hydraulic vs. Pneumatic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — Study this thoroughly:**

| Parameter | Hydraulic System | Pneumatic System |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Incompressible (oil) | Compressible (air) |
| Working pressure | High (70 – 210 bar) | Low (4 – 8 bar) |
| Force output | Very high | Moderate to low |
| Speed control | Precise | Less precise (air is springy) |
| Compressibility | Negligible | Significant (≈ 0.83 × 10⁻³ /bar) |
| Leakage | Messy, fire hazard (oil) | Clean, harmless (air) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Typical use | Heavy press, injection moulding | Pick-and-place, packaging |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Pascal's Law (The Foundation)

The entire theory of hydraulic and pneumatic actuation rests on **Pascal's Law**, formulated by Blaise Pascal in 1653.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pascal's Law:** *"Pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicularly on every unit area of the containing vessel."*

**Why this law matters for actuation:**
- A small force applied on a small-area piston can be multiplied into a huge force on a large-area piston.
- This is the principle behind every hydraulic jack, lift, and press.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Cylinder Operation

A **reciprocating cylinder** consists of the following components:

1. **Cylinder barrel (tube)** — the hollow body containing the pressurized fluid.
2. **Piston** — a disc that slides inside the barrel, separating two chambers.
3. **Piston rod (ram)** — a rigid shaft attached to the piston, transmitting the force outside the cylinder.
