4. **End caps** — seal the cylinder barrel at both ends and provide ports for fluid entry/exit.
5. **Seals** — prevent fluid leakage between piston and barrel, and around the rod.
6. **Cushions** — air pockets at the cap ends to absorb kinetic energy at the end of stroke.

**How the cylinder produces motion (logical step-by-step):**
- Step 1: Pressurized fluid (oil or air) is delivered by a pump/compressor to one chamber of the cylinder through a control valve.
- Step 2: The fluid pressure acts on the **effective piston area** $A$, generating a **thrust force** $F$.
- Step 3: This force overcomes the load, and the piston begins to move, pushing the rod out (or pulling it in).
- Step 4: The fluid on the opposite side is exhausted back to the tank (hydraulic) or vented to atmosphere (pneumatic).
- Step 5: Direction is reversed by switching the control valve (typically a **4/3 DCV**).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of KTU cylinder numericals.**

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thrust Force | $F = P \times A$ | N | $P$ in Pa, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | Effective Area (full bore side) | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$ | m² | $D$ = bore diameter |
| 3 | Effective Area (rod side) | $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2})$ | m² | $d$ = rod diameter |
| 4 | Piston Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s) |
| 5 | Cylinder Time Stroke | $t = \dfrac{L \times A}{Q}$ | s | $L$ = stroke length |
| 6 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{hyd} = P \times Q$ | W | $P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 7 | Mechanical Power Output | $P_{mech} = F \times v$ | W | Useful output |
| 8 | Pressure from Force | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa | Inverse of Eq. 1 |
| 9 | Cylinder Volume | $V = A \times L$ | m³ | Fluid consumed per stroke |
| 10 | Air Consumption (pneumatic) | $Q_{air} = Q_{free} \times \dfrac{P_{abs,work}}{P_{abs,atm}}$ | m³/s | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** In a double-acting cylinder, the rod-side area is **smaller** than the cap-side area because of the rod occupying space. This means the cylinder is **stronger during extension** than during retraction, and **faster during retraction** than extension (for the same flow rate).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Systems Are Used

- **Hydraulic systems:** Injection moulding machines (L&T Demag), ship steering gear, aircraft landing gear, hydraulic presses (50,000 kN forging presses), excavators, and CNC machine tool slides.
- **Pneumatic systems:** Pick-and-place robots in FMCG packaging, dental chairs, bus/aircraft door actuators, painting robots, and vibration testing shakers.
- **Real-world Kerala context:** Cochin Shipyard uses hydraulic systems for vessel launching; LPG bottling plants in Kochi use pneumatics for cylinder conveying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cylinder Thrust Force (The Most Important Derivation)

**Given:** A piston of diameter $D$ (bore) is subjected to a gauge pressure $P$ on one face inside a hydraulic cylinder.

**To find:** The thrust force $F$ developed on the piston rod.

**Step 1 — Definition of Pressure:**
By definition, pressure is the normal force per unit area acting on a surface.

$$P = \frac{F_{net}}{A_{eff}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Force:**
Rearranging the definition to isolate force gives the fundamental fluid power equation.

$$F = P \times A_{eff}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Area of the Piston (full bore side):**
For a circular piston of bore diameter $D$, the geometric area is the area of a circle.

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Effective Area of the Piston (rod side):**
On the rod side, the piston area is reduced by the cross-sectional area of the rod (diameter $d$).

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D^{2} - d^{2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Final Thrust Equations:**

$$\boxed{F_{extend} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{F_{retract} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D^{2} - d^{2} \right)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Piston Velocity and Stroke Time

**Given:** Volumetric flow rate $Q$ entering the cylinder, piston area $A$, stroke length $L$.

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate is the rate of change of displaced volume:**

$$Q = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot dx}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Velocity is the time derivative of position $x$:**

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting:**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Stroke time:** For a constant velocity and constant flow rate:

$$t_{stroke} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \times A}{Q}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Power Flow in a Hydraulic Cylinder

**Step 1 — Hydraulic power input** (pressure energy delivered to the cylinder):

$$P_{hyd} = P \times Q$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $Q = A \cdot v$:**

$$P_{hyd} = P \times A \times v$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $F = P \times A$:**

$$P_{hyd} = F \times v$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The fluid power equation reduces to the familiar mechanical power output. This proves the energy is fully converted (in the ideal, lossless case).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cylinder Stroke Forces

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Analytical Setup for KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern:**

A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 45$ mm, and is supplied with a gauge pressure of 100 bar. Find the extend force, retract force, and the ratio of velocities.

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units:**

$$D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}, \quad d = 45 \text{ mm} = 0.045 \text{ m}, \quad P = 100 \text{ bar} = 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute areas:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^{2} = 5.026 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.080)^{2} - (0.045)^{2} \right) = 3.435 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute forces:**

$$F_{extend} = 10^{7} \times 5.026 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{retract} = 10^{7} \times 3.435 \times 10^{-3} = 34{,}350 \text{ N} \approx 34.35 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity ratio (assuming constant $Q$):**

$$\frac{v_{retract}}{v_{extend}} = \frac{A_1}{A_2} = \frac{5.026}{3.435} = 1.463$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:** The cylinder retracts **1.46 times faster** than it extends, for the same pump flow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Fluid Power Actuation System (Energy Conversion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Energy Source] --> B[Electric Motor]
    B --> C[Pump or Compressor]
    D[Atmospheric Air or Oil Reservoir] --> C
    C --> E[Pressurized Fluid at High Pressure]
    E --> F[Direction Control Valve 4 by 3 DCV]
    F --> G[Flow Control Valve FCV]
    G --> H[Reciprocating Cylinder Actuator]
    H --> I[Mechanical Linear Motion and Force]
    H --> J[Exhaust or Return to Tank]
    J --> K[Filter and Reservoir]
    K --> D
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from input (electrical energy) → prime mover (motor) → pump (energy converter) → control valves (signal stage) → cylinder (actuator) → output (mechanical motion). The loop back to the reservoir represents the **return line** — essential for re-circulation.

### 4.2 Architecture of a Double-Acting Reciprocating Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cylA[Double Acting Cylinder Cross Section]
        dir1[Port A Pressurized Fluid Inlet] --> cap1[Front End Cap with Cushion]
        cap1 --> barrelA[Barrel Chamber A Full Bore Area]
        barrelA --> pistonA[Piston with Seals]
        pistonA --> barrelB[Barrel Chamber B Rod Side Area]
        barrelB --> cap2[Rear End Cap with Cushion]
        cap2 --> dir2[Port B Pressurized Fluid Inlet]
        pistonA --- rod[Piston Rod]
        rod --- rodout[Rod Outlet External Load]
    end
    style cylA fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style pistonA fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style rod fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Classification of Reciprocating Cylinders (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RC[Reciprocating Cylinders] --> SA[Single Acting Cylinders]
    RC --> DA[Double Acting Cylinders]
    RC --> SP[Special Cylinders]
    SA --> SA1[Push Type Spring Return]
    SA --> SA2[Pull Type Gravity Return]
    DA --> DA1[Double Ended Rod Both Sides]
    DA --> DA2[Tandem Cylinder Double Force]
    DA --> DA3[Non Differntial Cylinder Equal Areas]
    SP --> SP1[Telescopic Multi Stage]
    SP --> SP2[Impact Cylinder Fast Acting]
    SP --> SP3[Cushioned Cylinder End Stroke Damping]
    style RC fill:#4FC3F7,stroke:#01579B
    style SA fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20
    style DA fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100
    style SP fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — How a 4/3 DCV Drives the Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Pump Supply Line P] --> DCV[4 by 3 DCV DCV Neutral A and B Blocked]
    DCV -->|Extend Position| AA[Port A connects to Pump]
    DCV -->|Extend Position| BB[Port B connects to Tank]
    AA --> CY[Cap End of Cylinder Piston Moves Out]
    BB --> CY
    CY -->|Retract Position| CC[Port B connects to Pump]
    CY -->|Retract Position| DD[Port A connects to Tank]
    CC --> CY
    DD --> CY
    style DCV fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#333
    style CY fill:#FF7043,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always mention **end cushions** in the cylinder diagram for full marks. They are often asked as a separate 2-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between a single-acting and a double-acting cylinder. State one industrial application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Fluid ports | One port only | Two ports (A and B) |
| Force direction | Push only (spring/gravity return) | Both push and pull |
| Control | Simple 3/2 valve | 4/3 directional valve |
| Application | Hydraulic jack, dump truck lift | Injection moulding clamp |

(1½ marks for clear distinction + 1 mark for applications + ½ mark for neat tabulation)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Model]**
*State Pascal's Law. Why is it the governing principle of all hydraulic machines?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pascal's Law states that *pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions* (2 marks). It is the governing principle because it allows a small input force on a small piston to generate a proportionally large output force on a larger piston (force multiplication) — this is the basis of hydraulic press, jack, and cylinder operation (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never write** the formula $F = P \times A$ **without specifying** whether $A$ is the bore-side area or the rod-side area. Marks deducted if rod area is not distinguished in retract force.
> - **Unit conversion** is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks. Always convert bar → Pa (× 10⁵) and mm → m (÷ 1000) before substitution.
> - **Atmospheric pressure** must be added when the question states "gauge pressure" and you need **absolute pressure** (e.g., in pneumatic air consumption problems).
> - Do **not** confuse *pump flow rate* (Q) with *piston velocity* (v) — they differ by a factor of area.
> - In cushion design, mention **deceleration, not elimination** of momentum — full marks require this nuance.

---

**PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder**. Highlight the function of end cushions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 100 mm and a rod diameter of 60 mm. The pump supplies oil at a flow rate of 60 L/min at a pressure of 80 bar. Calculate:
(i) The extend and retract forces. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The extend and retract velocities. **(2 marks)**
(iii) The hydraulic power input. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

A double-acting hydraulic cylinder consists of a **cylindrical barrel (1)** closed at both ends by **end caps (2)**. Inside the barrel slides a **piston (3)** fitted with **cup or O-ring seals (4)**. A **piston rod (5)** of diameter $d$ is threaded or bolted to the piston and protrudes through a sealed gland in one end cap. Two **ports (A and B)** are provided — port A admits pressurized fluid to the cap-end chamber, port B admits fluid to the rod-end chamber. **End cushions (6)** are small adjustable air pockets at both end caps that throttle the exhaust fluid at the end of stroke, gradually decelerating the piston. (3 marks for labelled sketch, 2 marks for working description, 1 mark for cushion explanation, 1 mark for seal/gland mention)

**Working:** When port A is pressurized and port B is connected to tank, oil pushes the piston outward → **extension stroke**. When the 4/3 DCV is shifted, port B is pressurized and port A is exhausted → **retraction stroke**. (Bonus: 1 mark for stating cushion reduces noise and shock)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m; $d = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m; $Q = 60$ L/min $= 60 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s; $P = 80$ bar $= 80 \times 10^{5}$ Pa.

**(i) Forces (3 marks):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(0.100)^2 - (0.060)^2\right] = 5.026 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{extend} = P \times A_1 = 80 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 62{,}832 \text{ N} \approx 62.83 \text{ kN}$$

$$F_{retract} = P \times A_2 = 80 \times 10^5 \times 5.026 \times 10^{-3} = 40{,}212 \text{ N} \approx 40.21 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Velocities (2 marks):**

$$v_{extend} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{retract} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{5.026 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1990 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{hyd} = P \times Q = 80 \times 10^5 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} = 8000 \text{ W} = 8 \text{ kW}$$

---

**PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare **hydraulic and pneumatic actuation systems** under the following heads: working fluid, working pressure, force capability, speed, accuracy, cleanliness, and cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pneumatic cylinder has a bore of 50 mm and a stroke of 200 mm. It is operated at a gauge pressure of 6 bar. The compressor supplies free air at 1 bar (atmospheric). Calculate:
(i) The thrust force during extension. **(2 marks)**
(ii) The volume of air consumed per stroke (at working pressure). **(2 marks)**
(iii) The free air consumption per stroke (at 1 bar). **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks):** Present a clean markdown table with the 7 parameters (1 mark per parameter, partial marking for valid 4–5 points; 1 mark for the overall conclusion that hydraulic suits heavy-duty and pneumatic suits light-duty clean applications).

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Pressure | 70 – 210 bar | 4 – 8 bar |
| Force | Very high (up to MN range) | Moderate (kN range) |
| Speed | 0.1 – 0.5 m/s typical | Up to 1 m/s |
| Accuracy | High (stiff system) | Lower (air is springy) |
| Cleanliness | Oil leaks possible | Very clean |
| Cost | Higher (pump, filtration) | Lower (compressor cheap) |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m; $L = 200$ mm $= 0.200$ m; $P_{gauge} = 6$ bar; $P_{atm} = 1$ bar (free air).

**(i) Thrust Force (2 marks):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F = P \times A_1 = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 1178 \text{ N} \approx 1.18 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Air Volume at Working Pressure (2 marks):**

$$V_{work} = A_1 \times L = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.200 = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 392.7 \text{ cm}^3$$

**(iii) Free Air Consumption per Stroke (3 marks):**

$$P_{abs,work} = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ bar}; \quad P_{abs,atm} = 1 \text{ bar}$$

$$V_{free} = V_{work} \times \frac{P_{abs,work}}{P_{abs,atm}} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{7}{1} = 2.749 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 2.749 \text{ L per stroke}$$

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✔ Fluid power = **Hydraulic (oil, high pressure, heavy duty)** + **Pneumatic (air, low pressure, light duty)**.
- ✔ Governing principle = **Pascal's Law** (transmission of pressure in confined fluid).
- ✔ Thrust force: $F = P \times A$; always use the **appropriate area** ($A_1$ extend, $A_2$ retract).
- ✔ Bore-side area: $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$; Rod-side area: $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$.
- ✔ Piston velocity: $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$; Stroke time: $t = \dfrac{L \times A}{Q}$.
- ✔ Power: $P_{hyd} = P \times Q = F \times v$ (energy equivalence in ideal case).
- ✔ Double-acting cylinder ⇒ **two ports, two strokes, 4/3 DCV required**.
- ✔ Single-acting cylinder ⇒ **one port, spring/gravity return, 3/2 valve sufficient**.
- ✔ End cushions ⇒ throttle exhaust at end-of-stroke to **decelerate** piston (reduce shock).
- ✔ For pneumatic air consumption, remember **absolute pressure** = gauge + **1.013 bar**.
- ✔ Standard pressures: Hydraulic **70–210 bar**, Pneumatic **4–8 bar**.
- ✔ **Exam mantra:** "Convert bar to Pa, mm to m, and L/min to m³/s **before** substituting into any formula."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Actuators: Gear, Vane, and Lobe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rotary Actuators: Gear, Vane, and Lobe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Rotary Actuator?

A **rotary actuator** is a power-conversion device that transforms a fluid (hydraulic or pneumatic) energy supply into **rotational mechanical motion** (torque about a shaft) rather than linear motion. Unlike a linear cylinder that pushes or pulls, a rotary actuator spins its output shaft through a limited angular displacement (typically up to **270°** or **360°** depending on construction) or continuously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEAET895 Module 3):**
> "Rotary actuators convert fluid power into rotary motion. They include gear, vane, and lobe-type actuators, and are widely used in indexing, clamping, swinging, and valve-actuation applications."

### 1.2 Classification of Rotary Actuators

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAET895, Module 3) classifies rotary actuators into three principal families:

| S.No. | Type | Motion Range | Typical Torque Class | Defining Element |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Gear Actuator** | Limited (≤ 270°) | High Torque | External/Internal gear meshing |
| 2 | **Vane Actuator** | Limited (≤ 270°) or Continuous | Medium Torque | Sliding vane(s) in slotted rotor |
| 3 | **Lobe Actuator** | Limited (≤ 180°) | Low–Medium Torque | Oscillating lobed piston |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Spinning Sprinkler (Vane Actuator):**
> Think of a lawn sprinkler. Water enters and pushes curved vanes, causing the head to spin. A vane actuator works exactly this way — pressurized fluid strikes a curved vane, generating torque. Stop the water, and rotation halts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Ratchet Wrench (Gear Actuator):**
> Imagine a small gear meshed against a larger fixed internal gear, with a piston pushing the small gear. Each stroke rotates the output shaft a fraction of a turn with enormous torque. This is how a rack-and-pinion or piston-driven gear actuator delivers **heavy-duty torque** in a compact package.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Old Ship's Helm (Lobe Actuator):**
> Picture a ship captain turning a wheel back and forth (oscillating motion) to steer the rudder. A lobe actuator creates this exact back-and-forth rotary motion through lobed pistons rocking in chambers.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following key parameters govern the performance evaluation of rotary actuators (standard across KTU board problems):

- **Pressure ($P$):** Fluid supply pressure, typically **$\mathbf{5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}}$ to $\mathbf{2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}}$** (5 bar to 200 bar) for hydraulics, **$\mathbf{2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}}$ to $\mathbf{1 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}}$** (2 to 10 bar) for pneumatics.
- **Angular Displacement ($\theta$):** In **radians (rad)**, or **degrees (°)**. $360° = 2\pi$ rad.
- **Torque ($T$):** In **N·m**. The fundamental output parameter.
- **Volumetric Displacement ($V_d$):** Volume of fluid required per radian of rotation, in **m³/rad**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Relationship (memorize this for KTU):**
> $$\boxed{T = P \times V_d \times \eta_m}$$
> where $\eta_m$ is the **mechanical efficiency** (typically **0.85 – 0.95**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gear-Type Rotary Actuator

### 2.1.1 Construction & Working Principle

A gear-type rotary actuator uses a **rack-and-pinion** or **piston-with-external-gear** arrangement. Pressurized fluid acts on a piston with an integral **rack (linear gear teeth)**, which engages a **pinion gear** on the output shaft.

**Key Components:**
1. **Piston with Integral Rack** — Reciprocates inside a cylindrical bore.
2. **Pinion Gear** — Mounted on the output shaft; meshes with the rack.
3. **Porting Chambers** — Two chambers (A and B) for forward/reverse motion.
4. **Output Shaft with Pinion** — Delivers rotary motion.
5. **Bearings & Seals** — Support the shaft and prevent leakage.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. Pressurized fluid enters **Port A**.
2. Fluid pushes the rack-piston in one direction.
3. The rack engages and rotates the **pinion gear** on the output shaft.
4. Stroke length $L$ of the piston → **shaft rotation $\theta = L/r$**, where $r$ = pinion radius.
5. Reversing flow to Port B rotates the shaft in the opposite direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A double-rack (pistons on both sides of the pinion) configuration doubles the angular output to nearly **360°** and provides balanced loading.

### 2.1.3 Why Gear Actuators? — Engineering Utility

- **High Torque Density:** Gear reduction amplifies piston force into very high shaft torque.
- **Compact Design:** Delivers large torque in a small envelope.
- **Common Applications:** Conveyor turning, gate valve actuation, robotic joint drives, crane slewing.

## 2.2 Vane-Type Rotary Actuator

### 2.2.1 Construction & Working Principle

The vane actuator consists of a **cylindrical housing** (stator) containing a **rotor** mounted eccentrically or concentrically on the output shaft. **Vaness (blades)** are mounted on the rotor, dividing the housing into **2 to 6 chambers**.

**Key Components:**
1. **Stator (Housing)** — Contains the fluid ports and chambers.
2. **Rotor** — Carries the vanes and is fixed to the output shaft.
3. **Vanes (Blades)** — Slide in radial slots on the rotor; seal against the stator bore.
4. **Inlet/Outlet Ports** — Channel fluid into the expanding/contracting chambers.
5. **Seals** — At vane tips and shaft.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. Pressurized fluid enters one chamber through Port 1.
2. The fluid pushes against the vane surface, generating a **force perpendicular to the vane**.
3. The torque arm is the **radial distance from shaft center to vane tip ($R_2$)** minus the **rotor radius ($R_1$)**.
4. The rotor (and shaft) rotates as long as pressure is maintained.
5. Fluid exhausts from the opposite port (Port 2).
6. The number of vanes × pressure zones determines maximum rotation.

### 2.2.3 Vane Geometry

For a **single-vane** actuator: rotation can be up to **~280°** (limited by port placement).
For a **double-vane** actuator: rotation is limited to **~100°** per side (each vane separates two chambers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Double Vane?**
> Double-vane construction provides **balanced radial loading** on the shaft (no net side load) and **higher torque** because two pressure chambers act simultaneously.

## 2.3 Lobe-Type Rotary Actuator

### 2.3.1 Construction & Working Principle

A lobe actuator uses **oscillating pistons with lobed (cam-like) profiles** that mesh together inside a housing. Pressurized fluid pushes the lobes apart, causing the output shaft to oscillate back and forth (typically **± 90°**, total **180°**).

**Key Components:**
1. **Lobed Pistons (Rotor Lobes)** — Two or more lobed cams mounted on a common shaft.
2. **Housing with Cam Tracks** — Internal cam surfaces guide the lobes.
3. **Porting Passages** — Direct fluid to alternating sides of the lobes.
4. **Output Shaft** — Transmits oscillating motion.
5. **Bearings** — Support radial and thrust loads.

### 2.3.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. Fluid enters one side of the housing, pushing the lobes apart along a **cam-guided path**.
2. As the lobes separate, they rotate the shaft.
3. The fluid is exhausted from the opposite port.
4. Reversing the flow drives the shaft in the opposite direction.
5. The geometry of the cam track determines the **angular stroke** (typically **180°**).

### 2.3.3 Engineering Utility

- **Simple & Robust:** Few moving parts; no complex seals.
- **Limited Stroke:** Well-suited for **oscillating applications** like valve actuation, tool indexing.
- **Lower Cost:** Less precise than vane actuators, but more durable in dirty environments.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examination (Module 3, PEAET895)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = P \cdot A \cdot r \cdot \eta_m$ | Torque from a linear piston via rack-pinion (Gear actuator) | N·m |
| 2 | $\theta = L / r$ | Angular rotation from piston stroke $L$ and pinion radius $r$ | rad |
| 3 | $T = P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) / 2$ | Vane actuator torque (single vane, width $b$) | N·m |
| 4 | $T = P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) \cdot N_v / 2$ | Multi-vane torque ($N_v$ = number of vanes) | N·m |
| 5 | $V_d = b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) / 2$ | Volumetric displacement per chamber (vane) | m³/rad |
| 6 | $T = P \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_m$ | General torque-displacement relationship | N·m |
| 7 | $P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega$ | Output power | W (watts) |
| 8 | $\omega = 2 \pi N / 60$ | Angular velocity from rpm $N$ | rad/s |
| 9 | $\eta_v = Q_{\text{actual}} / Q_{\text{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Piston thrust force (gear actuator) | N |

**Where:**
- $P$ = Supply pressure (Pa)
- $A$ = Piston area (m²)
- $r$ = Pinion radius (m)
- $L$ = Piston stroke (m)
- $b$ = Vane width (m)
- $R_1$ = Rotor radius (m); $R_2$ = Housing inner radius (m)
- $N_v$ = Number of vanes
- $\eta_m$ = Mechanical efficiency
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)
- $N$ = Rotational speed (rpm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of these formulas:**
> - **Gear actuator:** Use Case 1 for design calculations; used in **industrial robots, heavy machinery**.
> - **Vane actuator:** Use Cases 3 & 5 for sizing valve actuators; used in **process control valves, packaging machines**.
> - **Lobe actuator:** Limited stroke applications; used in **oscillating tool drives, valve actuators in oil & gas**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Torque Produced by a Single-Vane Rotary Actuator

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a single-vane rotary actuator with:
- Housing inner radius: $R_2$
- Rotor radius: $R_1$
- Vane axial width: $b$
- Supply pressure: $P$

The vane surface, when exposed to pressure $P$, experiences a distributed pressure load. The force on a differential strip of the vane at radius $r$ and width $dr$ is:

$$dF = P \cdot dA = P \cdot (b \cdot dr)$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The differential torque contribution at radius $r$ is:
$$dT = r \cdot dF = r \cdot P \cdot b \cdot dr$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from $R_1$ to $R_2$ (the vane's radial extent):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{R_1}^{R_2} P \cdot b \cdot r \cdot dr \\
&= P \cdot b \cdot \int_{R_1}^{R_2} r \cdot dr \\
&= P \cdot b \cdot \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{R_1}^{R_2} \\
&= P \cdot b \cdot \left( \frac{R_2^2}{2} - \frac{R_1^2}{2} \right) \\
T &= \frac{P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a **double-vane** actuator, two pressure surfaces act simultaneously, doubling the torque:

$$T = \frac{P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) \cdot 2}{2} = P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)$$

**Step 4:** Generalizing to **$N_v$ vanes** (with the constraint that $N_v \leq 6$ for practical porting):

$$\boxed{T = \frac{P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) \cdot N_v}{2}}$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Verification

> **Sample KTU-style check:**
> Given: $P = 7 \times 10^5$ Pa, $b = 0.05$ m, $R_1 = 0.02$ m, $R_2 = 0.06$ m, $N_v = 2$, $\eta_m = 0.9$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{7 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.06^2 - 0.02^2) \cdot 2}{2} \\
&= \frac{7 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.0036 - 0.0004) \cdot 2}{2} \\
&= 7 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.0032 \\
&= 7 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 112 \text{ N·m (theoretical)} \\
T_{\text{actual}} &= 112 \times 0.9 = 100.8 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque of a Gear-Type (Rack-and-Pinion) Actuator

### 3.2.1 Setup

- Piston diameter: $D$ (m), area: $A = \pi D^2/4$
- Supply pressure: $P$
- Pinion pitch radius: $r$
- Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_m$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Piston thrust force:
$$F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Torque on pinion (force × moment arm):
$$T_{\text{ideal}} = F \cdot r = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot r$$

**Step 3:** Apply mechanical efficiency:
$$\boxed{T = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot r \cdot \eta_m}$$

**Step 4:** Angular rotation from piston stroke $L$:
$$\theta = \frac{L}{r} \text{ (radians)}$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Verification

> **Sample KTU-style check:**
> Given: $D = 0.05$ m, $r = 0.025$ m, $P = 50 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\eta_m = 0.85$, $L = 0.1$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
F &= 50 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 9817.5 \text{ N} \\
T_{\text{ideal}} &= 9817.5 \times 0.025 = 245.4 \text{ N·m} \\
T_{\text{actual}} &= 245.4 \times 0.85 = 208.6 \text{ N·m} \\
\theta &= 0.1 / 0.025 = 4 \text{ rad} = 229.2°
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Displacement of a Vane Actuator

### 3.3.1 Setup

The volume of fluid required to rotate the vane through a differential angle $d\theta$ is the swept area of the vane chamber multiplied by $d\theta$.

**Step 1:** Differential swept area (the annular sector between $R_1$ and $R_2$, width $b$):
$$dV = b \cdot \frac{(R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2} \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 2:** Volumetric displacement per radian:
$$\boxed{V_d = \frac{dV}{d\theta} = \frac{b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the general torque equation:
$$T = P \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_m = P \cdot \frac{b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2} \cdot \eta_m$$

This matches the **single-vane torque formula**, confirming the consistency of the analysis.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Rotary Actuator Types

> [!NOTE]
> **This comparison matrix is a frequent KTU exam question (often 7 marks).**

| Parameter | Gear Actuator | Vane Actuator | Lobe Actuator |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Operating Principle** | Rack-pinion conversion | Pressure on vane surface | Oscillating lobed pistons |
| **Angular Stroke** | Up to ~360° (double rack) | Up to ~280° (single) / ~100° (double) | Up to ~180° |
| **Torque Capacity** | Very High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Speed Capability** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Low |
| **Starting Torque** | Excellent | Good | Good |
| **Radial Loading on Shaft** | Balanced (double rack) | Balanced (double vane) | Balanced |
| **Leakage Risk** | Low (piston seals) | Moderate (vane tip seals) | Low |
| **Service Life** | Long | Moderate (vane wear) | Long |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Typical Application** | Heavy machinery, valve ops | Packaging, robotics, machine tools | Tool indexing, simple valve ops |
| **Stall Tolerance** | Excellent | Poor (vane damage) | Moderate |
| **Compactness** | Moderate | High | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a vane-type and a gear-type rotary actuator. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Vane Actuator | Gear Actuator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Working Principle** | Pressurized fluid acts on a sliding vane to produce torque | Pressurized fluid drives a piston with rack, which rotates a pinion |
| **Stroke** | Limited to ~280° (single vane) | Up to ~360° (double rack) |
| **Torque** | Medium | Very High |
| **Construction** | Rotor with vanes inside a stator | Piston-rack engaging a pinion gear |
| **Application** | Packaging, light valve actuation | Heavy machinery, large valve actuation |

Vane actuators are compact and faster; gear actuators deliver higher torque with greater mechanical advantage.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a lobe-type rotary actuator? Mention its typical applications. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **lobe-type rotary actuator** is a fluid-power device that converts fluid energy into **oscillating rotary motion** through the interaction of lobed pistons with a cam-track housing. The lobes separate as fluid enters, rotating the output shaft through a limited angular stroke (typically **180°**).

**Typical Applications:**
1. **Valve actuation** in oil and gas pipelines (quarter-turn ball/butterfly valves).
2. **Tool indexing** in machining centers.
3. **Conveyor swinging mechanisms** in material handling.
4. **Mixing and stirring** operations requiring oscillatory motion.
5. **Crane and hoist braking systems**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B1(a) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — CHOICE A (14 Marks)

**Question:**
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a **double-vane rotary actuator**. Derive the expression for the **torque** developed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-vane rotary actuator has a rotor radius of **25 mm**, housing inner radius of **75 mm**, and vane axial width of **50 mm**. The supply pressure is **6 bar** and mechanical efficiency is **90%**. Calculate the **(i)** theoretical torque, **(ii)** actual torque, and **(iii)** volumetric displacement per radian. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle:**
A double-vane rotary actuator has **two vanes** rigidly fixed to the rotor (180° apart). The housing is divided into **four chambers**. Pressurized fluid is admitted into two diametrically opposite chambers simultaneously, producing **balanced radial loading** on the shaft.

**Step 1 — Torque per vane (from derived formula):**
$$T_1 = \frac{P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2}$$

**Step 2 — For double-vane, two pressure surfaces act:**
$$\boxed{T = P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}$$

(After efficiency correction: $T = P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) \cdot \eta_m$)

**Step 3 — Schematic block description:**
Two vanes at 180°; four chambers; fluid enters chambers 1 and 3, exhausts from 2 and 4; rotor rotates by the difference in chamber pressures acting on both vanes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $R_1 = 0.025$ m, $R_2 = 0.075$ m, $b = 0.05$ m
- $P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\eta_m = 0.9$

**(i) Theoretical Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.075^2 - 0.025^2)}{2} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.005625 - 0.000625)}{2} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.005}{2} \\
&= \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual Torque:**
$$T_{\text{actual}} = T_{\text{theoretical}} \times \eta_m = 75 \times 0.9 = \mathbf{67.5 \text{ N·m}}$$

**(iii) Volumetric Displacement per radian:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2} \\
&= \frac{0.05 \cdot 0.005}{2} \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $T_{\text{theoretical}} = 75$ N·m
- $T_{\text{actual}} = 67.5$ N·m
- $V_d = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/rad

---

### Question B1(b) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — CHOICE B (14 Marks)

**Question:**
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **gear-type rotary actuator** with a neat sketch. List its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gear-type rotary actuator has a piston of diameter **80 mm**, pinion pitch radius of **40 mm**, and operates at a supply pressure of **100 bar**. If the mechanical efficiency is **88%**, determine the **(i)** thrust force, **(ii)** torque developed, and **(iii)** angular rotation for a piston stroke of **120 mm**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**
A gear-type rotary actuator consists of:
1. **Cylindrical housing** with two ports.
2. **Piston with integral rack teeth** (single or double).
3. **Pinion gear** mounted on the output shaft.
4. **Bearings, seals, and shaft assembly**.

**Working:**
Pressurized fluid enters one port and pushes the rack-piston linearly. The rack teeth engage the pinion, converting linear motion into rotary motion. The shaft rotates through a stroke-limited angle determined by piston travel and pinion radius.

**Advantages:**
- Very high torque output
- Compact and rugged
- Excellent starting torque
- Good for heavy-duty applications
- Double-rack provides 360° rotation

**Disadvantages:**
- Limited speed
- Higher cost
- Piston seals wear
- Requires precise rack-pinion alignment

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 0.08$ m, $r = 0.04$ m, $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\eta_m = 0.88$, $L = 0.12$ m

**(i) Thrust Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
F &= P \cdot A = 100 \times 10^5 \cdot 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= \mathbf{50{,}265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Torque Developed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= F \cdot r \cdot \eta_m \\
&= 50{,}265 \times 0.04 \times 0.88 \\
&= \mathbf{1769.3 \text{ N·m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Angular Rotation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{L}{r} = \frac{0.12}{0.04} = 3 \text{ rad} \\
\theta_{\text{deg}} &= 3 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \mathbf{171.9°}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $F = 50.27$ kN
- $T = 1769.3$ N·m
- $\theta = 3$ rad $= 171.9°$

---

### Question B1(c) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` — CHOICE C (14 Marks)

**Question:**
**(a)** Compare **gear, vane, and lobe** rotary actuators in terms of working principle, torque capacity, angular stroke, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **lobe-type rotary actuator** operates at a pressure of **80 bar** with a cam lobe area of **30 cm²** and effective moment arm of **50 mm**. Calculate the **(i)** force on the lobe and **(ii)** torque developed, assuming $\eta_m = 0.85$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Feature | Gear Actuator | Vane Actuator | Lobe Actuator |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Principle** | Rack-pinion conversion | Pressure on vane surface | Oscillating lobe in cam track |
| **Torque** | Very High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Stroke** | ~360° | ~280° (single) / ~100° (double) | ~180° |
| **Speed** | Low | Medium–High | Low |
| **Applications** | Heavy machinery, valves | Packaging, light robotics | Oscillating tools, indexing |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $P = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $A = 30 \text{ cm}^2 = 30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- $r_{\text{eff}} = 0.05$ m
- $\eta_m = 0.85$

**(i) Force on Lobe:**
$$F = P \cdot A = 80 \times 10^5 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{24{,}000 \text{ N} = 24 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Torque Developed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= F \cdot r_{\text{eff}} \cdot \eta_m \\
&= 24{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.85 \\
&= \mathbf{1020 \text{ N·m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $F = 24$ kN
- $T = 1020$ N·m

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert **bar to Pa** (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa). This single mistake costs **2–3 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing Rotor vs Housing Radius:** In vane actuator problems, $R_1$ is the **rotor** (shaft) radius and $R_2$ is the **housing** inner radius. Reversing them gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Forgetting Mechanical Efficiency:** The formula $T = P \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_m$ includes $\eta_m$. Leaving it out means you compute theoretical, not actual, torque.
> 4. **Stroke Confusion:** Single-vane actuators give up to **~280°**; double-vane only **~100°**. Marking schemes penalize wrong stroke values.
> 5. **No Sketches:** For 7-mark descriptive questions, **a labeled diagram is mandatory**. Skipping it loses **1–2 marks** even if the explanation is correct.
> 6. **Radian-Degree Mix-up:** Angular velocity $\omega$ is in rad/s; rotational speed $N$ is in rpm. Use $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3, PEAET895)**

### Core Concepts
- A **rotary actuator** converts fluid energy into **rotary (torque-based) motion** instead of linear motion.
- The **three principal types** are: **Gear, Vane, and Lobe**.

### Gear Actuator
- Uses **rack-and-pinion** mechanism; high torque; up to **360°** rotation (double-rack).
- Key formula: $T = P \cdot A \cdot r \cdot \eta_m$ where $A = \pi D^2/4$.
- $\theta = L/r$ relates linear stroke to angular rotation.
- Used in **heavy machinery, large valve actuation**.

### Vane Actuator
- Uses **sliding vanes** in a slotted rotor; pressure acts on the vane surface.
- Torque formula: $T = P \cdot b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) \cdot N_v / 2$.
- Volumetric displacement: $V_d = b \cdot (R_2^2 - R_1^2) / 2$.
- **Single-vane** = up to ~280°; **Double-vane** = ~100° per side, balanced loading.
- Used in **packaging machines, light robotics, small valve actuation**.

### Lobe Actuator
- Uses **oscillating lobed pistons** guided by cam tracks.
- Limited to **~180°** oscillatory motion; lower torque.
- Simple, robust, low cost; used in **tool indexing, simple valve ops**.

### Universal Equations
- $T = P \cdot V_d \cdot \eta_m$ — applies to all fluid-power actuators.
- $P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega$ — output power.
- $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ — rad/s from rpm.

### Conversion Reminders
- 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa
- $360° = 2\pi$ rad
- $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3}$ m

### Exam Strategy
- Always draw a **labeled block diagram** for 7-mark descriptive questions.
- Always convert all units to **SI (Pa, m, N, N·m)** before substituting.
- Always state the **assumptions** (e.g., "neglecting leakage, $\eta_m = 0.9$").
- Memorize the **comparison matrix** — it's a high-probability 7-mark question.

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- Confusing theoretical and actual torque (with/without $\eta_m$).
- Reversing $R_1$ and $R_2$ in vane actuator problems.
- Forgetting to include the **vane count $N_v$** for multi-vane actuators.
- Computing **force instead of torque** or vice versa.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison Between Electrical, Pneumatic, and Hydraulic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Comparison Between Electrical, Pneumatic, and Hydraulic Actuators # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Mechatronic systems, an **Actuator** is a device that converts a control signal (electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic energy) into a physical mechanical motion (linear or rotary) to perform useful work on a load. The choice of actuator fundamentally determines the performance, precision, cost, and safety envelope of any automated system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An actuator is the "muscle" of a mechatronic system. It takes the processed signal from the controller (PLC/Microcontroller) and the regulated energy from the power supply and converts them into a precisely controlled mechanical action. The three primary classes of actuators studied under KTU Module 3 are: 1. **Electrical Actuators** — Convert electrical energy (DC/AC) into mechanical motion. Examples: DC/AC Servo Motors, Stepper Motors, Solenoids, Piezoelectric actuators. 2. **Pneumatic Actuators** — Convert compressed **air** energy (typically **6 bar** working pressure) into mechanical motion. Examples: Single/Double-acting cylinders, Rotary vane actuators, Air motors. 3. **Hydraulic Actuators** — Convert pressurized **incompressible fluid** (typically mineral oil at **70–350 bar**) into mechanical motion. Examples: Hydraulic cylinders, Hydraulic motors, Vane-type rotary actuators. ## 1.2 The Core Operational Principle — The "Energy Conversion Chain" Every mechatronic actuation subsystem follows a **three-stage energy conversion chain**: $$ \text{Source Energy} \xrightarrow{\text{Converter / Power Modulator}} \text{Pressurized Medium (Air/Oil/Electricity)} \xrightarrow{\text{Actuator}} \text{Mechanical Work} $$ In **pneumatic and hydraulic systems**, energy is transmitted via a *fluid medium*, while in **electrical systems**, energy is transmitted via *electrons in a conductor*. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Three Types of Workers** > - **Electrical Actuator** = The *Precision Surgeon*. Fast, accurate, clean, requires well-controlled DC/AC power. Cannot lift huge weights but positions a needle perfectly. (Example: Robotic arm in a watch factory). > - **Pneumatic Actuator** = The *Strong Construction Worker with a Jackhammer*. Uses compressed air to deliver rapid, repetitive, linear blows. Cheap, fast, and safe (no fire risk), but not super precise. (Example: Pick-and-place in bottling plant). > - **Hydraulic Actuator** = The *Heavy-Lift Crane Operator*. Uses pressurized oil to lift enormous loads (hundreds of tonnes) with smooth, powerful motion. Slow and bulky, but unmatched in force density. (Example: Excavator arm, aircraft landing gear). ## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics at a Glance - **Air Compressibility Index (γ):** Air at 1 bar can be compressed to ~**1/8th** its volume at 7 bar. - **Oil Bulk Modulus:** ~**1.5–2.0 GPa** (effectively incompressible). - **Standard Pneumatic Pressure:** **6 bar (87 PSI)**. - **Standard Hydraulic Pressure:** **70–210 bar (1000–3000 PSI)**. - **Power-to-Weight Ratio of Servo Motors:** ~**3–5 kW/kg** for rare-earth permanent magnet types. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Operating Principles ### 2.1.1 Electrical Actuators - Operate on **electromagnetic principles** governed by the Lorentz force law. - DC motors generate torque proportional to armature current; AC induction motors operate on rotating magnetic fields. - Stepper motors achieve *open-loop* position control by energizing stator phases in a specific sequence. - Servo motors combine a feedback encoder + closed-loop PID controller for high-precision position/velocity control. ### 2.1.2 Pneumatic Actuators - **Compressible medium** (air) is stored in a receiver tank and fed through a **FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator). - A **directional control valve (DCV)** routes the air into the cylinder's blank-end or rod-end, producing reciprocating linear motion. - Speed is controlled via **flow control valves**; force is controlled via **pressure regulators**. - Air is *exhausted* to atmosphere after use — making it inherently *clean* in terms of fluid leakage. ### 2.1.3 Hydraulic Actuators - **Incompressible oil** is pressurized by a pump (gear/vane/piston) and routed via servo/proportional valves. - The high bulk modulus of oil provides **excellent stiffness and damping**, ideal for precision control at high loads. - Requires a **reservoir, return line filter, heat exchanger, and accumulator** — making the system more complex. - Sealing leakage is a key challenge (contamination + fire hazard with synthetic oils). ## 2.2 Comparative Operating Logic — Why the Three Behave Differently | Parameter | Electrical | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | |---|---|---|---| | **Energy Medium** | Electrons (no fluid) | Compressed Air (compressible gas) | Pressurized Oil (incompressible fluid) | | **Primary Conversion Law** | Lorentz: $F = BIL$ | Pascal + Air Expansion | Pascal's Law (exact) | | **Stiffness at Load** | Moderate (encoder feedback compensates) | Low (air cushion compresses) | Very High (oil incompressible) | | **Force Density (kN/kg)** | 0.5 – 2 | 1 – 3 | 5 – 25 | | **Typical Power Range** | **1 W – 500 kW** | **0.1 kW – 25 kW** | **1 kW – 250 kW** | | **Noise Level (dB)** | **40 – 60 dB** (low) | **70 – 100 dB** (high) | **60 – 80 dB** (moderate) | | **Fire/Explosion Safety** | Safe (intrinsically safe options) | **Very Safe** (no flammable fluid) | **Hazardous** (oil mist ignitable) | | **Cleanliness (Food/Pharma)** | Excellent | Good (exhausts to air) | Poor (oil leak risk) | | **Control Bandwidth (Hz)** | **50 – 1000 Hz** | **1 – 20 Hz** | **5 – 100 Hz** | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are the *high-yield* formulas frequently tested in KTU University Exam problems on actuator selection. $$ \boxed{\;F_{\text{hyd}} = P \cdot A_{\text{piston}} = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;F_{\text{pneu}} = P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot \eta_{m} \quad \text{where } A_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;P_{\text{motor}} = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;P_{\text{hyd}} = \frac{Q \cdot p}{60} \quad [\text{kW}], \;\; Q[\text{LPM}], \;\; p[\text{bar}]\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;P_{\text{pneu}} = p \cdot Q_{v} \quad [\text{Watts}], \;\; p[\text{Pa}], \;\; Q_{v}[\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}]\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;v_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}} \quad [\text{m/s}]\;} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{pump}} \cdot \eta_{\text{valve}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}}\;} $$ | Formula | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---| | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ [N], $P$ [Pa], $A$ [m²] | Cylinder force calculation | | $P = T\omega$ | $P$ [W], $T$ [Nm], $\omega$ [rad/s] | Motor power | | $P = pQ/60$ | $P$ [kW], $p$ [bar], $Q$ [LPM] | Hydraulic power | | $v = Q/A$ | $v$ [m/s], $Q$ [m³/s], $A$ [m²] | Cylinder piston speed | | $\tau = J \cdot \alpha$ | $\tau$ [Nm], $J$ [kg·m²], $\alpha$ [rad/s²] | Motor torque acceleration | > [!NOTE] > **Critical distinction for KTU:** In pneumatic systems, the *effective area* is reduced on the rod side by the cross-section of the piston rod, producing a different force in extension vs. retraction. Hydraulic systems often assume equal areas (double-rod cylinder) when force balance is critical. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Robotics:** Electrical servo drives (e.g., 6-axis articulated arms in automotive welding lines). - **Injection Molding:** Hydraulic actuators for the high clamping force (5000+ tonnes) required. - **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Pneumatic actuators in cleanroom pick-and-place handlers (no oil contamination). - **Aircraft Flight Control:** Hydraulic actuators for primary control surfaces (ailerons, rudders) due to power density and reliability. - **CNC Machine Tools:** Hybrid — electrical spindles + hydraulic tool changers. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Force Output of a Hydraulic Cylinder **Given:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D = 100$ mm and rod diameter $d = 60$ mm, supplied with $p = 100$ bar pressurized oil. Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.92$. **Find:** (a) Extension force, (b) Retraction force, (c) Piston velocity at flow $Q = 60$ LPM. ### Step 1 — Convert Pressure to SI Units Hydraulic pressure in KTU problems is commonly given in **bar**. Since $1\ \text{bar} = 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$: $$ \begin{aligned} p &= 100\ \text{bar} \\ &= 100 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning:** SI base unit for pressure is Pascal (N/m²); conversion ensures unit consistency with force and area in SI. ### Step 2 — Compute the Blank-End (Piston) Area $A_1$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_1 &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi (0.100)^{2}}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 0.01}{4} \\ &= 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2} \\ &\approx 78.54\ \text{cm}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning:** The full circular area is exposed to pressure on the extension stroke (blank end). ### Step 3 — Compute the Rod-End Area $A_2$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_2 &= \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi ((0.100)^{2} - (0.060)^{2})}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi (0.0100 - 0.0036)}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} \\ &= 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2} \\ &\approx 50.27\ \text{cm}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning:** On the rod side, the piston rod occupies the central region, reducing the effective pressure-bearing area. ### Step 4 — Compute the Theoretical Extension Force $F_{\text{ext}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{ext,th}} &= p \cdot A_1 \\ &= (1.0 \times 10^{7}) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 78{,}540\ \text{N} \\ &\approx 78.54\ \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Apply Mechanical Efficiency to Get Actual Force $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{ext,act}} &= F_{\text{ext,th}} \times \eta_m \\ &= 78{,}540 \times 0.92 \\ &= 72{,}257\ \text{N} \\ &\approx 72.26\ \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning:** Efficiency accounts for friction at piston seals, rod bearings, and fluid shear losses. ### Step 6 — Compute Theoretical Retraction Force $F_{\text{ret}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{ret,th}} &= p \cdot A_2 \\ &= (1.0 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 50{,}270\ \text{N} \\ &\approx 50.27\ \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{ret,act}} &= 50{,}270 \times 0.92 \\ &= 46{,}248\ \text{N} \\ &\approx 46.25\ \text{kN} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Compute Piston Velocity at $Q = 60$ LPM Convert flow to SI: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= 60\ \text{LPM} \\ &= \frac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{60}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \\ &= 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \end{aligned} $$ Extension velocity: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{\text{ext}} &= \frac{Q}{A_1} \\ &= \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.1273\ \text{m/s} \\ &\approx 12.73\ \text{cm/s} \end{aligned} $$ Retraction velocity (faster, as $A_2 < A_1$): $$ \begin{aligned} v_{\text{ret}} &= \frac{Q}{A_2} \\ &= \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.1989\ \text{m/s} \\ &\approx 19.89\ \text{cm/s} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Master Comparison Table > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important** study artifact for answering KTU Part A and Part B questions on actuator comparison. **Memorize the trade-off logic**, not just the numbers. | Comparison Criterion | Electrical Actuator | Pneumatic Actuator | Hydraulic Actuator | |---|---|---|---| | **Energy Source** | Electrical power (AC/DC bus) | Compressed air (6–8 bar) | Pressurized oil (70–350 bar) | | **Energy Transmission** | Conductors/wires | Compressible air | Incompressible fluid (hose/piping) | | **Working Fluid** | None (solid-state) | Atmospheric air | Mineral/Synthetic oil | | **Theoretical Force Equation** | $F = BIL$ (linear) / $T = k_t I$ (rotary) | $F = pA$ | $F = pA$ | | **Power Equation** | $P = VI = T\omega$ | $P = pQ$ | $P = pQ$ | | **Force/Torque Density** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | Very High | | **Speed Range** | Wide (1 RPM – 30,000 RPM) | High linear (up to 2 m/s) | Moderate linear (up to 0.5 m/s) | | **Position Accuracy** | Excellent ($\pm 0.01$ mm) | Poor ($\pm 1$ mm without servo) | Good ($\pm 0.1$ mm) | | **Repeatability** | $\pm 0.005$ mm | $\pm 0.5$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm | | **Starting Torque** | DC: High; AC: Limited | N/A (linear) | Very High | | **Reversibility** | Excellent (rapid) | Good (DCV switching) | Excellent (valve spool) | | **Overload Capability** | Poor (motor burn-out risk) | Excellent (air cushion protects) | Excellent | | **Self-Lubrication** | Yes (sealed bearings) | No (needs FRL) | Yes (oil) | | **Fire/Explosion Hazard** | Low | None | High (synthetic oil) | | **Cleanroom Compatibility** | Excellent | Good (oil-free) | Poor | | **Noise Generation** | 40–60 dB | 70–100 dB (with silencer: 60 dB) | 60–80 dB | | **Initial Cost** | High (driver + encoder) | Low (FRL + DCV) | Very High (pump + tank + valves) | | **Operating Cost** | Moderate (electricity) | High (compressed air is expensive) | Moderate (oil change) | | **Maintenance** | Low (bearings) | Low–Moderate (seal wear) | High (filters, seals, oil) | | **Typical Applications** | Robotics, CNC, printers | Packaging, food, pick-place | Presses, excavators, aircraft | | **Temperature Range** | $-40$°C to $+150$°C (motor dependent) | $-20$°C to $+80$°C (seals) | $-10$°C to $+90$°C (oil) | | **Life Expectancy** | 20,000 h (brush) / 50,000 h (brushless) | 5–10 million cycles | 10–20 million cycles | | **Energy Efficiency** | 70–95% | 10–30% (poor — air heating) | 60–80% | ## 3.3 Engineering Selection Logic (Decision Flow) The choice is governed by the **application envelope**: 1. **Is precision < 0.1 mm required?** → **Electrical Servo**. 2. **Is force > 50 kN required?** → **Hydraulic**. 3. **Is the environment explosive (mining, ATEX zones)?** → **Pneumatic** (or intrinsically safe electrical). 4. **Is cost the primary constraint and motion is simple ON/OFF?** → **Pneumatic**. 5. **Is the load position variable over long travels with high stiffness?** → **Hydraulic Servo** (e.g., injection molding). 6. **Is cleanroom/food-grade compliance mandatory?** → **Pneumatic (oil-free)** or **Electrical**. --- # 4.
