The *power modulator* stage (valve, inverter, chopper) is the critical interface between the controller command and the actuator's mechanical output. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** *Define an actuator. List any four applications of pneumatic actuators in industry.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** An actuator is a device that converts a control signal along with a regulated source of energy into a physical mechanical motion (linear or rotary) to perform work on a load. **Applications of Pneumatic Actuators (2 marks — any four):** 1. Pick-and-place mechanisms in packaging industries. 2. Clamping and tool-changing in CNC and assembly lines. 3. Bottle-filling and capping machines in food/beverage industry. 4. Drilling, riveting, and grinding tools in aircraft manufacturing. 5. Door-opening systems in automated mass-transit buses and trains. 6. Pneumatic press machines for forming and stamping operations. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]** *State any three advantages of hydraulic actuators over pneumatic actuators.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Higher force/torque density:** Hydraulic systems operate at 100–350 bar versus only 6 bar for pneumatics, producing forces **10–50 times greater** for the same actuator size. 2. **Stiffer and more precise:** Hydraulic oil is nearly incompressible (bulk modulus ~**1.7 GPa**), providing excellent positional stiffness and accuracy (typically **±0.1 mm** versus ±1 mm for pneumatics). 3. **Higher energy efficiency:** Hydraulic transmission retains **60–80%** of input energy, whereas pneumatic systems waste 70–90% of energy as heat due to air expansion and exhaust losses. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice A **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Compare electrical, pneumatic, and hydraulic actuators on the basis of any **seven** parameters. State one typical application of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of **80 mm** and a rod diameter of **45 mm**. The pump supplies oil at a pressure of **120 bar** with a flow rate of **40 LPM**. Assuming a mechanical efficiency of **90%**, calculate: (i) The effective extension force (ii) The effective retraction force (iii) The extension and retraction velocities. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per row of meaningful comparison) | Parameter | Electrical | Pneumatic | Hydraulic | |---|---|---|---| | **Energy source** | AC/DC electrical | Compressed air (6–8 bar) | Pressurized oil (70–350 bar) | | **Force/Power density** | Moderate | Low | Very High | | **Position accuracy** | Excellent (≤ 0.01 mm) | Poor (≥ 1 mm) | Good (~ 0.1 mm) | | **Speed** | High (up to 30,000 RPM) | Very high linear (up to 2 m/s) | Moderate (up to 0.5 m/s) | | **Cleanliness** | Excellent | Good (oil-free available) | Poor (oil leak risk) | | **Fire/explosion safety** | Generally safe | Very safe | Hazardous | | **Cost** | High initial, low running | Low initial, high running | Very high initial, moderate running | **Typical Applications (1 mark for the set):** - **Electrical:** Robotic arm in automotive welding. - **Pneumatic:** Pick-and-place in bottling plants. - **Hydraulic:** Excavator boom actuation. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given:** - Piston diameter: $D = 80\ \text{mm} = 0.080\ \text{m}$ - Rod diameter: $d = 45\ \text{mm} = 0.045\ \text{m}$ - Pressure: $p = 120\ \text{bar} = 120 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} = 1.2 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}$ - Flow rate: $Q = 40\ \text{LPM} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ - Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_m = 0.90$ **Step 1: Compute the piston (blank-end) area $A_1$** (1 mark) $$ \begin{aligned} A_1 &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} \\ &= 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute the rod-end area $A_2$** (1 mark) $$ \begin{aligned} A_2 &= \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi ((0.080)^{2} - (0.045)^{2})}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.002025)}{4} \\ &= 3.435 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute the effective extension force** (1.5 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{ext}} &= p \cdot A_1 \cdot \eta_m \\ &= (1.2 \times 10^{7}) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.90 \\ &= 60{,}324 \times 0.90 \\ &= 54{,}291.6\ \text{N} \\ &\approx \mathbf{54.29\ \text{kN}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute the effective retraction force** (1.5 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{ret}} &= p \cdot A_2 \cdot \eta_m \\ &= (1.2 \times 10^{7}) \times (3.435 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.90 \\ &= 41{,}220 \times 0.90 \\ &= 37{,}098\ \text{N} \\ &\approx \mathbf{37.10\ \text{kN}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute extension velocity** (1 mark) $$ \begin{aligned} v_{\text{ext}} &= \frac{Q}{A_1} \\ &= \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.1326\ \text{m/s} \\ &\approx \mathbf{13.26\ \text{cm/s}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Compute retraction velocity** (1 mark) $$ \begin{aligned} v_{\text{ret}} &= \frac{Q}{A_2} \\ &= \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{3.435 \times 10^{-3}} \\ &= 0.1941\ \text{m/s} \\ &\approx \mathbf{19.41\ \text{cm/s}} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Extension Force | **54.29 kN** | | Retraction Force | **37.10 kN** | | Extension Velocity | **0.133 m/s** | | Retraction Velocity | **0.194 m/s** | --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice B (Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **construction and working** of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with a neat block diagram. List its **three** main advantages over a single-acting cylinder. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pneumatic cylinder has a piston diameter of **50 mm** and a rod diameter of **20 mm**. The supply pressure is **6 bar**, and the cylinder extends a load of mass **40 kg** against gravity over a stroke of **200 mm** in **1.2 seconds**. Calculate: (i) The theoretical extension force. (ii) The actual work done against gravity. (iii) The average power developed. (iv) Comment on the energy efficiency if the input pneumatic power is **180 W**. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder (7 marks) **Construction (3 marks):** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder consists of: - **Cylinder barrel** (precision-honed steel tube). - **Piston** (sealed with polymer O-rings + backup rings). - **Piston rod** (hard-chromed steel, attached to the piston, exits through the gland). - **End caps** (blank end and rod end) with port connections. - **Seals:** O-rings, rod wiper, and static seals to prevent leakage. - **Cushioning bushings** (optional) for deceleration near stroke ends. **Working Principle (2 marks):** Pressurized oil from a directional control valve enters either the **blank-end port** (extension stroke) or the **rod-end port** (retraction stroke). The pressure acts on the differential piston area, generating a linear force. The displaced oil from the opposite side returns to the tank via the return line. A bleed valve and filter are included for air removal and contamination control. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Pump] --> nodeB[Pressure Relief Valve] nodeB --> nodeC[Directional Control Valve] nodeC -->|Pressurized oil to blank end| nodeD[Extension Stroke] nodeC -->|Pressurized oil to rod end| nodeE[Retraction Stroke] nodeD --> nodeF[Piston Rod and Load] nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG[Return Line to Tank] ``` **Three Advantages over Single-Acting Cylinder (1 mark):** 1. **Force in both directions** — generates thrust in both extension and retraction strokes, whereas single-acting depends on spring/load return. 2. **Better control** — speed and force are independently controllable in both directions. 3. **Higher load capacity and rigidity** — no spring deflection means stiffer response and higher energy efficiency. --- #### Part (b) — Pneumatic Cylinder Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** - $D = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.050\ \text{m}$ - $d = 20\ \text{mm} = 0.020\ \text{m}$ - $p = 6\ \text{bar} = 6 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$ - Mass $m = 40\ \text{kg}$, stroke $L = 200\ \text{mm} = 0.200\ \text{m}$, time $t = 1.2\ \text{s}$ - $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$ **Step 1: Compute the blank-end area $A_1$** (0.5 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} A_1 &= \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \\ &= \frac{\pi (0.050)^{2}}{4} \\ &= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute theoretical extension force** (1.5 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{th}} &= p \cdot A_1 \\ &= (6 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) \\ &= 1178.1\ \text{N} \\ &\approx \mathbf{1.178\ \text{kN}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute actual work done against gravity** (1.5 marks) Force required to lift the load vertically: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{load}} &= m \cdot g \\ &= 40 \times 9.81 \\ &= 392.4\ \text{N} \end{aligned} $$ Work done: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{out}} &= F_{\text{load}} \cdot L \\ &= 392.4 \times 0.200 \\ &= 78.48\ \text{J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute average power developed** (1.5 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{out}} &= \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{t} \\ &= \frac{78.48}{1.2} \\ &= 65.4\ \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Compute efficiency and comment** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{65.4}{180} \times 100\% \\ &= 36.33\% \end{aligned} $$ **Comment (1 mark):** The efficiency is **~36%**, which is *typical for pneumatic systems* (10–40% range). The remaining ~64% is lost primarily as heat from air expansion, exhaust throttling losses, and friction in seals. This illustrates the **inherent energy inefficiency of pneumatic actuators** — a key disadvantage compared to electrical and hydraulic systems. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Places Students Lose Marks on Actuator Comparison Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion of pressure** from bar to Pa (or vice versa). A student writing $F = 120 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}$ without bar-to-Pa conversion loses 1 mark instantly. > 2. **Confusing extension and retraction areas.** Always verify whether the rod-side or blank-side is pressurized. > 3. **Omitting the mechanical efficiency** when the problem explicitly states $\eta_m$. This is a free 0.5 mark many students miss. > 4. **Writing the wrong velocity formula.** $v = Q/A$ uses SI flow (m³/s) and SI area (m²); failing to convert LPM leads to off-by-60 errors. > 5. **Listing advantages without quantification** in Part A. Statements like *"hydraulic is more powerful"* without numbers (e.g., *"operates at 100–350 bar vs. 6 bar for pneumatics"*) are weak — KTU examiners reward numerical substantiation. > 6. **In Part B, mixing up the efficiency factor location.** Efficiency is applied to the *force output* OR the *input power* — NOT both. Pick one consistent convention. > 7. **Not labeling Mermaid/block diagrams with port names and flow direction** — examiners expect a *schematic*, not a generic block. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Actuator Comparison** - ✅ **Three classes of actuators:** Electrical, Pneumatic, Hydraulic — each with distinct energy medium, pressure level, force density, and accuracy. - ✅ **Pressure benchmark values (commit to memory):** - Pneumatic: **6 bar** (max ~10 bar for industrial) - Hydraulic: **70–350 bar** - Electrical: No "pressure" parameter — use *voltage* and *current*. - ✅ **Core formulas (must know cold):** - $F = P \cdot A$ (linear actuator force) - $A_{\text{blank}} = \pi D^{2}/4$ - $A_{\text{rod}} = \pi (D^{2} - d^{2})/4$ - $v = Q/A$ (piston velocity) - $P_{\text{motor}} = T\omega$ and $P_{\text{fluid}} = pQ$ - ✅ **Selection heuristic (memorize the trade-offs):** - High force → Hydraulic - High precision → Electrical - Low cost, simple ON/OFF → Pneumatic - Explosive / clean environment → Pneumatic - Continuous heavy duty → Hydraulic Servo - ✅ **Efficiency ranking (best to worst):** Electrical (70–95%) > Hydraulic (60–80%) >> Pneumatic (10–30%). - ✅ **Speed ranking (fastest linear motion):** Pneumatic > Electrical > Hydraulic. - ✅ **Compressibility behavior:** Air is compressible (10–30% efficient due to expansion losses) → poor stiffness. Oil is incompressible → high stiffness. - ✅ **Typical applications to remember:** - Electrical → CNC, robotics, printers, hard drives. - Pneumatic → bottling, packaging, food handling, mining. - Hydraulic → excavators, aircraft controls, injection molding, press brakes. - ✅ **Pneumatic system components (FRL + DCV):** Filter-Regulator-Lubricator + Directional Control Valve. - ✅ **Hydraulic system components:** Reservoir, pump, relief valve, filter, accumulator, heat exchanger, servo valve. - ✅ **KTU favorite numerical problems:** Cylinder force/velocity calculations; motor power from torque-speed; efficiency comparisons; selection justification questions. - ✅ **Key physical constants to memorize:** $1\ \text{bar} = 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$, $P_{\text{hyd}} = pQ/60$ (with $Q$ in LPM, $p$ in bar, $P$ in kW), $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Comparison of Electrical, Pneumatic & Hydraulic Actuators** ---

topics.title: Mechanical Power Transmission Systems: Spur Gears, Bevel Gear, Planetary Gear, Belts and Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET895 - MECHATRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Power Transmission Systems: Spur Gears, Bevel Gear, Planetary Gear, Belts and Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Mechanical Power Transmission Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Mechanical Power Transmission System** is an assembly of mechanical elements (gears, belts, chains, shafts, couplings, bearings, clutches, and brakes) used to transmit power/torque from the **prime mover (driver)** to the **driven machine (load)** while modifying the **speed, torque, or direction** of rotation as required by the application.

In mechatronics, mechanical transmission is the *actuation layer* between the controller (electrical/electronic) and the physical load. When a motor (the *driver*) is given a command by the microcontroller, the transmission system shapes the mechanical output.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Real-World Analogy"

Imagine a **bicycle**:

- The **pedal gear (chainring)** and the **rear sprocket** form a *gear + chain* transmission.
- When you shift gears, you change the **gear ratio** — small chainring + large sprocket → easier to pedal but slower (high torque, low speed).
- The **chain** transmits power between the two sprockets over a distance.

Similarly, a **car gearbox** uses spur/helical/bevel gears and a chain/belt to deliver torque to the wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in Mechatronics?**
> Because most electric motors in robotics/CNC run at **1500–3000 RPM**, while the *output* (robot joint, conveyor, robot wheel) usually needs **10–500 RPM** and **high torque**. Transmission systems achieve this **speed reduction + torque multiplication**.

---

## 1.3 The Five Building Blocks (As Per KTU 2024 Module-3 Syllabus)

| # | Element | Function | Key Application |
|---|---------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Spur Gear** | Transmits power between **parallel** shafts. | Gearboxes, printers, robotics joints. |
| 2 | **Bevel Gear** | Transmits power between **intersecting** shafts (typically 90°). | Differential in cars, hand drills. |
| 3 | **Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear** | Compact, coaxial gear train with sun, planet, and ring gears. | Automatic transmissions, servo reducers. |
| 4 | **Belt Drive** | Flexible friction drive for **long center distances**. | Conveyors, fans, washing machines. |
| 5 | **Chain Drive** | Positive (no-slip) drive for **large distances & heavy loads**. | Motorcycles, bicycles, industrial machinery. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — "Mechanical Actuation":** Transmission elements convert the *rotational input* of the motor into the *mechanical motion* required by the mechatronic load. They form the *Musculo-Skeletal system* of any mechatronic product.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 SPUR GEARS — Operating Principle

A **spur gear** is a cylindrical gear with teeth *parallel to the axis of rotation*. It transmits motion between two parallel, coplanar shafts by positive engagement (no slip).

### 2.1.1 Geometric Terms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Geometry (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **Module (m):** The ratio of pitch circle diameter to number of teeth. *m = d / Z*
> - **Pitch Circle Diameter (d):** The imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll.
> - **Circular Pitch (p):** The arc length along the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth. *p = πm*
> - **Addendum (a):** Radial distance from pitch circle to tooth tip = **1 module (1m)**.
> - **Dedendum (b):** Radial distance from pitch circle to tooth root = **1.25 m**.
> - **Pressure Angle (φ):** Standard values are **20°** (full-depth) and **14.5°**.

### 2.1.2 Fundamental Relations

$$d = m \cdot Z \quad \text{(Pitch diameter in mm)}$$

$$p = \pi \cdot m \quad \text{(Circular pitch in mm)}$$

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(Linear pitch velocity in m/s)}$$

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \frac{d_2}{d_1} \quad \text{(Speed / Velocity ratio)}$$

### 2.1.3 Centre Distance & Length of Path of Contact

$$a = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{m(Z_1 + Z_2)}{2}$$

**Length of path of contact:**

$$L_{PC} = \sqrt{R_a^2 - R_b^2} + \frac{R_b}{\sin\phi} - R_a \sin\phi$$

where $R_a$ = addendum circle radius of pinion, $R_b$ = addendum circle radius of gear.

---

## 2.2 BEVEL GEARS — Operating Principle

A **bevel gear** transmits motion between two shafts whose axes **intersect** (most commonly at **90°**). They are essentially *cones* with teeth cut on their lateral surfaces.

### 2.2.1 Pitch Cone Angles

> [!NOTE]
> For shafts intersecting at 90°:
> - Pitch cone angle of pinion: $\gamma = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{Z_1}{Z_2}\right)$
> - Pitch cone angle of gear: $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1}\right)$
> - Constraint: $\gamma + \alpha = 90°$

### 2.2.2 Equivalent Pitch Radius (For Lewis / Beam Strength Calculations)

$$R_{e1} = R_1 - b \sin\gamma \quad ; \quad R_{e2} = R_2 - b \sin\alpha$$

where $b$ is the face width, and $R_1$, $R_2$ are pitch cone radii at the larger end.

**Pitch cone radius (larger end):**

$$R = \frac{m \cdot Z}{2}$$

---

## 2.3 PLANETARY (EPICYCLIC) GEAR TRAINS

A **planetary gear train** consists of:
- **Sun gear** (central, S)
- **Planet gears** (revolve around sun, P)
- **Ring (Annulus) gear** (internal teeth, R)
- **Arm / Carrier** (holds planets, C)

### 2.3.1 Two Methods of Analysis (KTU-Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 requires the *Tabular Method* (also called the *Relative Velocity Method*).**

**Method 1 — Formula Method (fast):**
Speed of any element = (Speed of arm) + (Speed of element relative to arm) × (Teeth ratio).

**Method 2 — Tabular Method (systematic):**
Hold the *arm* stationary by subtracting arm speed from all components. Then apply ordinary gear-train formula. Restore arm speed at the end.

### 2.3.2 Standard Formulae for Common Configurations

$$i_{S,R}^{C} = 1 + \frac{Z_R}{Z_S} \quad \text{(Sun driving, Ring fixed, Carrier output)}$$

$$i_{R,S}^{C} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{Z_S}{Z_R}} \quad \text{(Ring driving, Sun fixed, Carrier output)}$$

---

## 2.4 BELT DRIVES

Belt drives transmit power by **friction** between the belt and the pulleys. Types: **Open belt, Crossed belt, and Quarter-turn belt**.

### 2.4.1 Velocity Ratio

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} \quad \text{(ideal, no slip)}$$

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1 \cdot (1 + s)} \quad \text{(with slip } s \text{)}$$

### 2.4.2 Length of Open Belt

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

where $C$ = centre distance between pulleys.

### 2.4.3 Length of Crossed Belt

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2}{4C}$$

### 2.4.4 Angle of Contact (Lap Angle)

For open belt on smaller pulley:

$$\theta = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}\right)$$

### 2.4.5 Centrifugal Tension

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 \quad \text{(where } m = \text{mass per unit length)}$$

Maximum power transmission (Condition): $T_c = \dfrac{T_1}{3}$, where $T_1$ is the tight-side tension.

---

## 2.5 CHAIN DRIVES

Chain drives are **positive drives** (no slip, no tension in slack side). Used when **synchronization** is critical (engine camshafts, conveyors).

### 2.5.1 Velocity Ratio & Chain Length

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \frac{P \cdot Z_2}{P \cdot Z_1}$$

where $P$ = pitch of chain (same on both sprockets).

### 2.5.2 Length of Chain (in pitches)

$$L_p = 2\left(\frac{Z_1 + Z_2}{2}\right) + \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{2\pi}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{P}{C} + \frac{C}{P}$$

or the more compact form:

$$L_p = \frac{2C}{P} + \frac{Z_1 + Z_2}{2} + \frac{P \cdot (Z_2 - Z_1)^2}{4\pi^2 C}$$

### 2.5.3 Chain Velocity

$$v = \frac{P \cdot N}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(m/s, with N in RPM and P in mm)}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM TIP:** Use `\vert` notation for *modulus/absolute* values to avoid breaking markdown tables.

| # | System | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|--------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Spur Gear — Pitch Diameter | $d = m Z$ | m in mm, Z dimensionless |
| 2 | Spur Gear — Circular Pitch | $p = \pi m$ | p in mm |
| 3 | Spur Gear — Centre Distance | $a = m (Z_1 + Z_2)/2$ | a in mm |
| 4 | Spur Gear — Velocity | $v = \pi d N / 60{,}000$ | v in m/s, N in RPM |
| 5 | Spur Gear — Speed Ratio | $i = N_1/N_2 = Z_2/Z_1$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Bevel — Cone Half Angle (90° shafts) | $\gamma = \tan^{-1}\!\left(Z_1/Z_2\right)$ | in degrees/radians |
| 7 | Bevel — Equivalent Radius | $R_e = R - b \sin\gamma$ | R, b, $R_e$ in mm |
| 8 | Planetary — Sun/Ring/Carrier | $i_{S,R}^C = 1 + Z_R/Z_S$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Belt — Open Belt Length | $L = 2C + \pi(D_1+D_2)/2 + (D_2-D_1)^2/(4C)$ | all lengths in m or mm |
| 10 | Belt — Crossed Belt Length | $L = 2C + \pi(D_1+D_2)/2 + (D_1+D_2)^2/(4C)$ | lengths consistent |
| 11 | Belt — Angle of Contact | $\theta = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}\!\left[(D_2 - D_1)/(2C)\right]$ | in degrees |
| 12 | Belt — Centrifugal Tension | $T_c = m v^2$ | N (where m in kg/m) |
| 13 | Chain — Length in Pitches | $L_p = 2C/P + (Z_1+Z_2)/2 + P(Z_2-Z_1)^2/(4\pi^2 C)$ | $L_p$ dimensionless |
| 14 | Chain — Velocity | $v = P N/60{,}000$ | v in m/s, P in mm |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Industry Use Case |
|--------|-------------------|
| Spur Gears | Watch movements, electric toothbrushes, CNC lead screws, robotics |
| Bevel Gears | Differentials (cars), hand drills, lawn mowers |
| Planetary Gears | Robotic harmonic drives, automatic gearboxes, helicopter transmissions |
| Belt Drives | Washing machines, conveyor belts, vacuum cleaners, printing presses |
| Chain Drives | Motorcycles, bicycles, forklift lifting mechanisms, industrial conveyors |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Spur Gear Centre Distance

We know that two meshing gears must have the **same circular pitch** at the pitch circle. If the gears have modules $m_1$ and $m_2$ respectively, then:

$$p_1 = p_2 \implies \pi m_1 = \pi m_2 \implies m_1 = m_2 = m$$

Let $d_1 = m Z_1$ and $d_2 = m Z_2$ be their pitch circle diameters. The **centre distance** $a$ between the two parallel shaft axes equals half the sum of the pitch diameters:

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} \\
  &= \frac{m Z_1 + m Z_2}{2} \\
  &= \frac{m (Z_1 + Z_2)}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

This relation is **fundamental** to KTU gear-design problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Gear Train Speed Ratio

For two meshed gears, the linear velocity at the pitch point must be equal (no slip):

$$\begin{aligned}
v_1 &= v_2 \\
\frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} &= \frac{\pi d_2 N_2}{60} \\
d_1 N_1 &= d_2 N_2
\end{aligned}$$

Since $d = m Z$ and modules are equal:

$$\begin{aligned}
m Z_1 N_1 &= m Z_2 N_2 \\
\frac{N_1}{N_2} &= \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \frac{d_2}{d_1}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental gear-train ratio** applicable to spur, helical, and bevel gears (with equivalent diameters for bevel).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Bevel Gear Cone Half Angles (90° Shafts)

Consider two bevel gears in mesh, with axes intersecting at $O$ at $90°$. Let $\gamma$ and $\alpha$ be the half-cone angles of pinion and gear respectively.

From the right-triangle geometry of the pitch cones:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\gamma &= \frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{m Z_1 / 2}{m Z_2 / 2} = \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \\
\tan\alpha &= \frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}
\end{aligned}$$

And since the shaft angle $\Sigma = \gamma + \alpha = 90°$:

$$\alpha = 90° - \gamma$$

These two equations allow complete determination of bevel-gear geometry.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Length of Open Belt (Step-by-Step)

Consider two pulleys of diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ with their centres separated by distance $C$. The open belt has two **straight portions** of length $L_1 = L_2$ and two **wrap portions** along the pulleys.

**Step 1 — Straight portions:**
Each straight portion acts as a tangent to both pulleys. From the right-triangle geometry:

$$L_s = \sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2}\right)^2}$$

Since both straight segments have the same length:

$$L_{\text{straight,total}} = 2\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Belt around pulleys (arcs):**
The belt wraps each pulley through an angle $\alpha$ (in radians):

$$\alpha = 2 \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2C}\right)$$

Arc length on each pulley = $D \cdot \alpha / 2$.

Sum of the two arcs:

$$L_{\text{arcs}} = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} \cdot \alpha = \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Total belt length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= L_{\text{straight,total}} + L_{\text{arcs}} \\
  &= 2\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2}\right)^2} + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding the radical using the binomial expansion (for $C \gg D$):

$$\sqrt{C^2 - x^2} \approx C - \frac{x^2}{2C}$$

We obtain the **standard engineering formula**:

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Planetary Gear Speed Ratio (Tabular Method Demonstration)

Let a planetary gear train have:
- Sun gear S with $Z_S$ teeth
- Ring gear R with $Z_R$ teeth
- Carrier C rotating at $N_C$ RPM
- Planet gear P with $Z_P$ teeth

**Standard geometric constraint** (for a workable planetary gear):

$$Z_R = Z_S + 2 Z_P$$

**Tabular Method Procedure:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Tabular Method — Step by Step:**
> 1. List all elements (S, P, R, C) in a row.
> 2. Add an extra row at the bottom labelled "Arm fixed" — subtract the arm's speed from every element above.
> 3. In the "Arm fixed" row, write the speed of one element as $x$ and compute the others using ordinary gear ratio (gears in mesh rotate in opposite directions).
> 4. Multiply each value in the "Arm fixed" row by the ratio $(x = x)$ — i.e., introduce multiplier $x$.
> 5. Express the actual speed of any element as: $\text{Actual} = \text{Arm speed} + x \cdot (\text{Arm-fixed value})$.

**Worked Example — Sun drives, Ring fixed, Output on Carrier:**

| Step | Sun (S) | Planet (P) | Ring (R) | Arm (C) |
|------|---------|------------|----------|---------|
| 1. Actual speeds | $N_S$ | — | 0 | $N_C$ |
| 2. Subtract arm speed | $N_S - N_C$ | — | $-N_C$ | 0 |
| 3. Arm-fixed (assume S = +1) | $+1$ | $-Z_S/Z_P$ | $+Z_S/Z_R$ | 0 |
| 4. Add arm speed × multiplier | $x$ | $-x Z_S/Z_P$ | $+x Z_S/Z_R$ | 0 |

In row 2, the ring is at $-N_C$. In row 3, ring is at $+x Z_S / Z_R$. Equating:

$$-N_C = x \cdot \frac{Z_S}{Z_R} \implies x = -\frac{N_C Z_R}{Z_S}$$

Substituting into Sun:

$$N_S - N_C = x = -\frac{N_C Z_R}{Z_S}$$

Solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_S &= N_C - \frac{N_C Z_R}{Z_S} \\
N_S &= N_C \left(1 - \frac{Z_R}{Z_S}\right) \\
\frac{N_S}{N_C} &= 1 - \frac{Z_R}{Z_S} \\
\frac{N_C}{N_S} &= \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{Z_R}{Z_S}}
\end{aligned}$$

For $Z_S = 20$ and $Z_R = 60$:

$$\frac{N_C}{N_S} = \frac{1}{1 - 60/20} = \frac{1}{1 - 3} = -\frac{1}{4}$$

Negative sign indicates reversal; magnitude 4:1 reduction. Output is **slow and powerful** — typical of harmonic drives and servo motors.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Spur vs Bevel vs Planetary vs Belt vs Chain)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *ready-made 7–14 mark answer* for "Compare the various transmission systems" type questions.

| Parameter | Spur Gear | Bevel Gear | Planetary Gear | Belt Drive | Chain Drive |
|-----------|-----------|------------|----------------|------------|-------------|
| **Shaft orientation** | Parallel | Intersecting (90°) | Co-axial / coaxial | Parallel / non-intersecting | Parallel |
| **Drive type** | Positive (no slip) | Positive | Positive | Friction (may slip) | Positive (no slip) |
| **Efficiency** | 98–99 % per mesh | 97–98 % | 97–98 % (per stage) | 95–98 % | 96–98 % |
| **Speed ratio per stage** | up to 7 : 1 | up to 4 : 1 | up to 12 : 1 (per stage) | up to 5 : 1 | up to 8 : 1 |
| **Compactness** | Moderate | Moderate | Very high | Low (needs long C) | Low |
| **Centre distance** | Short | Short | Very short | Long | Long |
| **Shock absorption** | Poor | Poor | Poor | Good | Good |
| **Maintenance** | Lubrication only | Lubrication only | Lubrication only | Tension adjustment | Lubrication, tensioning |
| **Lubrication required** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Not critical | Yes |
| **Typical failure mode** | Tooth breakage / wear | Tooth wear | Planet bearing failure | Belt slip / break | Chain stretch |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Higher | Highest (precision) | Lowest | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Mechatronic Transmission System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Source - Electric Motor] --> B[Speed Reducer Stage 1 - Spur Gear]
    B --> C[Direction Change Stage - Bevel Gear]
    C --> D[Compact Multiplication Stage - Planetary Gear]
    D --> E[Long Distance Transfer - Belt or Chain Drive]
    E --> F[Coupling Stage]
    F --> G[Mechanical Load - Robot Joint / Wheel]
    G --> H[Feedback Sensor - Encoder]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** In a *typical CNC spindle* or *robotic joint*, all five elements can appear in cascade: a motor → spur reduction → bevel direction change → planetary final reduction → chain drive to load. The encoder closes the mechatronic loop.

---

## 4.2 Spur Gear Engagement Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pinion - Driver - Z1 teeth] -->|meshing contact| G1[Gear - Driven - Z2 teeth]
    G1 --> O1[Output Shaft - Reduced Speed, Increased Torque]
    P1 --> O2[Input Shaft - Motor]
```

---

## 4.3 Bevel Gear Mesh Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Shaft 1 - Horizontal] --> BG1[Bevel Pinion]
    BG1 -->|90 deg intersecting mesh| BG2[Bevel Gear]
    BG2 --> SS[Shaft 2 - Vertical]
    BG1 -.cone angle gamma.-> BG1
    BG2 -.cone angle alpha.-> BG2
```

---

## 4.4 Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CENTRE
        SUN((Sun Gear - Zs))
    end

    subgraph PLANETS
        P1((Planet - Zp))
        P2((Planet - Zp))
        P3((Planet - Zp))
    end

    subgraph RING
        RING((Ring Annulus - Zr))
    end

    CAR[Carrier Arm - Output]

    P1 --> CAR
    P2 --> CAR
    P3 --> CAR
    P1 -->|meshes| SUN
    P2 -->|meshes| SUN
    P3 -->|meshes| SUN
    P1 -->|meshes internally| RING
    P2 -->|meshes internally| RING
    P3 -->|meshes internally| RING
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three planets mesh with the central sun and the surrounding ring. The carrier rotates about the same axis as the sun, taking the planets with it like a "planetary orbit."

---

## 4.5 Belt Drive Open vs Crossed Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN_BELT
        direction LR
        P1A[Pulley 1 - Driver] -->|straight top segment| P2A[Pulley 2 - Driven]
        P2A -->|straight bottom segment| P1A
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CROSSED_BELT
        direction LR
        P1B[Pulley 1] -->|crossed over to bottom| P2B[Pulley 2]
        P2B -->|crossed back to top| P1B
    end
```

---

## 4.6 Chain Drive Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Driving Sprocket - Z1 teeth] -->|tension side| SP2[Driven Sprocket - Z2 teeth]
    SP2 -->|slack side - return| SP1
    SP1 --> PK1[Pitch Circle 1]
    SP2 --> PK2[Pitch Circle 2]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why chain, not belt, for high-torque applications?**
> Belts rely on **friction** (will slip if overloaded). Chains use **positive mesh** with sprocket teeth — they cannot slip, and they transmit much higher torque per unit width.

---

## 4.7 Power Flow Block Diagram (Concept Integration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL[Mechatronic Controller] --> DRV[Motor Driver]
    DRV --> MOT[DC / Servo Motor - High Speed Low Torque]
    MOT --> STG1[Stage 1 - Spur Gear - 3:1]
    STG1 --> STG2[Stage 2 - Planetary - 5:1]
    STG2 --> STG3[Stage 3 - Belt or Chain]
    STG3 --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    LOAD --> FB[Encoder / Resolver]
    FB --> CTRL
```

This is the **canonical mechatronic transmission block** — used in almost every robotic arm, CNC, and AGV (automated guided vehicle).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Module" and "Circular Pitch" for a spur gear. A spur gear has 24 teeth and a module of 3 mm. Determine its pitch diameter and addendum.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module (m):** The ratio of pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth, expressed in mm.
> $$\text{Module (m)} = \frac{d}{Z}$$
>
> **Circular Pitch (p):** The arc distance measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points of two adjacent teeth.
> $$p = \pi \cdot m$$

**Calculations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= m \cdot Z = 3 \times 24 = 72 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Addendum } a &= 1 \cdot m = 1 \times 3 = 3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Pitch diameter = **72 mm**; Addendum = **3 mm**.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a belt drive and a chain drive. List any four points of distinction.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Belt Drive | Chain Drive |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Power transmitted by **friction** between belt and pulley | Power transmitted by **positive engagement** of teeth |
| 2 | **Slip** can occur under heavy load | **No slip**; positive drive |
| 3 | Suitable for **moderate centre distances** | Suitable for **short to moderate** centre distances |
| 4 | Lower cost; quieter | Higher cost; slight noise due to articulation |
| 5 | Used where shock absorption is needed | Used where synchronization is critical |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Module-3 internal choice.* You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Show all derivations neatly.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Two spur gears are in mesh. The pinion has 18 teeth and the gear has 72 teeth. The module is 4 mm and the pressure angle is 20°. If the pinion rotates at 1440 RPM, determine:
**(i)** Centre distance, **(ii)** Pitch line velocity, **(iii)** Velocity ratio, **(iv)** Angular velocity of the gear.

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **length of an open belt drive** connecting two pulleys of diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ with centre distance $C$.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (a)** (7 marks)

**Given:** $Z_1 = 18$, $Z_2 = 72$, $m = 4$ mm, $\phi = 20°$, $N_1 = 1440$ RPM.

**(i) Centre distance:**

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{m (Z_1 + Z_2)}{2} \\
  &= \frac{4 \times (18 + 72)}{2} \\
  &= \frac{4 \times 90}{2} \\
  &= 180 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Pitch line velocity** (using $d_1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= m Z_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm} \\
v &= \frac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60{,}000} \\
  &= \frac{\pi \times 72 \times 1440}{60{,}000} \\
  &= \frac{325{,}722.07}{60{,}000} \\
  &\approx 5.43 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Velocity ratio:**

$$i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = \frac{72}{18} = 4$$

**(iv) Angular velocity of gear:**

$$N_2 = \frac{N_1}{i} = \frac{1440}{4} = 360 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2 \pi N_2}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 360}{60} = 12 \pi \approx 37.7 \text{ rad/s}$$

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A (b)** (7 marks)

**Derivation of Open Belt Length** — Already derived in detail in **Section 3.4** above. Recap the standard result:

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$

Each step must be shown:
1. Two straight portions each of length $\sqrt{C^2 - \left(\frac{D_2 - D_1}{2}\right)^2}$.
2. Two arc portions summing to $\frac{\pi(D_1 + D_2)}{2}$.
3. Binomial expansion of the radical to obtain the standard formula.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** An **epicyclic (planetary) gear train** has a sun gear with 20 teeth, planet gears with 30 teeth each, and a ring gear with 80 teeth. The sun gear is driven at 1500 RPM and the ring gear is **fixed**. Determine the speed and direction of the carrier (output).

**(b)** A crossed belt connects two pulleys of diameters 400 mm and 200 mm. The centre distance is 1500 mm. Calculate the **length of the belt** required.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (a)** (7 marks)

**Given:** $Z_S = 20$, $Z_P = 30$, $Z_R = 80$, $N_S = 1500$ RPM, Ring fixed ($N_R = 0$).

**Step 1 — Verify geometry:** $Z_R = Z_S + 2 Z_P = 20 + 60 = 80$ ✓ (workable train)

**Step 2 — Tabular method:**

| Row | Sun S | Planet P | Ring R | Carrier C |
|-----|-------|----------|--------|-----------|
| 1. Arm fixed: assign S = +1 | +1 | $-\dfrac{Z_S}{Z_P} = -\dfrac{20}{30} = -\dfrac{2}{3}$ | $+\dfrac{Z_S}{Z_R} = +\dfrac{20}{80} = +\dfrac{1}{4}$ | 0 |
| 2. Multiply by $x$ | $x$ | $-\dfrac{2x}{3}$ | $+\dfrac{x}{4}$ | 0 |
| 3. Add carrier speed $N_C$ | $x + N_C$ | $-\dfrac{2x}{3} + N_C$ | $+\dfrac{x}{4} + N_C$ | $N_C$ |

**Step 3 — Apply ring-fixed condition** (row 3, ring = 0):

$$\frac{x}{4} + N_C = 0 \implies x = -4 N_C$$

**Step 4 — Sun speed** (row 3, sun = $N_S = 1500$):

$$x + N_C = 1500$$

Substitute $x = -4 N_C$:

$$-4 N_C + N_C = 1500 \implies -3 N_C = 1500 \implies N_C = -500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**
- Magnitude: **500 RPM**
- Negative sign: the carrier rotates in the **opposite direction** to the sun.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** With the ring fixed, this planetary set produces a **3 : 1 speed reduction** (1500 → 500) with directional reversal. The output is the carrier — used as a *servo reduction stage* in robotic joints.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B (b)** (7 marks)

**Given:** $D_1 = 400$ mm (driver), $D_2 = 200$ mm (driven), $C = 1500$ mm, **Crossed belt**.

**Formula (crossed belt):**

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2}{4C}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \times 1500 + \frac{\pi (400 + 200)}{2} + \frac{(400 + 200)^2}{4 \times 1500} \\
  &= 3000 + \frac{\pi \times 600}{2} + \frac{600^2}{6000} \\
  &= 3000 + 300\pi + \frac{360{,}000}{6000} \\
  &= 3000 + 942.48 + 60 \\
  &= 4002.48 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Belt length ≈ **4002.5 mm** (≈ 4 m).

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall #1 — Confusing Open and Crossed Belt Formulae:** The open-belt formula has $(D_2 - D_1)^2$, while the crossed-belt formula has $(D_1 + D_2)^2$. Reversing these is a guaranteed **−2 marks** deduction.
>
> **Pitfall #2 — Forgetting Direction of Rotation:** In planetary gear problems, the sign of the output speed carries marks. *Always state the direction explicitly* ("carrier rotates opposite to sun") — do not write only the magnitude.
>
> **Pitfall #3 — Mixing Units:** RPM must be converted carefully when computing pitch-line velocity. KTU standard: **$v = \pi d N / 60{,}000$** when $d$ is in mm and $v$ is in m/s.
>
> **Pitfall #4 — Bevel Gear Cone Angle Confusion:** The cone half-angle belongs to the gear with that gear's own number of teeth in the numerator: $\tan \gamma_1 = Z_1/Z_2$. Mixing up pinion and gear is a common 2-mark loss.
>
> **Pitfall #5 — Skipping Geometric Constraint Check:** For planetary gears, always verify $Z_R = Z_S + 2 Z_P$ before proceeding. Examiners *trap* students with non-workable geometries.
>
> **Pitfall #6 — Centre Distance Formula:** Remember that for two meshing gears, $a = m(Z_1 + Z_2)/2$, not $m \cdot Z_{\text{mean}}$. Easy slip-up.
>
> **Pitfall #7 — Chain Length Units:** Chain length formula gives **number of pitches** ($L_p$), not the actual length in metres. Multiply by pitch $P$ at the end.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and tick each box before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Spur Gears:** Module $m = d/Z$, circular pitch $p = \pi m$, centre distance $a = m(Z_1 + Z_2)/2$.
- [ ] Spur gear addendum = **1 m**, dedendum = **1.25 m**, clearance = **0.25 m**.
- [ ] Standard pressure angle = **20°** (full-depth system).
- [ ] Pitch line velocity $v = \pi d N / 60{,}000$ m/s (when $d$ in mm).
- [ ] Velocity ratio $i = N_1/N_2 = Z_2/Z_1$ (for all positive gear trains).
- [ ] **Bevel Gears:** For 90° shafts, $\tan \gamma_1 = Z_1/Z_2$, $\gamma_1 + \gamma_2 = 90°$.
- [ ] Bevel equivalent radius $R_e = R - b \sin \gamma$ for Lewis-beam strength.
- [ ] **Planetary Gears:** Geometric rule $Z_R = Z_S + 2 Z_P$ (must hold for any workable train).
- [ ] Tabular method: subtract arm speed, apply ordinary gear-train ratio, restore arm speed.
- [ ] Planetary output (Ring fixed): $N_C/N_S = 1/(1 + Z_R/Z_S)$ — *memorize this for fast answer*.
- [ ] **Open Belt Length:** $L = 2C + \pi(D_1+D_2)/2 + (D_2 - D_1)^2/(4C)$.
- [ ] **Crossed Belt Length:** $L = 2C + \pi(D_1+D_2)/2 + (D_1 + D_2)^2/(4C)$.
- [ ] Belt angle of contact (smaller pulley) $\theta = 180° - 2\sin^{-1}[(D_2-D_1)/(2C)]$.
- [ ] Centrifugal tension $T_c = m v^2$; max power when $T_c = T_1/3$.
- [ ] **Chain Length (in pitches):** $L_p = 2C/P + (Z_1+Z_2)/2 + P(Z_2 - Z_1)^2/(4\pi^2 C)$.
- [ ] Chain drive is **positive** (no slip); belt drive relies on **friction**.
- [ ] Remember to **state direction** of output in planetary gear solutions.
- [ ] Always **mention units** explicitly in numerical answers.
- [ ] Compare-and-contrast questions: use the **Analytical Comparison Matrix** (Section 3.6).

---

**END OF MODULE-3 NOTES — Mechanical Power Transmission Systems**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PEAET895 Mechatronics*
